# iowabankruptcy.org -- Full Content

This file contains the full text content of all pages on iowabankruptcy.org.
Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project (openbankruptcyproject.org).


---

## The 341 Meeting of Creditors in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/341-meeting.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesCedar RapidsChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›341 Meeting

The 341 Meeting of Creditors in Iowa

The 341 meeting is one of the most anxiety-inducing parts of bankruptcy, but it is usually quick and straightforward. Here is what to expect in Iowa.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Is the 341 Meeting?

The 341 meeting of creditors is a brief hearing required in every bankruptcy case under 11 U.S.C. section 341. Despite its name, creditors rarely attend. The meeting is conducted by the bankruptcy trustee assigned to your case - not the bankruptcy judge.

In Iowa, meetings are held at U.S. Bankruptcy Court, 123 E Walnut St, Des Moines, IA 50309 (S.D. Iowa). Many meetings are now conducted by phone or video conference. The typical meeting lasts 5-15 minutes.

What to Bring

1

Photo ID

Valid driver's license, state ID, or passport

2

SSN Proof

Social Security card, tax return, or W-2 showing your full SSN

3

Your Schedules

Copies of your bankruptcy filing for reference

Common Trustee Questions

The trustee will ask you questions under oath. Most are straightforward yes/no questions to verify the accuracy of your filing:

Did you sign the petition and schedules, and is the information accurate?

Did you list all of your assets - including bank accounts, vehicles, real estate, and personal property?

Did you list all of your debts?

Have you sold, transferred, or given away any property in the last 2-4 years?

Are you expecting any tax refunds, inheritance, or lawsuit proceeds?

Is anyone holding property for you?

Have you filed all required tax returns?

Have you previously filed for bankruptcy?

Tips: Answer honestly and briefly. If you do not know the answer, say so. Do not guess. Your attorney will be present and can help if needed.

What Happens After

After Chapter 7 Meeting

Trustee may request additional documents

60-day period for creditor objections

If no issues, discharge in about 60 days

Case closes shortly after discharge

After Chapter 13 Meeting

Plan confirmation hearing follows (30-45 days later)

Trustee may request plan modifications

Once confirmed, you make plan payments for 3-5 years

Discharge after completing all payments

Tips for Success

Arrive early. If in person, arrive 15 minutes before your scheduled time.

Dress appropriately. Business casual is fine. You do not need a suit.

Be honest. You are under oath. Lying to the trustee is a federal crime.

Be brief. Answer the question asked and stop. Do not volunteer extra information.

Review your schedules. Read through your filing the night before so the information is fresh.

Relax. The vast majority of 341 meetings are routine and uneventful.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the 341 meeting of creditors?

The 341 meeting (named after section 341 of the Bankruptcy Code) is a required hearing in every bankruptcy case. It is held about 30 days after filing and is conducted by the bankruptcy trustee, not the judge. In Iowa, meetings are held at U.S. Bankruptcy Court, 123 E Walnut St, Des Moines, IA 50309 (S.D. Iowa) or by phone/video.

What questions will the trustee ask?

Common questions include: Did you list all your assets and debts? Is the information in your schedules accurate? Have you sold or transferred any property recently? Are you expecting any tax refunds, lawsuits, or inheritances? Did you read and understand the bankruptcy forms you signed?

Do I have to go in person?

In Iowa, many 341 meetings are conducted by phone or video since the COVID pandemic. Check with your attorney or the court clerk for the current format. If in person, meetings are held at U.S. Bankruptcy Court, 123 E Walnut St, Des Moines, IA 50309 (S.D. Iowa).

Will creditors attend?

In most consumer cases, creditors do not attend. If a creditor does attend, they can ask you questions under oath. Your attorney will be present to object to improper questions.

What should I bring?

Bring: (1) a valid photo ID (driver's license or passport), (2) proof of Social Security number (SSN card, tax return, or W-2), (3) copies of your bankruptcy schedules, and (4) any documents your attorney has requested.

What happens after the 341 meeting?

After the 341 meeting, the trustee may request additional documents or information. In Chapter 7, creditors have 60 days to object to discharge. In Chapter 13, the confirmation hearing follows. If no issues arise, your case proceeds toward discharge.

Preparing for Bankruptcy?

Learn about the full process and check your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## The Automatic Stay in Iowa Bankruptcy
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/automatic-stay.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesCedar RapidsChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Automatic Stay

The Automatic Stay in Iowa Bankruptcy

The moment you file bankruptcy in Iowa, a powerful federal court order called the automatic stay goes into effect. It immediately stops most creditor actions against you. Here is what it covers and what it does not.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What the Automatic Stay Stops Immediately

Stopped by the Stay

Wage garnishment - Your employer must stop withholding garnished wages once notified of the filing

Foreclosure - All foreclosure proceedings and sales are halted, even if a sale date is already scheduled

Vehicle repossession - Creditors cannot repossess your car, truck, or other vehicles

Lawsuits - All pending civil lawsuits for debt collection are paused

Creditor calls and letters - All collection contacts must stop

Bank account levies - Creditors cannot freeze or seize your bank account

Utility shutoffs - Utilities cannot be disconnected for 20 days after filing (you must provide adequate assurance of future payment)

IRS collection - Tax liens and levies are paused (though the lien itself survives)

Eviction - In some circumstances, pending eviction actions are paused

NOT Stopped by the Stay

Child support collection - Domestic support obligations (child support, alimony) continue

Criminal proceedings - Criminal cases, including those involving bad checks or fraud, continue

Tax audits - The IRS can continue auditing your tax returns (but cannot collect)

Pension loan repayment - Deductions from wages for pension plan loan repayments continue

Paternity and custody - Family court proceedings regarding custody, visitation, and paternity continue

Domestic violence proceedings - Protective orders and related actions continue

Government regulatory actions - Actions to enforce police or regulatory powers

Post-judgment evictions - If the landlord already has a judgment for possession before filing

Stay Violations - What to Do in Iowa

If a creditor continues collection activity after you file bankruptcy in Iowa, that is a violation of the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. section 362(k). Common violations include:

Continued phone calls or letters demanding payment

Repossessing a vehicle after the case was filed

Proceeding with a foreclosure sale

Continuing to garnish wages

Filing or continuing a lawsuit

Refusing to return property already seized

Steps to Take If Your Stay Is Violated

Document everything. Save letters, record dates and times of phone calls, take screenshots, keep voicemails.

Notify the creditor in writing. Send a letter informing them of your bankruptcy case number and filing date.

Notify your attorney. Your attorney can file a motion for contempt and sanctions with the N.D. and S.D. Iowa bankruptcy court.

You may be entitled to damages. Under section 362(k), individuals harmed by willful stay violations can recover actual damages, attorney fees, and in egregious cases, punitive damages.

How Long Does the Automatic Stay Last?

Chapter 7

The stay lasts until your case is closed, dismissed, or your discharge is granted - typically 3-4 months. After discharge, the discharge injunction (11 U.S.C. section 524) permanently replaces the stay for discharged debts.

Chapter 13

The stay lasts for the duration of your 3-5 year repayment plan. This extended protection is one of Chapter 13's key advantages, particularly for saving homes and vehicles.

Repeat Filers: Reduced Stay Protection

If you had a bankruptcy case dismissed within the past year, special rules apply:

One prior dismissal: The automatic stay expires after 30 days unless you file a motion to extend it (section 362(c)(3)).

Two or more prior dismissals: The automatic stay does not go into effect at all unless the court imposes it (section 362(c)(4)).

Use the free 1328(f) Screener to check your eligibility before filing again.

Iowa-Specific Considerations

Iowa is one of the most debtor-friendly states in the country. The unlimited homestead exemption, combined with the 12-month redemption period after foreclosure, provides powerful protections for homeowners facing financial difficulty.

Foreclosure process: Iowa uses non-judicial foreclosure. Iowa allows non-judicial foreclosure, but most foreclosures proceed judicially. Iowa provides a generous 12-month right of redemption after a foreclosure sale, giving homeowners significant time to reclaim their property.

Garnishment rules: Iowa follows the federal standard: creditors can garnish the lesser of 25% of disposable earnings or the amount by which weekly disposable earnings exceed 30 times the federal minimum wage ($217.50/week in 2026).

Homestead exemption: Unlimited - the stay protects your home while the exemption determines what you keep

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the automatic stay in bankruptcy?

The automatic stay is a federal court order under 11 U.S.C. section 362 that takes effect the instant you file bankruptcy. It immediately stops most collection actions including wage garnishment, foreclosure proceedings, repossession attempts, lawsuits, harassing phone calls, and utility shutoffs. It applies to all creditors in Iowa whether they know about the filing or not.

What does the automatic stay stop?

The automatic stay stops: wage garnishment, foreclosure sales and proceedings, vehicle repossession, debt collection lawsuits, creditor phone calls and letters, bank account levies, utility disconnection (for 20 days), eviction (in some cases), and IRS collection activity on tax debts.

What does the automatic stay NOT stop?

The automatic stay does not stop: child support and alimony collection (domestic support obligations), most criminal proceedings, certain tax audit proceedings, pension plan loan repayments, and evictions where the landlord already had a judgment for possession before filing.

What happens if a creditor violates the automatic stay?

If a creditor violates the automatic stay in Iowa, you may be entitled to actual damages, attorney fees, and in some cases punitive damages under 11 U.S.C. section 362(k). Document the violation and notify your attorney immediately. Your attorney can file a motion for contempt with the N.D. and S.D. Iowa bankruptcy court.

Does the automatic stay apply if I filed bankruptcy before?

If you had a case dismissed within the past year, the stay in your new case lasts only 30 days unless extended by court order. If you had two or more dismissals in the past year, the stay does not go into effect at all without a court order. Check your eligibility with the 1328(f) screener at 1328f.com.

How long does the automatic stay last?

In Chapter 7, the stay lasts until your case is closed, dismissed, or discharge is granted (typically 3-4 months). In Chapter 13, it lasts for the duration of your 3-5 year repayment plan. The discharge injunction under section 524 permanently replaces the stay for discharged debts.

Learn More About the Automatic Stay

Visit our dedicated automatic stay resource for a comprehensive guide to how the stay works nationwide.

Automatic Stay Guide

## Bank Accounts and Bankruptcy in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/bank-account.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Bank Accounts

Bank Accounts and Bankruptcy in Iowa

Understanding how bankruptcy interacts with your bank accounts in Iowa can help you protect your money and avoid unexpected freezes or seizures.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Bank Account Levies and the Automatic Stay

Before bankruptcy, judgment creditors can obtain a court order to levy (freeze and seize) your bank account in Iowa. The moment you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay prohibits all new levies.

New levies: Completely blocked by the stay

Pending levies: May need a motion to release frozen funds

Government debts: Tax levies are paused but not permanently blocked by the stay

Bank Setoff Rights

If you owe money to the same bank where your accounts are held (credit card, loan, line of credit), the bank has a right of setoff - meaning they can take money from your deposit account to cover what you owe, even without a court order.

How to Protect Yourself

Before filing: Open a new account at a bank or credit union where you have no debts

Move direct deposit: Switch your payroll direct deposit to the new bank

Keep records: Do not move large sums that could look like a fraudulent transfer

Timing: Coordinate with your attorney to minimize risk around the filing date

Do not drain accounts or make large transfers without attorney guidance. Transfers of property before filing can be scrutinized as potential fraudulent conveyances.

Protecting Bank Deposits in Iowa

In Chapter 7, the trustee can claim non-exempt cash in your bank accounts on the date of filing. In Iowa:

Homestead exemption: unlimited (does not protect cash)

Vehicle exemption: $7,000 (does not protect cash)

Check Iowa exemption statutes for any wildcard or cash exemptions that may protect bank deposits

Many Iowa filers protect bank deposits by timing the filing when account balances are low (right after paying normal living expenses like rent, utilities, and food).

Iowa exemptions guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Can creditors freeze my bank account in Iowa?

Yes. Before bankruptcy, a creditor with a judgment can levy (freeze and seize funds from) your bank account in Iowa. After you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay immediately prohibits any new levies and may require release of frozen funds.

Should I open a new bank account before filing?

If you owe money to the same bank where you have checking or savings accounts, the bank may exercise its right of setoff - taking money from your account to cover the debt. Opening an account at a bank where you have no debts before filing is a common protective measure.

Will bankruptcy affect my bank account?

Filing bankruptcy does not close your bank accounts. However, a Chapter 7 trustee could claim non-exempt funds. In Iowa, check which exemptions protect your cash and bank deposits.

Prepare Before You File

Check your discharge eligibility and learn how to protect your assets.

Discharge Screener
Iowa Filing Checklist

## Can I Buy a Car While in Chapter 13 in Iowa?
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/buy-car-chapter-13.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Buy Car in Ch. 13

Can I Buy a Car While in Chapter 13 in Iowa?

Yes - but you need court permission first. Buying a car during Chapter 13 requires filing a motion, getting trustee approval, and showing the purchase fits your budget.

Quick answer: You can buy a car during Chapter 13 in Iowa, but you must file a Motion to Incur New Debt and get court/trustee approval before signing anything. The court will evaluate whether the purchase is necessary and affordable within your plan.

The Process: Step by Step

1

Get Pre-Approved

Find a lender willing to work with Chapter 13 debtors. Get the terms in writing - vehicle, price, interest rate, monthly payment.

2

File the Motion

File a Motion to Incur New Debt with the Northern and Southern Districts of Iowa bankruptcy court. Include all purchase details and an updated budget.

3

Court Approval

The trustee reviews and may object. If no objection, the court typically grants the motion within 2-3 weeks. Do not sign anything until approved.

What the Court Wants to See

Necessity: Is the vehicle necessary for work, medical appointments, or family needs? Luxury vehicles are almost always denied.

Affordability: Can you make both the car payment AND your Chapter 13 plan payment? The court will review your current budget.

Reasonable terms: Interest rates above 20-25% may be questioned. The court wants to see you are not being exploited.

Vehicle value: A used car in the $5,000-$15,000 range is typical. Requests for new or expensive vehicles face heavier scrutiny.

Down payment source: The court may ask where the down payment is coming from. It should not come from plan payments or exempt funds earmarked for creditors.

Do NOT sign a purchase agreement, make a deposit, or take possession of a vehicle before the court grants your motion. Unauthorized debt during Chapter 13 can result in case dismissal.

Interest Rates and Lender Options

Borrowers in active Chapter 13 cases face limited lender options and higher interest rates. Typical options in Iowa include:

Credit unions: Often the best rates for Chapter 13 borrowers. Some Iowa credit unions specifically work with bankruptcy filers.

Subprime auto lenders: Companies like Capital One Auto Finance, Westlake Financial, and DriveTime specialize in lending to borrowers in bankruptcy. Rates range from 12-24%.

Buy-here-pay-here dealers: Available but typically the worst terms. Interest rates can exceed 25%, and vehicle quality is often poor.

Section 506 cramdown: If your current vehicle loan is more than 910 days old, you may be able to reduce the balance to the vehicle's value through your Chapter 13 plan rather than buying a new car.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I buy a car while in Chapter 13 in Iowa?

Yes, but you need court approval. You must file a motion with the bankruptcy court requesting permission to incur new debt. The trustee and court will evaluate whether the purchase is necessary, whether you can afford it within your plan, and whether the terms are reasonable.

Do I need trustee approval to buy a car in Iowa Chapter 13?

Yes. In Iowa, as in all states, you must get trustee approval before taking on new debt during Chapter 13. The trustee will review the purchase price, interest rate, monthly payment, and whether it fits within your budget and plan obligations.

What interest rate can I expect when buying a car during Chapter 13?

Interest rates for borrowers in Chapter 13 are typically higher - often 15-25% from subprime lenders. Some credit unions and specialized lenders work with Chapter 13 debtors at better rates. The court may reject a purchase with an unreasonably high interest rate.

How do I file a motion to buy a car in Chapter 13?

You or your attorney file a Motion to Incur New Debt with the bankruptcy court. The motion should include the vehicle details, purchase price, interest rate, monthly payment, and an updated budget showing you can afford both the car payment and your plan payment.

Can I lease a car during Chapter 13 in Iowa?

Leasing during Chapter 13 is treated the same as buying - you need court approval. Some courts view leases less favorably because they do not build equity. The total lease cost will be compared against purchasing.

What happens if I buy a car without court approval?

Taking on new debt without court approval is a violation of your Chapter 13 plan. The trustee may file a motion to dismiss your case or convert it to Chapter 7. The unauthorized debt could also be treated as fraudulent.

Related Topics

## Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/buying-a-house.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Buying a House

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Iowa

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent homeownership. Thousands of Americans buy homes after bankruptcy every year. Here are the waiting periods, requirements, and strategies for Iowa residents.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Waiting Periods by Loan Type

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA Loan2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years from discharge

VA Loan2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with lender approval)

USDA Loan3 years from discharge1 year into plan (with approval)

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

The waiting period starts from the discharge date, not the filing date. For Chapter 7, that is typically 3-4 months after filing. For Chapter 13, it is at the end of the 3-5 year plan.

Steps to Prepare for Homeownership

1

Rebuild Credit

Get a secured credit card immediately after discharge. Make small purchases and pay in full monthly. Consider a credit-builder loan. Target a 620+ score for FHA, 640+ for conventional.

2

Save for Down Payment

FHA requires 3.5% down with a 580+ score (10% with 500-579). Conventional requires 3-5%. VA and USDA offer zero-down options.

3

Stabilize Employment

Lenders want to see 2 years of stable employment. Consistent income documentation is critical. Self-employed borrowers may need additional tax returns.

Iowa Homestead Exemption

When you do buy a home in Iowa, the state homestead exemption protects unlimited in home equity from future creditors. This means:

Your home equity up to unlimited is protected from judgment creditors

If you ever need to file bankruptcy again, this equity is exempt from the Chapter 7 trustee

Learn more about keeping your house in Iowa bankruptcy →

Frequently Asked Questions

How long after bankruptcy can I buy a house in Iowa?

After Chapter 7: 2-4 years depending on the loan type (FHA requires 2 years, conventional requires 4 years). After Chapter 13: 1-2 years after discharge (FHA may allow applications during an active plan with trustee approval). VA loans require 2 years after Chapter 7 and may allow applications during Chapter 13.

Can I get a mortgage during Chapter 13?

Some lenders and the FHA allow mortgage applications during an active Chapter 13 plan if you have made 12 consecutive on-time payments and receive court and trustee approval. This is difficult but not impossible.

Will bankruptcy destroy my credit score?

Bankruptcy causes an initial credit score drop of 100-200 points. However, many Iowa filers see improvement within 12-18 months because the debt-to-income ratio improves and negative accounts stop accumulating late payments. Active rebuilding efforts accelerate recovery.

Start Planning Your Path to Homeownership

Check your discharge eligibility and learn about rebuilding credit after bankruptcy.

Discharge Screener
Rebuild Credit Guide

## Car Leases and Bankruptcy in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/car-lease.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Car Leases

Car Leases and Bankruptcy in Iowa

You have two options: assume the lease (keep the car) or reject it (return the car). The 60-day clock starts when you file.

Quick answer: When you file bankruptcy in Iowa, you must decide within 60 days whether to keep or surrender your leased vehicle. If you keep it, you must stay current. If you surrender it, remaining lease obligations become dischargeable unsecured debt.

Assume vs. Reject: Your Two Options

Assume (Keep the Car)

Continue making lease payments as normal

Cure any past-due payments

Lease continues as if bankruptcy never happened

Must not be a financial burden on your budget

Must decide within 60 days of filing

Good option if payments are affordable and you need the car

Reject (Return the Car)

Surrender the vehicle to the leasing company

Early termination fees become unsecured debt

Remaining lease payments become unsecured debt

Deficiency balance discharged in Chapter 7

Paid at reduced rate in Chapter 13 plan

Good option if payments are too high or car is not needed

The 60-Day Deadline

Under 11 U.S.C. section 365(d)(2), you have 60 days from the date of filing to assume or reject your car lease in Chapter 7. This deadline can be extended by the court for cause, but extensions are not automatic.

If you fail to act within 60 days, the lease is deemed rejected. This means you lose the vehicle and the remaining obligations become unsecured debt.

Mark your calendar: 60 days from your bankruptcy filing date is your deadline for the lease decision. Missing it means automatic rejection.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 Lease Treatment

Chapter 7

60-day assume/reject deadline applies

Rejected lease: deficiency balance discharged

Assumed lease: continues normally

No option to modify lease terms

Best if you want a clean break from a bad lease

Chapter 13

Decision made as part of your plan

Can cure past-due payments through plan

Rejected lease: deficiency paid through plan at reduced rate

More flexibility on timing

Best if you are behind on payments but want to keep the car

Practical Considerations

Mileage and wear: If you are over your mileage limit or the car has excess wear, rejecting the lease in bankruptcy eliminates those penalties. They become dischargeable unsecured debt.

Gap insurance: If the car is worth less than the payoff, gap insurance may cover the difference. Bankruptcy rejection does not trigger gap insurance - it covers total loss/theft only.

New lease after bankruptcy: You can lease a new car after bankruptcy, but expect higher deposits and potentially higher rates. Some Iowa dealers work with recent bankruptcy filers.

Automatic stay protection: While your case is pending, the leasing company cannot repossess the vehicle. If you plan to reject, you have time to arrange alternative transportation.

Frequently Asked Questions

What happens to my car lease if I file bankruptcy in Iowa?

In both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13, you must decide whether to assume (keep) or reject (surrender) your car lease within 60 days of filing. If you assume, you keep the car and continue making payments. If you reject, you return the vehicle and any remaining balance is treated as unsecured debt.

What does it mean to assume a lease in bankruptcy?

Assuming a lease means you agree to keep the lease, stay current on all payments, and cure any past-due amounts. The lease continues as if bankruptcy was never filed. You must be current on payments and the vehicle must not be a financial burden.

What does it mean to reject a lease in bankruptcy?

Rejecting a lease means surrendering the vehicle to the lessor. Any early termination fees, remaining payments, or deficiency balance become unsecured debt that can be discharged in Chapter 7 or paid through a Chapter 13 plan at pennies on the dollar.

What is the 60-day deadline for lease decisions?

Under 11 U.S.C. section 365(d)(2), you must assume or reject an unexpired lease within 60 days of filing (for Chapter 7). If you do not act within 60 days, the lease is deemed rejected. In Chapter 13, you can assume or reject as part of your plan.

Can I get out of a bad car lease through bankruptcy in Iowa?

Yes. Rejecting a car lease in bankruptcy is one of the cleanest ways to exit a bad lease. Early termination fees and remaining balance become unsecured debt. In Chapter 7, this debt is typically discharged entirely.

Will the leasing company repossess my car during bankruptcy?

No. The automatic stay prevents the leasing company from repossessing the vehicle once you file. However, you must make your decision within 60 days and stay current on payments if you want to keep the car.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 Comparison -- chapter7vs13.org

Open Bankruptcy Project -- openbankruptcyproject.org

---

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Cedar Rapids, Iowa - Repayment Plans & Eligibility
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/cedar-rapids/chapter-13.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
Cedar RapidsChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy›Cedar Rapids›Chapter 13

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Cedar Rapids, Iowa

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Here is how it works for Cedar Rapids residents.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

3-5 yrs

Plan Duration

$313

Filing Fee

Unlimited

Homestead Exemption

No

Means Test Required

What Is Chapter 13 Bankruptcy?

Chapter 13 bankruptcy - often called the "wage earner's plan" - lets you reorganize your debts into a manageable repayment plan. Unlike Chapter 7, you keep all of your property while making payments over 3-5 years.

Chapter 13 is particularly useful if you are behind on mortgage payments, need to save a home from foreclosure, or have income that exceeds the Chapter 7 means test threshold.

How Chapter 13 Works in Cedar Rapids

1Credit counseling - Complete an approved course before filing.

2File petition and plan - Submit your petition and proposed repayment plan to the Northern District of Iowa (N.D. Iowa). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

3Begin payments - You must begin making plan payments within 30 days of filing, even before the plan is confirmed.

4341 meeting - Meet briefly with the trustee approximately 30 days after filing.

5Confirmation hearing - The court reviews and approves your plan, typically 30-45 days after the 341 meeting.

6Complete plan payments - Make all payments over 3-5 years.

7Discharge - After completing all payments, remaining qualifying unsecured debts are discharged.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13

Chapter 7

Eliminates debt in 3-4 months

Must pass means test

May lose non-exempt property

Cannot catch up on mortgage arrears

Chapter 13

3-5 year repayment plan

No means test

Keep all property

Can cure mortgage defaults

Detailed Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison →

Frequently Asked Questions

How does Chapter 13 work in Cedar Rapids?

Chapter 13 lets you reorganize your debts into a 3-5 year repayment plan. You make monthly payments to a trustee who distributes funds to creditors. At the end of the plan, remaining qualifying debts are discharged.

How long is a Chapter 13 plan?

If your income is below the state median, your plan can be as short as 3 years. If above the median, the plan must be 5 years. The maximum is 5 years regardless of income.

Can I keep my house in Chapter 13?

Yes. Chapter 13 is specifically designed to help you keep your home. You can catch up on missed mortgage payments through your plan while maintaining current payments. Iowa's Unlimited homestead exemption provides additional protection.

How much does Chapter 13 cost?

The court filing fee is $313. Attorney fees in Cedar Rapids typically range from $3,000 to $5,000 and are usually paid through the plan itself, so you do not need the full amount up front.

What debts must I repay in Chapter 13?

Priority debts (taxes, child support) must be paid in full. Secured debts (mortgage, car loans) must be kept current. Unsecured creditors receive a percentage based on your disposable income - sometimes as low as 0%.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Cedar Rapids, Iowa - Eligibility, Process & Cost
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/cedar-rapids/chapter-7.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
Cedar RapidsChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy›Cedar Rapids›Chapter 7

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Cedar Rapids, Iowa

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months. Here is how it works for Cedar Rapids residents, including eligibility requirements, Iowa exemptions, and what to expect.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

3-4 mo

Average Timeline

$338

Filing Fee

$55,724

Median Income (1 person)

Unlimited

Homestead Exemption

What Is Chapter 7 Bankruptcy?

Chapter 7, sometimes called "liquidation bankruptcy," eliminates most unsecured debts - credit cards, medical bills, personal loans - in exchange for surrendering non-exempt assets. In practice, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases, meaning filers keep everything they own.

Cedar Rapids residents file Chapter 7 through the Northern District of Iowa (N.D. Iowa) at 111 7th Ave SE, Cedar Rapids, IA 52401.

Means Test: Do You Qualify?

The means test compares your income to Iowa's median income. If you are below the median for your household size, you automatically qualify.

Household SizeAnnual Median Income

1 person$55,724

2 persons$72,116

3 persons$85,880

4 persons$104,316

If your income exceeds the median, you may still qualify after deducting allowed expenses. If not, Chapter 13 is an alternative that does not require a means test.

Chapter 7 Timeline in Cedar Rapids

1Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing.

2File petition - Submit forms to the Northern District of Iowa (N.D. Iowa). The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

3341 meeting - Approximately 30 days after filing, you attend a brief meeting with the trustee. Creditors may attend but rarely do.

4Debtor education - Complete a second financial management course before discharge.

5Discharge - Approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting, the court enters your discharge order eliminating qualifying debts.

Iowa Exemptions in Chapter 7

Iowa exemptions: UNLIMITED homestead (must be less than 1/2 acre in a city or 40 acres elsewhere), $7,000 vehicle. Iowa is one of the most debtor-friendly states in the country for homestead protection.

ExemptionAmount

HomesteadUnlimited

Vehicle$7,000

Wildcard$1,000

Full Iowa exemptions guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Do I qualify for Chapter 7 in Cedar Rapids?

To qualify for Chapter 7 in Iowa, your household income must be below the state median or you must pass the means test calculation. For a single filer in Iowa, the median income threshold is approximately $55,724.

How long does Chapter 7 take in Cedar Rapids?

Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months from filing to discharge in the Northern District of Iowa (N.D. Iowa). The 341 meeting of creditors is usually scheduled about 30 days after filing.

Will I lose my car in Chapter 7?

Iowa allows you to exempt up to $7,000 in vehicle equity. If your car equity is within this limit, you can keep it. If you are still making payments, you can reaffirm the loan and keep the vehicle.

What debts does Chapter 7 eliminate?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts including credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and old utility bills. It does not eliminate student loans (in most cases), child support, alimony, most tax debts, or criminal fines.

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Cedar Rapids?

The court filing fee is $338. Attorney fees in Cedar Rapids typically range from $1,000 to $2,500. You must also complete two credit counseling courses at approximately $25-$50 total.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

## Iowa Bankruptcy Exemptions for Cedar Rapids Residents (Unlimited Homestead)
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/cedar-rapids/exemptions.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
Cedar RapidsChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy›Cedar Rapids›Exemptions

Iowa Bankruptcy Exemptions

When you file bankruptcy in Cedar Rapids, Iowa exemptions determine what property you can keep. Here is a complete breakdown of every exemption available.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Unlimited

Homestead

$7,000

Vehicle

$1,000

Wildcard

Understanding Exemptions

Bankruptcy exemptions are the legal protections that let you keep property when you file. Iowa exemptions: UNLIMITED homestead (must be less than 1/2 acre in a city or 40 acres elsewhere), $7,000 vehicle. Iowa is one of the most debtor-friendly states in the country for homestead protection.

In Chapter 7, exemptions determine what a trustee can sell to pay creditors. In Chapter 13, exemptions affect how much you must pay unsecured creditors through your plan.

Iowa Exemption Amounts

CategoryAmountNotes

HomesteadUnlimitedEquity in primary residence

Motor vehicle$7,000Equity in one vehicle

Wildcard / personal property$1,000Any property of your choosing

Clothing and necessitiesVariesNecessary wearing apparel

Household goodsVariesFurniture, appliances, etc.

Retirement accountsUnlimited401(k), IRA, pensions (ERISA-qualified)

Public benefitsUnlimitedSocial Security, unemployment, veterans

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the homestead exemption in Iowa?

Iowa provides a Unlimited homestead exemption that protects equity in your primary residence. If your home equity is within this amount, you can keep your home when filing Chapter 7 bankruptcy.

Can I keep my car filing bankruptcy in Cedar Rapids?

Iowa allows you to exempt up to $7,000 in vehicle equity. If you owe more than your car is worth or your equity is within the exemption, you keep the car.

Does Iowa allow federal bankruptcy exemptions?

Iowa allows filers to choose between state and federal exemptions, though state exemptions are typically more favorable.

What is the wildcard exemption in Iowa?

Iowa provides a $1,000 wildcard exemption that can be applied to any property of your choosing. This is useful for protecting assets that do not fit into other exemption categories.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

## Bankruptcy FAQ for Cedar Rapids, Iowa - Common Questions Answered
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/cedar-rapids/faq.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
Cedar RapidsChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy›Cedar Rapids›FAQ

Bankruptcy FAQ - Cedar Rapids, Iowa

Answers to the most common questions about filing bankruptcy in Cedar Rapids.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file for bankruptcy in Cedar Rapids?

You file through the Northern District of Iowa (N.D. Iowa) at 111 7th Ave SE, Cedar Rapids, IA 52401. Start by completing credit counseling, gathering financial documents, and consulting with an attorney. You then file a petition, attend a 341 meeting, and complete a debtor education course.

What exemptions apply in Cedar Rapids?

Iowa exemptions: UNLIMITED homestead (must be less than 1/2 acre in a city or 40 acres elsewhere), $7,000 vehicle. Iowa is one of the most debtor-friendly states in the country for homestead protection.

Will bankruptcy stop wage garnishment?

Yes. The moment you file, the automatic stay stops virtually all collection actions including wage garnishments, lawsuits, phone calls, and letters. Learn more about the automatic stay.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit report?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people see their credit scores begin recovering within 1-2 years of discharge.

Can I file bankruptcy without my spouse?

Yes, you can file individually even if you are married. However, your spouse's income may be considered for the means test, and joint debts will not be discharged as to your spouse.

Will I lose my retirement in bankruptcy?

No. Retirement accounts including 401(k)s, IRAs, pensions, and other ERISA-qualified plans are fully protected in bankruptcy with no dollar limit.

Can I file bankruptcy if I am unemployed?

Yes. Being unemployed does not prevent you from filing. In fact, having low or no income makes it more likely you will qualify for Chapter 7.

How often can I file bankruptcy?

For Chapter 7, you must wait 8 years between discharge dates. For Chapter 13, you must wait 2 years. If you received a Chapter 7 discharge, you must wait 4 years before receiving a Chapter 13 discharge. Use the 1328(f) screener to check your eligibility.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener
Means Test Guide

## Filing Bankruptcy in Cedar Rapids, Iowa - Complete Guide | Cedar Rapids Bankruptcy
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/cedar-rapids/

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
Cedar RapidsChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsFAQOBP

Filing Bankruptcy in Cedar Rapids, Iowa

Your complete guide to bankruptcy in Cedar Rapids. Which chapter to file, Iowa exemptions, courthouse locations, costs, and free local resources.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Unlimited

Homestead Exemption

$7,000

Vehicle Exemption

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

$313

Ch. 13 Filing Fee

Bankruptcy Basics in Cedar Rapids

If you are struggling with debt in Cedar Rapids, bankruptcy may give you a path to a fresh start. Most people file either Chapter 7 (liquidation) or Chapter 13 (repayment plan).

Cedar Rapids residents file through the Northern District of Iowa (N.D. Iowa). The courthouse is located at 111 7th Ave SE, Cedar Rapids, IA 52401.

Iowa exemptions: UNLIMITED homestead (must be less than 1/2 acre in a city or 40 acres elsewhere), $7,000 vehicle. Iowa is one of the most debtor-friendly states in the country for homestead protection.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 in Cedar Rapids

Chapter 7

Eliminates most unsecured debt

Takes 3-4 months

Must pass the means test

May lose non-exempt property

Filing fee: $338

Chapter 13

Repay debts over 3-5 years

Keep all your property

No means test required

Can catch up on mortgage/car payments

Filing fee: $313

Iowa Bankruptcy Exemptions

ExemptionAmountDetails

HomesteadUnlimitedEquity in your primary residence

Vehicle$7,000Equity in one motor vehicle

Wildcard$1,000Applied to any property

Full Cedar Rapids exemptions guide →

Local Resources

Courthouse

Northern District of Iowa (N.D. Iowa)
111 7th Ave SE, Cedar Rapids, IA 52401

Free Legal Aid

Iowa Legal Aid provides free bankruptcy assistance to qualifying low-income residents.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file for bankruptcy in Cedar Rapids?

You file for bankruptcy in Cedar Rapids through the Northern District of Iowa (N.D. Iowa). The courthouse is located at 111 7th Ave SE, Cedar Rapids, IA 52401. You will need to complete credit counseling, gather financial documents, and file a petition with the court. Most people hire an attorney, though you can file pro se.

What bankruptcy exemptions apply in Cedar Rapids?

Iowa exemptions: UNLIMITED homestead (must be less than 1/2 acre in a city or 40 acres elsewhere), $7,000 vehicle. Iowa is one of the most debtor-friendly states in the country for homestead protection.

How much does bankruptcy cost in Cedar Rapids?

Chapter 7 filing fees are $338 and Chapter 13 filing fees are $313. Attorney fees in Cedar Rapids typically range from $1,000 to $2,500 for Chapter 7 and $3,000 to $5,000 for Chapter 13.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13 in Cedar Rapids?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months but requires passing a means test. Chapter 13 lets you keep property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Your income, assets, and goals determine which is best.

Can I keep my house if I file bankruptcy in Cedar Rapids?

Iowa provides a Unlimited homestead exemption. If your equity is within that limit, you can keep your home in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home while catching up on missed payments.

Check Your Eligibility

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy Eligibility in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/chapter-13-eligibility.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Chapter 13 Eligibility

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy Eligibility in Iowa

Chapter 13 lets Iowa residents keep their property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Here are the eligibility requirements.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Core Eligibility Requirements

1. Regular Income

You must have regular income to fund a Chapter 13 repayment plan. Acceptable sources include:

Wages or salary

Self-employment income

Social Security benefits

Pension or retirement income

Rental income

Regular contributions from a spouse or household member

2. Debt Limits

Your total debts (secured plus unsecured) must not exceed $2,750,000. This combined limit was established by the Bankruptcy Threshold Adjustment and Technical Corrections Act of 2022.

3. Tax Filing

You must have filed all required federal and state tax returns for the four years before filing.

4. Credit Counseling

You must complete a credit counseling course from an approved provider within 180 days before filing.

Plan Length: 3 Years vs. 5 Years

Below Median Income

If your household income is at or below the Iowa median, your plan can be as short as 36 months (3 years) and no longer than 60 months (5 years).

Above Median Income

If your household income exceeds the Iowa median, your plan must be 60 months (5 years). This is called the applicable commitment period.

Prior Filing Bars for Chapter 13

Under 11 U.S.C. section 1328(f), you cannot receive a Chapter 13 discharge if:

You received a Chapter 7 discharge within the past 4 years

You received a Chapter 13 discharge within the past 2 years

These time bars only prevent a discharge - you can still file Chapter 13 to use the automatic stay and structured repayment even without eligibility for a discharge.

Check your eligibility with the 1328(f) Screener →

Chapter 13 Advantages in Iowa

Keep your home: Catch up on mortgage arrears while protecting up to unlimited in equity

Keep your car: Restructure auto loans (potential cramdown if older than 910 days) with $7,000 exemption protection

No means test barrier: Unlike Chapter 7, high earners can file Chapter 13

Co-debtor stay: Protects co-signers on consumer debts during the plan

Broader discharge: Some debts not dischargeable in Chapter 7 may be discharged in Chapter 13

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the debt limits for Chapter 13 in Iowa?

As of 2024, the debt limit for Chapter 13 is $2,750,000 in combined secured and unsecured debts. This limit was updated by the Bankruptcy Threshold Adjustment and Technical Corrections Act. If your debts exceed this amount, you may need to file Chapter 11 instead.

Do I need regular income to file Chapter 13?

Yes. Chapter 13 requires regular income sufficient to fund a repayment plan. This can include wages, salary, commissions, self-employment income, Social Security, pension, rental income, or even consistent support from a spouse or domestic partner.

How long is a Chapter 13 plan in Iowa?

If your income is below the Iowa median for your household size, your plan can be 3-5 years. If your income is above the median, your plan must be 5 years. The plan cannot exceed 5 years (60 months).

Check Your Eligibility

See if prior filings affect your Chapter 13 discharge eligibility.

## Chapter 13 Payments in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/chapter-13-payments.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Chapter 13 Payments

Chapter 13 Payments in Iowa

Your Chapter 13 plan payment is based on your income, expenses, and debts. Here is how it works in Iowa and what to do if you cannot keep up.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

How Payments Are Calculated

Three tests determine your minimum Chapter 13 payment:

1. Disposable Income Test

Your monthly income minus reasonable living expenses (IRS standards and actual secured debt payments). All disposable income must go into the plan.

2. Liquidation Test (Best Interest of Creditors)

Unsecured creditors must receive at least what they would get if you filed Chapter 7. If you have significant non-exempt assets, this raises your payment.

3. Secured and Priority Debt Test

Your plan must pay all priority debts in full (taxes, child support arrears) and cure any secured debt arrears (mortgage, car loan). These are non-negotiable minimums.

Plan Length in Iowa

Below Median Income

36-60 months. Your plan can be as short as 3 years but cannot exceed 5 years. Shorter plans mean higher monthly payments but less time in bankruptcy.

Above Median Income

60 months (required). If your income exceeds the Iowa median for your household size, you must commit to a full 5-year plan.

What Gets Paid in a Chapter 13 Plan

Debt TypeTreatment

Mortgage arrearsPaid in full to cure default and keep home (homestead exemption: unlimited)

Car loanPaid through plan, possible cramdown if older than 910 days

Priority taxesPaid in full over plan length

Child support arrearsPaid in full

Unsecured debtPaid 0-100% depending on disposable income

Trustee feesTypically 5-10% of plan payments, built into payment amount

Missing Payments and Modification

If you fall behind on Chapter 13 payments:

Contact the trustee immediately - many will work with you on temporary hardship

File a plan modification if the change is permanent (job loss, pay cut, medical issues)

Hardship discharge may be available under section 1328(b) if you have paid the equivalent of what Chapter 7 creditors would have received

Convert to Chapter 7 if you no longer have sufficient income for any plan

Do not simply stop paying. Communicate with the trustee and your attorney to explore all options before your case is dismissed.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much will my Chapter 13 payment be in Iowa?

Your monthly payment depends on your disposable income (income minus allowed expenses), the amount of secured and priority debts you must pay, and the liquidation test (unsecured creditors must receive at least what they would get in Chapter 7). Most Iowa Chapter 13 payments range from a few hundred to a few thousand dollars per month.

What happens if I miss a Chapter 13 payment?

Missing payments can result in the trustee filing a motion to dismiss your case. If your case is dismissed, you lose the protection of the automatic stay and all the progress you have made. Contact the trustee immediately if you are going to be late - many allow temporary payment modifications.

Can I change my Chapter 13 payment amount?

Yes. You can file a modified plan with the N.D. Iowa and S.D. Iowa court if your financial situation changes. Common reasons include job loss, pay cut, medical emergency, or increased expenses. The modified plan must still meet all legal requirements.

Explore Your Options

Iowa Chapter 13 Guide
Discharge Screener

Browse Other States

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Iowa - Payment Plans, Timeline
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/chapter-13.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Chapter 13

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Iowa

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Understanding the process and your Iowa exemptions is critical to success.

3-5 yrs

Plan Duration

$313

Filing Fee

Unlimited

Homestead Exemption

What Is Chapter 13?

Chapter 13, sometimes called a "wage earner's plan," allows people with regular income to create a court-supervised repayment plan lasting 3 to 5 years. You make monthly payments to a bankruptcy trustee, who distributes the money to your creditors according to the plan.

Unlike Chapter 7, Chapter 13 does not require liquidating assets. You keep all your property. At the end of the plan, remaining eligible debts are discharged.

Chapter 13 is particularly useful for:

Saving a home from foreclosure by catching up on missed mortgage payments

Keeping a car while catching up on auto loan payments

Paying tax debts or other priority debts over time

Protecting non-exempt property that would be lost in Chapter 7

Filers who do not qualify for Chapter 7 under the means test

How a Chapter 13 Plan Works

The Monthly Payment

Your monthly payment is calculated based on your disposable income - the difference between your income and reasonable living expenses. The payment must cover:

Priority debts in full: Recent taxes, child support arrears, attorney fees

Secured debt arrears: Mortgage arrears, car loan arrears

Trustee fees: Typically around 10% of plan payments

Unsecured creditors: Whatever remains goes to credit cards, medical bills - often pennies on the dollar

Plan Duration: 3 or 5 Years?

If your income is below the Iowa median, you may be able to complete a 3-year plan. If your income is above the median, your plan must be 5 years. The court will not approve a plan longer than 5 years.

What Happens at the End?

After completing all plan payments, the court enters a discharge under 11 U.S.C. section 1328. This eliminates remaining eligible unsecured debts. You keep your home, car, and other property.

If you received a prior discharge, time bars under section 1328(f) may prevent you from receiving another discharge.

Chapter 13 Timeline

Before filing: Complete credit counseling ($15-25). Gather income documentation, tax returns, and debt records.

Day 1 - Filing: Petition filed. Automatic stay takes effect. Foreclosures, repossessions, and garnishments stop immediately.

Day 14: Proposed plan filed (if not filed with the petition).

Day 30: First plan payment due to the trustee.

Day 30-45: 341 meeting of creditors. The trustee reviews your plan and finances.

Day 45-120: Confirmation hearing. The court decides whether to approve your plan.

Years 1-5: Monthly payments to the trustee. Report income changes. File tax returns on time.

Plan completion: Complete debtor education course. Court enters discharge.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long is a Chapter 13 repayment plan?

Chapter 13 plans last 3 to 5 years. If your income is below the Iowa median, you may qualify for a 3-year plan. If above the median, your plan must be 5 years. During this time, you make monthly payments to a trustee who distributes the money to your creditors.

Can I keep my house in Chapter 13?

Yes. One of the main benefits of Chapter 13 is the ability to keep your home and catch up on missed mortgage payments through your repayment plan. The automatic stay stops foreclosure proceedings immediately upon filing. Iowa's homestead exemption of Unlimited also applies.

What happens if my Chapter 13 is dismissed?

If your Chapter 13 case is dismissed, the automatic stay ends and creditors can resume collection activity. You lose the protection of the bankruptcy court. Any payments you made through the plan were distributed to creditors, but you do not receive a discharge of your remaining debts. Learn more.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

Ch. 7 vs 13

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy Eligibility in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/chapter-7-eligibility.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Chapter 7 Eligibility

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy Eligibility in Iowa

Chapter 7 bankruptcy can eliminate most unsecured debt in 3-4 months. Here is how to determine whether you qualify to file in Iowa.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

The Means Test

The primary eligibility requirement for Chapter 7 is the means test under 11 U.S.C. section 707(b). It has two parts:

Step 1: Income Comparison

Compare your current monthly income (averaged over the 6 months before filing) to the Iowa median income for your household size. If your income is at or below the median, you automatically pass.

Step 2: Expense Deductions (If Above Median)

If your income exceeds the median, you deduct allowed expenses (IRS standards plus actual secured debt payments) from your income. If the remaining disposable income is below the threshold, you still qualify for Chapter 7.

Tip: Use the means test calculator to check your eligibility before paying for a consultation.

Prior Filing Bars

Under 11 U.S.C. section 727(a)(8) and (a)(9), you cannot receive a Chapter 7 discharge if:

You received a Chapter 7 discharge within the past 8 years

You received a Chapter 13 discharge within the past 6 years (unless you paid 100% of unsecured claims or at least 70% in a good-faith plan)

Use the 1328(f) Screener to check whether prior filings affect your eligibility.

Other Eligibility Requirements

Credit counseling: Must complete an approved course within 180 days before filing

No recent dismissals for cause: If a case was dismissed in the past 180 days for violating a court order or failure to appear, you cannot file immediately

Domicile requirement: You file in the N.D. Iowa and S.D. Iowa if you have lived in Iowa for the greater portion of the past 180 days

No fraud: Fraudulent transfers or concealment of assets can bar discharge under section 727(a)(2)

Tax returns: You must provide your most recent tax return to the trustee at least 7 days before the 341 meeting

Iowa Exemption Snapshot

If you qualify for Chapter 7, your exempt property is protected from liquidation:

Homestead: unlimited

Vehicle: $7,000

Complete Iowa exemptions list →

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the income limits for Chapter 7 in Iowa?

To qualify for Chapter 7 in Iowa, your household income must be at or below the state median income for your household size. If above median, you must pass the means test, which deducts allowed expenses from your income. If the result shows insufficient disposable income, you still qualify. Current Iowa median income figures are updated twice yearly.

What disqualifies you from filing Chapter 7?

You may be disqualified from Chapter 7 if: you received a Chapter 7 discharge within the past 8 years, a Chapter 13 discharge within the past 6 years, your income is too high to pass the means test, you committed bankruptcy fraud, or you had a case dismissed within 180 days for specific reasons.

Do I have to take a class before filing Chapter 7 in Iowa?

Yes. You must complete a credit counseling course from an approved provider within 180 days before filing. After filing, you must also complete a financial management course before receiving your discharge. Both courses can be done online and typically cost $25-$50 each.

Check Your Eligibility Now

Our free screener checks prior filings and time bars instantly.

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Iowa - Eligibility, Exemptions, Timeline
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/chapter-7.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Chapter 7

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Iowa

Chapter 7 is the fastest path to debt relief. Most unsecured debts are eliminated in 3-4 months. Here is what Iowa residents need to know.

What Is Chapter 7?

Chapter 7, sometimes called "liquidation bankruptcy" or a "fresh start," eliminates most unsecured debts. A court-appointed trustee reviews your assets. If you have non-exempt property, the trustee may sell it to pay creditors - but most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases where the filer keeps everything.

What Debts Does Chapter 7 Eliminate?

Dischargeable (eliminated)

Credit card debt

Medical bills

Personal loans and payday loans

Old utility bills

Deficiency balances from repossessions

Some older tax debts

Civil judgments (in most cases)

Not dischargeable

Student loans (except in rare hardship cases)

Recent income taxes (generally last 3 years)

Child support and alimony

Court-ordered restitution and fines

Debts from fraud or willful injury

Certain debts under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)

The Means Test in Iowa

To file Chapter 7, you must pass the means test. This compares your household income to the Iowa median:

Below median income: You automatically qualify for Chapter 7

Above median income: A detailed calculation of your disposable income determines eligibility

For a single filer in Iowa, the current median is approximately $50,000/year. These figures increase with household size.

Iowa Exemptions in Chapter 7

Exemptions determine what property you keep.

PropertyIowa Exemption

HomesteadUnlimited

VehicleUp to $7,000 (one motor vehicle)

Household goods$7,000 total

Wildcard$1,000

Tools of trade$10,000

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exempt

Social Security benefitsFully exempt

Full exemption details →

Chapter 7 Timeline

Weeks 1-2: Preparation - Gather documents (tax returns, pay stubs, bank statements). Complete credit counseling course ($15-25).

Day 1: Filing - Petition filed with the court. Automatic stay takes effect immediately - creditors must stop all collection activity.

Day 30-45: 341 Meeting - Brief meeting of creditors. The trustee asks questions under oath about your finances. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Day 30-60: Debtor education - Complete the second required financial education course ($10-25).

Day 90-120: Discharge - The court enters your discharge order, eliminating eligible debts.

Frequently Asked Questions

What debts does Chapter 7 eliminate?

Chapter 7 discharges most unsecured debts including credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and old utility bills. It does not discharge student loans (in most cases), recent taxes, child support, alimony, or debts incurred through fraud. See section 523(a) for the full list.

What is the means test for Chapter 7 in Iowa?

The means test compares your household income to the Iowa median. If your income is below the median for your household size, you automatically qualify. If above, a more detailed calculation determines whether you have enough disposable income to fund a Chapter 13 plan instead. For a single filer in Iowa, the current median is approximately $50,000/year.

How long does Chapter 7 take?

From filing to discharge, Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months. The 341 meeting of creditors occurs about 30 days after filing, and the discharge is usually entered about 60 days after that.

What property can I keep in Chapter 7 in Iowa?

Iowa exemptions protect: homestead equity up to Unlimited, vehicle equity up to Up to $7,000 (one motor vehicle), household goods up to $7,000 total, and retirement accounts are fully exempt. Full exemption details.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy Filing Checklist for Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/checklist.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesCedar RapidsChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Checklist

Bankruptcy Filing Checklist for Iowa

Filing bankruptcy requires careful preparation. Use this comprehensive checklist to gather everything you need before filing in Iowa.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Income Documents

Last 6 months of pay stubs (all jobs)

Last 2 years of federal tax returns (filed copies)

Last 2 years of Iowa state tax returns

Current year income documentation (W-2s, 1099s)

Social Security or disability income statements

Unemployment compensation documentation

Pension or retirement income statements

Rental income documentation

Child support or alimony received

Any other sources of income

Asset Documents

Bank statements (all accounts, last 6 months)

Vehicle titles or registration for all vehicles

Most recent mortgage statement

Home value estimate (Zillow, Redfin, or appraisal)

Retirement account statements (401k, IRA, pension)

Life insurance policies (cash value)

Investment and brokerage account statements

List of personal property with estimated values

Business ownership documents (if applicable)

Any property held by others on your behalf

Debt Documents

List of all creditors with names, addresses, account numbers, and balances

Recent statements for all credit cards

Medical bills and collection notices

Student loan statements

Auto loan statements

Personal loan documentation

Judgments, liens, or garnishment orders

Tax debts (IRS notices, state tax notices)

Past-due utility bills

Payday loan documentation

Any debts from lawsuits or legal disputes

Required Steps Before Filing

1

Credit Counseling

Complete an approved credit counseling course within 180 days before filing. Cost: $15-$50. Available online.

2

Credit Reports

Pull free reports from AnnualCreditReport.com to verify all debts are listed.

3

Means Test

Calculate if you qualify for Chapter 7 based on Iowa median income.

Iowa Exemption Quick Reference

Key Iowa Exemptions

Homestead: Unlimited

Vehicle: $7,000

Federal exemptions: Not available (must use state exemptions)

Full Iowa exemptions guide →

Filing Fees

Chapter 7: $338

Chapter 13: $313

Fee waiver: Available for income below 150% of poverty level

Installments: Court may allow payment in installments

Where to File in Iowa

Court: U.S. Bankruptcy Court, 123 E Walnut St, Des Moines, IA 50309 (S.D. Iowa)

Districts: Iowa has N.D. Iowa and S.D. Iowa. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Legal assistance: Iowa Legal Aid (iowalegalaid.org) may provide free or reduced-cost help with your filing.

Frequently Asked Questions

What documents do I need to file bankruptcy in Iowa?

You will need: last 6 months of pay stubs, last 2 years of tax returns, bank statements for all accounts, a list of all debts with account numbers and balances, vehicle titles or registration, mortgage statements, and a list of all property you own with estimated values.

How long does it take to prepare a bankruptcy filing?

Gathering documents and completing the required credit counseling course typically takes 1-4 weeks. The actual filing can be done in a day once everything is prepared.

Do I need a lawyer to file bankruptcy?

While you can file pro se (without a lawyer), bankruptcy is complex. Mistakes can cost you property, result in case dismissal, or lead to debts not being discharged. Iowa Legal Aid (iowalegalaid.org) may provide free or low-cost assistance if you cannot afford an attorney.

What is the means test?

The means test compares your household income to Iowa's median income. If your income is below the median, you automatically qualify for Chapter 7. If above, additional calculations determine eligibility. The median income varies by household size.

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy?

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338. Chapter 13 filing fee: $313. Attorney fees vary but typically range from $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13. You may be able to pay in installments or qualify for a fee waiver.

Ready to Get Started?

Check your eligibility and learn about your options.

Free Discharge Screener

## Converting Bankruptcy Chapters in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/converting-chapters.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Converting Chapters

Converting Bankruptcy Chapters in Iowa

If your financial situation changes after filing, Iowa debtors can convert between bankruptcy chapters. Understanding your conversion rights can help you get the best outcome.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Conversion Options

Chapter 7 to Chapter 13

One-time right of conversion under section 706(a)

Common when you have assets at risk of liquidation

Useful when income increases or you want to save property

Must be able to propose a feasible repayment plan

No additional filing fee

Chapter 13 to Chapter 7

Right to convert at any time under section 1307(a)

Not available if case was originally Chapter 7 and converted to 13

Common when income drops and plan payments become unaffordable

Must still pass the means test if required

No additional filing fee

Common Reasons for Conversion in Iowa

Job loss or income reduction - Chapter 13 plan payments become unaffordable, convert to Chapter 7

Income increase - No longer pass the means test for Chapter 7, convert to Chapter 13

Property at risk - Chapter 7 trustee identifies non-exempt property, convert to Chapter 13 to protect it

Mortgage arrears - Need Chapter 13's structured repayment to catch up on missed mortgage payments and keep your home (homestead exemption: unlimited)

Plan failure - Unable to complete Chapter 13 plan, convert to Chapter 7 for immediate discharge

What Happens When You Convert

File a motion or notice of conversion with the N.D. Iowa and S.D. Iowa bankruptcy court

New schedules may be required - particularly if significant time has passed or circumstances have changed

New trustee is assigned - Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 trustees are different

The automatic stay continues - no gap in protection

A new 341 meeting may be scheduled - the new trustee needs to examine you

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I convert my bankruptcy case to a different chapter?

Yes. Under 11 U.S.C. section 706 and section 1307, you generally have the right to convert between chapters. Chapter 7 debtors can convert to Chapter 13 once as a matter of right. Chapter 13 debtors can convert to Chapter 7 at any time if they have not previously converted from Chapter 7.

Does converting restart my case?

Converting does not start a completely new case. Your case number stays the same, and the automatic stay remains in effect. However, new schedules and forms may need to be filed, and certain deadlines reset.

Are there fees to convert?

There is no additional filing fee to convert from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7. Converting from Chapter 7 to Chapter 13 requires paying the difference in filing fees ($313 minus $338, so no additional fee). Attorney fees may apply for the additional work.

Not Sure Which Chapter Is Right?

Compare the two main consumer bankruptcy chapters side by side.

Ch. 7 vs 13 Guide
Discharge Screener

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Iowa? - Filing Fees, Attorney Fees
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/cost.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Cost

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Iowa?

Bankruptcy has real costs, but they are often far less than the debt you are trying to eliminate. Here is a transparent breakdown of every cost you should expect.

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

$25-50

Credit Counseling (2 courses)

Chapter 7 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$338Can pay in installments; fee waiver available

Attorney fees$1,000 to $1,800Typical Iowa range; usually paid upfront

Pre-filing credit counseling$15 - $25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10 - $25Required before discharge

Credit report$0 - $30Free from annualcreditreport.com

Fee Waiver (Chapter 7 Only)

If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines, you can apply to have the $338 filing fee waived entirely. You file the fee waiver application (Official Form 103B) with your petition.

Chapter 13 Cost Breakdown

Cost ItemAmountNotes

Court filing fee$313Can be paid through your plan

Attorney fees$2,500 to $4,000Typical Iowa range; mostly paid through plan

Pre-filing credit counseling$15 - $25Required within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10 - $25Required before discharge

The Chapter 13 Payment Advantage

Attorney fees and the filing fee can be included in your repayment plan. You typically need only a small upfront payment (often $0-$500), with the rest paid through your monthly plan payments over 3-5 years.

Ways to Reduce Costs

Free and Low-Cost Options

Iowa Legal Aid - Free legal services for low-income Iowans, including bankruptcy assistance.

Fee waiver - eliminates the $338 Chapter 7 filing fee for income below 150% of poverty

Installment payments - pay the filing fee in up to 4 installments over 120 days

Pro se filing - filing without an attorney is possible but not recommended for most filers

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Iowa?

The Chapter 7 filing fee is $338. Attorney fees in Iowa typically range from $1,000 to $1,800. Credit counseling courses cost about $25-50 total (two required). Fee waivers are available if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines. National cost comparison.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Iowa?

The Chapter 13 filing fee is $313. Attorney fees typically range from $2,500 to $4,000 in Iowa. The filing fee and attorney fees can be paid through your repayment plan, so you usually do not need to pay everything upfront.

Can I file bankruptcy for free?

The court filing fee can be waived in Chapter 7 if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines. Legal aid organizations in Iowa may provide free or reduced-cost legal representation for qualifying individuals.

Do I have to pay an attorney upfront?

For Chapter 7, most attorneys require full payment before filing. For Chapter 13, attorney fees are typically included in your repayment plan, so you may only need a small upfront payment.

## Rebuilding Credit After Bankruptcy in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/credit-after-bankruptcy.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesCedar RapidsChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Credit After Bankruptcy

Rebuilding Credit After Bankruptcy in Iowa

Bankruptcy is not the end of your financial life - it is a fresh start. Here is a practical guide to rebuilding your credit after bankruptcy in Iowa.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

The Credit Score Timeline

Your credit score will drop significantly when you file bankruptcy, but the trajectory improves from there. Here is what to expect:

Filing day: Your score drops, but if you were already behind on payments, the drop may be smaller than expected

Discharge (3-4 months for Ch. 7): The baseline from which rebuilding begins

6-12 months post-discharge: Score begins climbing with responsible credit use

12-24 months: Many filers reach 650+ with disciplined rebuilding

2-3 years: Scores of 700+ are achievable

4+ years: FHA and conventional mortgage options become available

Step-by-Step Rebuilding Strategy

1

Check Your Reports

Get free reports from AnnualCreditReport.com. Verify all discharged debts show $0 balance. Dispute any errors.

2

Secured Credit Card

Open a secured card with a $200-$500 deposit. Use it for one small recurring purchase. Pay in full monthly.

3

Credit Builder Loan

Many credit unions in Iowa offer credit builder loans. The money goes into savings while you make payments, building payment history.

4

Authorized User

Ask a trusted family member to add you as an authorized user on their card. Their positive payment history helps your score.

5

Unsecured Card

After 6-12 months of on-time payments, apply for an unsecured card. Keep utilization below 30%.

6

Diversify Credit

Over time, add different types of credit (installment loan, auto loan). Payment diversity improves your score mix.

Buying a Home After Bankruptcy in Iowa

After Chapter 7

FHA loan: 2 years after discharge

VA loan: 2 years after discharge

USDA loan: 3 years after discharge

Conventional: 4 years after discharge

Iowa's homestead exemption of Unlimited will protect your new home equity in any future financial difficulty.

After Chapter 13

FHA loan: 1 year into plan (with court permission) or at discharge

VA loan: 1 year into plan or at discharge

USDA loan: 1 year after discharge

Conventional: 2 years after discharge

Chapter 13's shorter waiting periods are an advantage for those planning to buy a home.

Common Mistakes to Avoid

Applying for too much credit at once. Each application creates a hard inquiry that temporarily lowers your score. Space applications 3-6 months apart.

Carrying balances. Pay your credit card balance in full every month. Carrying a balance does not build credit faster - it just costs you interest.

Ignoring your credit reports. Check your reports regularly. Discharged debts sometimes still appear as owing a balance, which drags down your score.

Payday loans and predatory lenders. Avoid high-interest lenders that target people after bankruptcy. They trap you in a cycle of debt.

Co-signing loans. Do not co-sign for anyone until your own credit is fully rebuilt. If they default, you are responsible.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit report in Iowa?

Chapter 7 bankruptcy remains on your credit report for 10 years from the filing date. Chapter 13 remains for 7 years from the filing date. However, your credit score can begin improving much sooner than that.

Can I get a credit card after bankruptcy in Iowa?

Yes. Many people receive credit card offers shortly after discharge. Secured credit cards (where you provide a deposit) are the most common starting point. Use the card for small purchases and pay the balance in full each month to rebuild your credit score.

How quickly can my credit score improve after bankruptcy?

Many people see meaningful credit score improvement within 12-18 months after discharge. Some filers report scores above 700 within 2-3 years by following disciplined rebuilding strategies.

Can I buy a house after bankruptcy in Iowa?

Yes. FHA loans are available 2 years after a Chapter 7 discharge and 1 year into a Chapter 13 plan (with court permission). Conventional loans typically require a 4-year waiting period after Chapter 7 and 2 years after Chapter 13 discharge.

Can I buy a car after bankruptcy in Iowa?

Yes. Auto loans are available almost immediately after discharge, though interest rates will be higher initially. Rates improve as your credit score recovers. Putting a larger down payment can help secure better terms.

Check Your Discharge Eligibility

Use our free screener to check if you qualify for a bankruptcy discharge before taking steps to rebuild.

Free Discharge Screener
Credit Rebuilding Guide

Minnesota
Rhode Island
Vermont
