## Credit Card Debt and Bankruptcy in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/credit-card-debt.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Credit Card Debt

Credit Card Debt and Bankruptcy in Iowa

Credit card debt is one of the most common reasons Iowa residents file for bankruptcy. In most cases, credit card balances can be completely eliminated through Chapter 7 or significantly reduced through Chapter 13.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

How Each Chapter Handles Credit Card Debt

Chapter 7

Credit card debt is eliminated entirely in Chapter 7. The discharge typically comes in 3-4 months. You must pass the means test to qualify. Any non-exempt assets could be liquidated, but most Iowa filers keep all their property under the state's exemptions.

Chapter 13

Credit card debt is included in your 3-5 year repayment plan. You pay a percentage of the total based on your disposable income. Many filers pay only 10-30 cents on the dollar. The remaining balance is discharged at the end of the plan.

Recent Charges: The 90-Day and 70-Day Rules

Under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C), certain recent credit card activity is presumed to be non-dischargeable:

Luxury goods/services over $800 charged within 90 days before filing

Cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days before filing

These are rebuttable presumptions. The credit card company must file an adversary proceeding to challenge discharge, and you can present evidence that the charges were made with the intent and ability to repay.

Best practice: Stop using credit cards well before filing. Using cards when you know you will file bankruptcy can be considered fraud.

Credit Card Lawsuits and Judgments in Iowa

If a credit card company sues you in Iowa and obtains a judgment, they can:

Garnish your wages

Levy your bank account

Place a lien on your property

Filing bankruptcy stops all of these through the automatic stay. Pre-existing judgment liens on personal property can often be avoided (removed) under 11 U.S.C. section 522(f) if they impair your Iowa exemptions.

Iowa Exemptions Relevant to Credit Card Debt

Homestead: unlimited - protects home equity from credit card judgment liens

Vehicle: $7,000

Full Iowa exemptions guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I discharge credit card debt in Iowa bankruptcy?

Yes. Credit card debt is unsecured debt and is generally dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Iowa. In Chapter 7, it is eliminated entirely. In Chapter 13, you pay a percentage based on your disposable income over 3-5 years, and the rest is discharged.

Will recent credit card charges be discharged?

Charges of over $800 for luxury goods or services within 90 days of filing, and cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days of filing, are presumed non-dischargeable under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C). The creditor must object for this to apply.

Can creditors sue me for credit card debt?

Yes. In Iowa, credit card companies can sue you, obtain a judgment, and garnish your wages or levy your bank account. Filing bankruptcy stops these actions through the automatic stay.

Ready to Explore Your Options?

Find out if you qualify for a bankruptcy discharge to eliminate your credit card debt.

Discharge Screener
Cost Calculator

## Using Credit Cards Before Bankruptcy in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/credit-cards-before-filing.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Credit Cards Before Filing

Using Credit Cards Before Bankruptcy in Iowa

Stop using credit cards once you decide to file bankruptcy. Charges made near filing can be challenged as fraud - and the presumption works against you.

Quick answer: Do not use credit cards once you decide to file bankruptcy. Luxury purchases over $800 within 90 days and cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days are presumed fraudulent. These debts may survive your discharge.

The Two Presumption Periods

90-Day Rule: Luxury Goods

Applies to purchases of luxury goods/services

Threshold: $800+ to a single creditor

Within 90 days of filing

Presumed non-dischargeable (you must prove otherwise)

11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C)(i)(I)

70-Day Rule: Cash Advances

Applies to cash advances

Threshold: $1,100+ in aggregate

Within 70 days of filing

Presumed non-dischargeable (you must prove otherwise)

11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C)(i)(II)

What You Should Do

Stop Using Cards Immediately

The moment you decide to file bankruptcy, stop all credit card use. Here is why:

Every charge creates risk. Even necessary purchases can be challenged if made close to filing.

Intent matters. Using a credit card when you know you will not repay it is the definition of fraud in this context.

The burden shifts to you. Within the 90/70 day windows, YOU must prove the charges were not fraudulent. Outside those windows, the creditor must prove fraud.

What Charges Are Safe?

Groceries and essential food - not luxury, reasonably necessary

Gas for work commute - necessary for income

Medical expenses - necessary for health

Basic utilities - if auto-billed to the card, switch to bank payment

Even "safe" charges should be avoided if possible. Use cash or debit instead. The cleaner your pre-filing credit card activity, the smoother your case.

What Happens If Charges Are Challenged

If a credit card company believes you ran up charges with no intent to repay, they can file an adversary proceeding - a lawsuit within your bankruptcy case. The process:

Creditor files a complaint alleging non-dischargeability under section 523(a)(2)

You must respond within 30 days

Discovery phase: both sides exchange evidence (statements, purchase records)

Trial or settlement: most adversary proceedings settle before trial

If the creditor wins, those specific charges survive your discharge

Adversary proceedings are expensive and time-consuming for both sides. Creditors typically only file them for significant amounts ($2,000+). But avoiding the risk entirely is the smartest strategy.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I use credit cards before filing bankruptcy in Iowa?

Technically you can, but it is strongly discouraged. Charges made with no intent to repay can be challenged as fraud. Luxury purchases over $800 within 90 days of filing are presumed fraudulent. Cash advances over $1,100 within 70 days are also presumed fraudulent.

What is the 90-day rule for credit cards before bankruptcy?

Under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C), charges for luxury goods or services exceeding $800 made within 90 days of filing bankruptcy are presumed to be non-dischargeable. The creditor does not have to prove fraud - you must prove the charges were not fraudulent.

What is the 70-day rule for cash advances?

Cash advances exceeding $1,100 taken within 70 days of filing are presumed non-dischargeable under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)(C). This includes balance transfers, convenience checks, and ATM cash advances.

What counts as a luxury purchase?

Luxury goods and services are items not reasonably necessary for support or maintenance. Groceries, gas, medical expenses, and basic clothing are not luxury. Electronics, jewelry, vacations, expensive dining, and entertainment are typically considered luxury.

What happens if I used credit cards right before filing?

The credit card company may file an adversary proceeding arguing the charges are non-dischargeable due to fraud. If the charges are within the 90/70 day presumption periods, you bear the burden of proving they were not fraudulent. Charges outside those windows require the creditor to prove actual fraud.

Should I close my credit cards before filing bankruptcy?

Do not close accounts before filing - it does not help and can look like you are trying to hide something. Simply stop using the cards. They will be listed in your bankruptcy schedules and the issuers will close them after receiving notice of your filing.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Garnishment
FAQ

## Types of Debt in Iowa Bankruptcy
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/debt-types.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesCedar RapidsChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Debt Types

Types of Debt in Iowa Bankruptcy

Not all debts are treated equally in bankruptcy. Understanding which debts can be eliminated and which survive is essential for making the right filing decision in Iowa.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Dischargeable Debts (Can Be Eliminated)

Credit card debt: Fully dischargeable in Chapter 7 and Chapter 13

Medical bills: Fully dischargeable - the most common reason for filing

Personal loans: Including payday loans, signature loans, and promissory notes

Utility arrears: Past-due electric, gas, water, and phone bills

Old tax debt: Income taxes meeting the 3-year/2-year/240-day tests

Deficiency balances: Remaining debt after repossession or foreclosure

Lawsuit judgments: Most money judgments from civil lawsuits

Lease obligations: Remaining rent or equipment lease payments

HOA fees: Past-due homeowner association assessments (pre-filing)

Non-Dischargeable Debts (Usually Survive)

Student loans: Generally survive unless you prove undue hardship in an adversary proceeding

Child support and alimony: Domestic support obligations are never dischargeable

Recent taxes: Tax debts that do not meet the discharge requirements

Fraud-based debts: Debts obtained through fraud, false pretenses, or false financial statements

DUI/DWI injury debts: Debts for death or personal injury caused by intoxicated driving

Criminal fines and restitution: Government fines and criminal restitution orders

Willful and malicious injury: Debts from intentional harm to person or property

Unlisted debts: Debts you fail to include in your bankruptcy schedules

Secured vs. Unsecured Debt

Secured Debt

Backed by collateral. The creditor has a lien on specific property.

Mortgage (secured by your home)

Car loan (secured by your vehicle)

Furniture/appliance loans

Home equity loans (HELOCs)

In bankruptcy: You typically must surrender the collateral, reaffirm the debt, or redeem the property. In Chapter 13, you can catch up on arrears through your plan.

Unsecured Debt

No collateral. The creditor's only remedy is to sue you for a money judgment.

Credit cards

Medical bills

Personal loans

Collection accounts

In bankruptcy: Usually eliminated entirely in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you pay what you can afford and the rest is discharged.

Priority Debt

Priority debts receive special treatment and must be paid in full in a Chapter 13 plan:

Domestic support obligations: Child support and alimony arrears

Certain tax debts: Recent income taxes, trust fund taxes

Employee wages: Unpaid wages earned within 180 days before filing (up to a limit)

Administrative expenses: Costs of administering the bankruptcy case

Frequently Asked Questions

What debts can be discharged in bankruptcy?

Most unsecured debts can be discharged, including credit card debt, medical bills, personal loans, utility arrears, old tax debt (if it meets age requirements), and deficiency balances from repossession or foreclosure. Some debts like student loans, recent taxes, child support, and fraud-based debts generally cannot be discharged.

What is the difference between secured and unsecured debt?

Secured debt is backed by collateral (like a mortgage or car loan). If you do not pay, the creditor can take the collateral. Unsecured debt has no collateral (like credit cards or medical bills). In bankruptcy, unsecured debt is usually eliminated entirely, while secured debt may require you to return the collateral or keep paying.

What debts cannot be discharged in bankruptcy?

Non-dischargeable debts include: most student loans (absent undue hardship), child support and alimony, recent tax debts, debts from fraud or willful injury, criminal fines and restitution, and debts not listed in your bankruptcy schedules.

What is priority debt?

Priority debts are given special treatment in bankruptcy. They must be paid in full in Chapter 13 plans. Examples include recent tax debts, child support arrears, alimony arrears, and certain employee wages. Priority debts generally cannot be discharged in Chapter 7.

Does Iowa treat any debts differently?

Bankruptcy law is federal, so debt treatment is largely the same everywhere. However, Iowa's exemptions determine what property you can protect: Unlimited homestead, $7,000 vehicle. State law also governs the statute of limitations on debt collection and garnishment rules.

Find Out Which Debts You Can Eliminate

Use our free screener to check your discharge eligibility.

## 341 Meeting of Creditors in Des Moines, Iowa - What to Expect
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/des-moines/341-meeting.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
Des MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy›Des Moines›341 Meeting

341 Meeting of Creditors in Des Moines

The 341 meeting is a required step in every bankruptcy case. Here is what Des Moines residents can expect.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Is the 341 Meeting?

The 341 meeting (named after Section 341 of the Bankruptcy Code) is a brief hearing where the bankruptcy trustee asks you questions about your finances and petition. Despite its name, creditors rarely attend.

The meeting typically occurs about 30 days after you file your petition at the Southern District of Iowa (S.D. Iowa).

What to Expect

1Check in - Arrive early. You and your attorney will check in with the trustee.

2Oath - You will be placed under oath to tell the truth.

3Questions - The trustee asks standard questions: Did you list all your assets? Is the information in your petition accurate? Have you made any large transfers recently?

4Duration - Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

5Adjournment - The trustee may conclude the meeting or continue it to request additional documents.

Frequently Asked Questions

What happens at the 341 meeting?

The trustee asks you questions under oath about your financial situation, assets, and the information in your petition. The meeting typically lasts 5-10 minutes. Creditors may attend but rarely do.

Where is the 341 meeting held?

341 meetings for Des Moines cases are held at or near the Southern District of Iowa (S.D. Iowa) courthouse at 123 E. Walnut St, Des Moines, IA 50309. Some meetings may be conducted by telephone or video.

What should I bring to the 341 meeting?

Bring a government-issued photo ID, proof of Social Security number, your most recent tax return, recent pay stubs, and bank statements. Your attorney will provide a complete checklist.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

## Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Des Moines, Iowa - Repayment Plans & Eligibility
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/des-moines/chapter-13.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
Des MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy›Des Moines›Chapter 13

Chapter 13 Bankruptcy in Des Moines, Iowa

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Here is how it works for Des Moines residents.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

3-5 yrs

Plan Duration

$313

Filing Fee

Unlimited

Homestead Exemption

No

Means Test Required

What Is Chapter 13 Bankruptcy?

Chapter 13 bankruptcy - often called the "wage earner's plan" - lets you reorganize your debts into a manageable repayment plan. Unlike Chapter 7, you keep all of your property while making payments over 3-5 years.

Chapter 13 is particularly useful if you are behind on mortgage payments, need to save a home from foreclosure, or have income that exceeds the Chapter 7 means test threshold.

How Chapter 13 Works in Des Moines

1Credit counseling - Complete an approved course before filing.

2File petition and plan - Submit your petition and proposed repayment plan to the Southern District of Iowa (S.D. Iowa). The automatic stay takes effect immediately.

3Begin payments - You must begin making plan payments within 30 days of filing, even before the plan is confirmed.

4341 meeting - Meet briefly with the trustee approximately 30 days after filing.

5Confirmation hearing - The court reviews and approves your plan, typically 30-45 days after the 341 meeting.

6Complete plan payments - Make all payments over 3-5 years.

7Discharge - After completing all payments, remaining qualifying unsecured debts are discharged.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13

Chapter 7

Eliminates debt in 3-4 months

Must pass means test

May lose non-exempt property

Cannot catch up on mortgage arrears

Chapter 13

3-5 year repayment plan

No means test

Keep all property

Can cure mortgage defaults

Detailed Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 comparison →

Frequently Asked Questions

How does Chapter 13 work in Des Moines?

Chapter 13 lets you reorganize your debts into a 3-5 year repayment plan. You make monthly payments to a trustee who distributes funds to creditors. At the end of the plan, remaining qualifying debts are discharged.

How long is a Chapter 13 plan?

If your income is below the state median, your plan can be as short as 3 years. If above the median, the plan must be 5 years. The maximum is 5 years regardless of income.

Can I keep my house in Chapter 13?

Yes. Chapter 13 is specifically designed to help you keep your home. You can catch up on missed mortgage payments through your plan while maintaining current payments. Iowa's Unlimited homestead exemption provides additional protection.

How much does Chapter 13 cost?

The court filing fee is $313. Attorney fees in Des Moines typically range from $3,000 to $5,000 and are usually paid through the plan itself, so you do not need the full amount up front.

What debts must I repay in Chapter 13?

Priority debts (taxes, child support) must be paid in full. Secured debts (mortgage, car loans) must be kept current. Unsecured creditors receive a percentage based on your disposable income - sometimes as low as 0%.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

## Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Des Moines, Iowa - Eligibility, Process & Cost
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/des-moines/chapter-7.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
Des MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy›Des Moines›Chapter 7

Chapter 7 Bankruptcy in Des Moines, Iowa

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months. Here is how it works for Des Moines residents, including eligibility requirements, Iowa exemptions, and what to expect.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

3-4 mo

Average Timeline

$338

Filing Fee

$55,724

Median Income (1 person)

Unlimited

Homestead Exemption

What Is Chapter 7 Bankruptcy?

Chapter 7, sometimes called "liquidation bankruptcy," eliminates most unsecured debts - credit cards, medical bills, personal loans - in exchange for surrendering non-exempt assets. In practice, most Chapter 7 cases are "no-asset" cases, meaning filers keep everything they own.

Des Moines residents file Chapter 7 through the Southern District of Iowa (S.D. Iowa) at 123 E. Walnut St, Des Moines, IA 50309.

Means Test: Do You Qualify?

The means test compares your income to Iowa's median income. If you are below the median for your household size, you automatically qualify.

Household SizeAnnual Median Income

1 person$55,724

2 persons$72,116

3 persons$85,880

4 persons$104,316

If your income exceeds the median, you may still qualify after deducting allowed expenses. If not, Chapter 13 is an alternative that does not require a means test.

Chapter 7 Timeline in Des Moines

1Credit counseling - Complete an approved course within 180 days before filing.

2File petition - Submit forms to the Southern District of Iowa (S.D. Iowa). The automatic stay takes effect immediately, stopping collections, garnishments, and lawsuits.

3341 meeting - Approximately 30 days after filing, you attend a brief meeting with the trustee. Creditors may attend but rarely do.

4Debtor education - Complete a second financial management course before discharge.

5Discharge - Approximately 60 days after the 341 meeting, the court enters your discharge order eliminating qualifying debts.

Iowa Exemptions in Chapter 7

Iowa exemptions: UNLIMITED homestead (must be less than 1/2 acre in a city or 40 acres elsewhere), $7,000 vehicle. Iowa is one of the most debtor-friendly states in the country for homestead protection.

ExemptionAmount

HomesteadUnlimited

Vehicle$7,000

Wildcard$1,000

Full Iowa exemptions guide →

Frequently Asked Questions

Do I qualify for Chapter 7 in Des Moines?

To qualify for Chapter 7 in Iowa, your household income must be below the state median or you must pass the means test calculation. For a single filer in Iowa, the median income threshold is approximately $55,724.

How long does Chapter 7 take in Des Moines?

Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months from filing to discharge in the Southern District of Iowa (S.D. Iowa). The 341 meeting of creditors is usually scheduled about 30 days after filing.

Will I lose my car in Chapter 7?

Iowa allows you to exempt up to $7,000 in vehicle equity. If your car equity is within this limit, you can keep it. If you are still making payments, you can reaffirm the loan and keep the vehicle.

What debts does Chapter 7 eliminate?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts including credit cards, medical bills, personal loans, and old utility bills. It does not eliminate student loans (in most cases), child support, alimony, most tax debts, or criminal fines.

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Des Moines?

The court filing fee is $338. Attorney fees in Des Moines typically range from $1,000 to $2,500. You must also complete two credit counseling courses at approximately $25-$50 total.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

## How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Des Moines, Iowa? (2026 Guide)
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/des-moines/cost.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
Des MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy›Des Moines›Cost

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Des Moines?

A transparent breakdown of every cost involved in filing bankruptcy in Des Moines, Iowa - filing fees, attorney fees, and required courses.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

$313

Ch. 13 Filing Fee

$1K-$2.5K

Ch. 7 Attorney Fee

$3K-$5K

Ch. 13 Attorney Fee

Court Filing Fees

ChapterFiling FeeNotes

Chapter 7$338Due at filing or in up to 4 installments

Chapter 13$313Can be paid through the plan

If your household income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines, you may qualify for a complete fee waiver.

Attorney Fees in Des Moines

Attorney fees vary by complexity, chapter, and attorney. Here are typical ranges for Des Moines:

ChapterTypical RangePayment

Chapter 7$1,000 - $2,500Usually paid in full before filing

Chapter 13$3,000 - $5,000Often paid through the repayment plan

Other Required Costs

RequirementCostWhen

Pre-filing credit counseling$10 - $25Within 180 days before filing

Post-filing debtor education$10 - $25After filing, before discharge

Credit report$0 - $30Annual free report at annualcreditreport.com

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does Chapter 7 cost in Des Moines?

The court filing fee for Chapter 7 is $338. Attorney fees in Des Moines typically range from $1,000 to $2,500. You will also pay $25-$50 total for two required credit counseling courses.

How much does Chapter 13 cost in Des Moines?

The court filing fee for Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees typically range from $3,000 to $5,000, but most attorneys fold their fees into the repayment plan so you do not need the full amount up front.

Can I file bankruptcy for free?

If you cannot afford the filing fee, you can request a fee waiver (for those below 150% of the poverty line) or pay in installments. Iowa legal aid organizations may provide free legal representation if you qualify.

Can I file bankruptcy without a lawyer?

Yes, you can file pro se (without an attorney). However, bankruptcy law is complex and mistakes can result in case dismissal, loss of property, or debts not being discharged. Most experts recommend hiring an attorney.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

## Iowa Bankruptcy Exemptions for Des Moines Residents (Unlimited Homestead)
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/des-moines/exemptions.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
Des MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy›Des Moines›Exemptions

Iowa Bankruptcy Exemptions

When you file bankruptcy in Des Moines, Iowa exemptions determine what property you can keep. Here is a complete breakdown of every exemption available.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Unlimited

Homestead

$7,000

Vehicle

$1,000

Wildcard

Understanding Exemptions

Bankruptcy exemptions are the legal protections that let you keep property when you file. Iowa exemptions: UNLIMITED homestead (must be less than 1/2 acre in a city or 40 acres elsewhere), $7,000 vehicle. Iowa is one of the most debtor-friendly states in the country for homestead protection.

In Chapter 7, exemptions determine what a trustee can sell to pay creditors. In Chapter 13, exemptions affect how much you must pay unsecured creditors through your plan.

Iowa Exemption Amounts

CategoryAmountNotes

HomesteadUnlimitedEquity in primary residence

Motor vehicle$7,000Equity in one vehicle

Wildcard / personal property$1,000Any property of your choosing

Clothing and necessitiesVariesNecessary wearing apparel

Household goodsVariesFurniture, appliances, etc.

Retirement accountsUnlimited401(k), IRA, pensions (ERISA-qualified)

Public benefitsUnlimitedSocial Security, unemployment, veterans

Homestead acreage1/2 acre (city) / 40 acres (rural)Size limit on unlimited homestead

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the homestead exemption in Iowa?

Iowa provides a Unlimited homestead exemption that protects equity in your primary residence. If your home equity is within this amount, you can keep your home when filing Chapter 7 bankruptcy.

Can I keep my car filing bankruptcy in Des Moines?

Iowa allows you to exempt up to $7,000 in vehicle equity. If you owe more than your car is worth or your equity is within the exemption, you keep the car.

Does Iowa allow federal bankruptcy exemptions?

Iowa allows filers to choose between state and federal exemptions, though state exemptions are typically more favorable.

What is the wildcard exemption in Iowa?

Iowa provides a $1,000 wildcard exemption that can be applied to any property of your choosing. This is useful for protecting assets that do not fit into other exemption categories.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

## Bankruptcy FAQ for Des Moines, Iowa - Common Questions Answered
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/des-moines/faq.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
Des MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy›Des Moines›FAQ

Bankruptcy FAQ - Des Moines, Iowa

Answers to the most common questions about filing bankruptcy in Des Moines.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file for bankruptcy in Des Moines?

You file through the Southern District of Iowa (S.D. Iowa) at 123 E. Walnut St, Des Moines, IA 50309. Start by completing credit counseling, gathering financial documents, and consulting with an attorney. You then file a petition, attend a 341 meeting, and complete a debtor education course.

What exemptions apply in Des Moines?

Iowa exemptions: UNLIMITED homestead (must be less than 1/2 acre in a city or 40 acres elsewhere), $7,000 vehicle. Iowa is one of the most debtor-friendly states in the country for homestead protection.

Will bankruptcy stop wage garnishment?

Yes. The moment you file, the automatic stay stops virtually all collection actions including wage garnishments, lawsuits, phone calls, and letters. Learn more about the automatic stay.

How long does bankruptcy stay on my credit report?

Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years. Chapter 13 stays for 7 years. However, many people see their credit scores begin recovering within 1-2 years of discharge.

Can I file bankruptcy without my spouse?

Yes, you can file individually even if you are married. However, your spouse's income may be considered for the means test, and joint debts will not be discharged as to your spouse.

Will I lose my retirement in bankruptcy?

No. Retirement accounts including 401(k)s, IRAs, pensions, and other ERISA-qualified plans are fully protected in bankruptcy with no dollar limit.

Can I file bankruptcy if I am unemployed?

Yes. Being unemployed does not prevent you from filing. In fact, having low or no income makes it more likely you will qualify for Chapter 7.

How often can I file bankruptcy?

For Chapter 7, you must wait 8 years between discharge dates. For Chapter 13, you must wait 2 years. If you received a Chapter 7 discharge, you must wait 4 years before receiving a Chapter 13 discharge. Use the 1328(f) screener to check your eligibility.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener
Means Test Guide

## Filing Bankruptcy in Des Moines, Iowa - Complete Guide | Des Moines Bankruptcy
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/des-moines/

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
Des MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Filing Bankruptcy in Des Moines, Iowa

Your complete guide to bankruptcy in Des Moines. Which chapter to file, Iowa exemptions, courthouse locations, costs, and free local resources.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Unlimited

Homestead Exemption

$7,000

Vehicle Exemption

$338

Ch. 7 Filing Fee

$313

Ch. 13 Filing Fee

Bankruptcy Basics in Des Moines

If you are struggling with debt in Des Moines, bankruptcy may give you a path to a fresh start. Most people file either Chapter 7 (liquidation) or Chapter 13 (repayment plan).

Des Moines residents file through the Southern District of Iowa (S.D. Iowa). The courthouse is located at 123 E. Walnut St, Des Moines, IA 50309.

Iowa exemptions: UNLIMITED homestead (must be less than 1/2 acre in a city or 40 acres elsewhere), $7,000 vehicle. Iowa is one of the most debtor-friendly states in the country for homestead protection.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 in Des Moines

Chapter 7

Eliminates most unsecured debt

Takes 3-4 months

Must pass the means test

May lose non-exempt property

Filing fee: $338

Chapter 13

Repay debts over 3-5 years

Keep all your property

No means test required

Can catch up on mortgage/car payments

Filing fee: $313

Iowa Bankruptcy Exemptions

ExemptionAmountDetails

HomesteadUnlimitedEquity in your primary residence

Vehicle$7,000Equity in one motor vehicle

Wildcard$1,000Applied to any property

Full Des Moines exemptions guide →

Local Resources

Courthouse

Southern District of Iowa (S.D. Iowa)
123 E. Walnut St, Des Moines, IA 50309

Free Legal Aid

Iowa Legal Aid provides free bankruptcy assistance to qualifying low-income residents.

Nearby Communities

These guides cover bankruptcy filing for residents of the greater Des Moines metro area.

West Des Moines→
Ankeny→
Ames→
Urbandale→
Johnston→
Waukee→

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I file for bankruptcy in Des Moines?

You file for bankruptcy in Des Moines through the Southern District of Iowa (S.D. Iowa). The courthouse is located at 123 E. Walnut St, Des Moines, IA 50309. You will need to complete credit counseling, gather financial documents, and file a petition with the court. Most people hire an attorney, though you can file pro se.

What bankruptcy exemptions apply in Des Moines?

Iowa exemptions: UNLIMITED homestead (must be less than 1/2 acre in a city or 40 acres elsewhere), $7,000 vehicle. Iowa is one of the most debtor-friendly states in the country for homestead protection.

How much does bankruptcy cost in Des Moines?

Chapter 7 filing fees are $338 and Chapter 13 filing fees are $313. Attorney fees in Des Moines typically range from $1,000 to $2,500 for Chapter 7 and $3,000 to $5,000 for Chapter 13.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13 in Des Moines?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months but requires passing a means test. Chapter 13 lets you keep property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Your income, assets, and goals determine which is best.

Can I keep my house if I file bankruptcy in Des Moines?

Iowa provides a Unlimited homestead exemption. If your equity is within that limit, you can keep your home in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home while catching up on missed payments.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

## Can I Keep My Car in Bankruptcy? Des Moines, Iowa Guide
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/des-moines/keep-your-car.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
Des MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy›Des Moines›Keep Your Car

Can I Keep My Car Filing Bankruptcy in Des Moines?

Yes, most people keep their vehicles in bankruptcy. Here is how the Iowa vehicle exemption and reaffirmation work for Des Moines residents.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Iowa Vehicle Exemption

Iowa allows you to protect up to $7,000 in vehicle equity. Equity means the difference between your car's value and what you owe on it.

If your car is worth $10,000 and you owe $8,000, your equity is $2,000. If that is within the exemption limit, you keep the car.

Options for Keeping Your Car

Chapter 7

Reaffirmation: Continue payments, keep the car

Redemption: Pay current value in lump sum

Surrender: Give back the car, discharge the debt

Chapter 13

Keep and pay through plan: Include car payments in your plan

Cramdown: Reduce loan to car value (if owned 910+ days)

Cure arrears: Catch up on missed payments

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the vehicle exemption in Iowa?

Iowa allows you to exempt up to $7,000 in vehicle equity. If your car is worth less than you owe, or your equity is within the exemption amount, you can keep it.

What is reaffirmation?

Reaffirmation means you agree to continue paying your car loan as if you never filed bankruptcy. You sign a reaffirmation agreement and keep making payments. This is the most common way to keep a financed car in Chapter 7.

Can I reduce my car payment in bankruptcy?

In Chapter 13, if you have owned your car for more than 910 days, you may be able to 'cram down' the loan to the car's current value, potentially reducing your payments significantly.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener
Means Test Guide

Ch. 7 vs 13

## Can I Keep My House in Bankruptcy? Des Moines, Iowa Guide
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/des-moines/keep-your-house.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
Des MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy›Des Moines›Keep Your House

Can I Keep My House Filing Bankruptcy in Des Moines?

Iowa's Unlimited homestead exemption protects your home equity. Here is how to keep your house in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Iowa Homestead Exemption

Iowa protects up to Unlimited in home equity. If your equity is within this amount, your home is safe in Chapter 7.

Equity = your home's fair market value minus all mortgage balances and liens. If your home is worth $200,000 and you owe $190,000, your equity is $10,000.

Saving Your Home in Chapter 13

Chapter 13 is specifically designed to help homeowners:

Stop foreclosure - the automatic stay halts proceedings immediately

Cure arrears - catch up on missed payments over 3-5 years

Strip junior liens - if your home is underwater, second mortgages may be eliminated

Keep your home - you never give up possession

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the homestead exemption in Iowa?

Iowa provides a Unlimited homestead exemption protecting equity in your primary residence.

Can Chapter 13 save my home from foreclosure?

Yes. Chapter 13 stops foreclosure through the automatic stay and lets you catch up on missed mortgage payments over your 3-5 year plan while maintaining current payments.

What if my home equity exceeds the exemption?

If your equity exceeds the exemption, a Chapter 7 trustee could potentially sell your home. Chapter 13 may be a better option since you keep all property while repaying creditors.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

## Des Moines Means Test: Do You Qualify for Chapter 7 in Iowa?
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/des-moines/means-test.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
Des MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy›Des Moines›Means Test

Des Moines Means Test: Do You Qualify for Chapter 7?

The means test determines whether you can file Chapter 7 bankruptcy in Des Moines. Your income is compared to Iowa's median income for your household size.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

2026 Iowa Median Income Thresholds

Household SizeAnnual Median Income

1 person$55,724

2 persons$72,116

3 persons$85,880

4 persons$104,316

Each additional personAdd approximately $9,900

Step-by-Step Walkthrough

1Calculate your current monthly income (CMI) - Add all income from all sources for the six months before filing. Divide by six, then multiply by 12 to annualize.

2Determine your household size - Count everyone who lives in your household and depends on your income.

3Compare to median - If your annualized CMI is at or below the median for your household size, you qualify for Chapter 7 automatically.

4If above median - Deduct IRS-approved expenses, secured debt payments, and priority debts. If your remaining disposable income over 60 months is less than $9,075, you still qualify.

What If You Do Not Qualify?

File Chapter 13 instead - no means test required

Wait and refile - the test uses the six months before filing, so a drop in income can change the result

Challenge with special circumstances - medical conditions, job loss, or other factors

Re-examine deductions - health insurance, childcare, education, retirement contributions

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the bankruptcy means test?

The means test compares your income to the median income in your state. If your income is below the median for your household size, you automatically qualify for Chapter 7. If above, you must pass a second calculation factoring in allowed expenses.

What is the median income for the means test in Iowa in 2026?

The 2026 Iowa median income thresholds are: 1-person household $55,724, 2-person $72,116, 3-person $85,880, 4-person $104,316. Add approximately $9,900 for each additional person.

What happens if I fail the means test?

You can file Chapter 13 instead (no means test), wait until your income drops, challenge the presumption of abuse with special circumstances, or re-examine your allowable deductions.

Does the means test apply to Chapter 13?

No. However, your income level determines whether your Chapter 13 plan lasts 3 years (below median) or 5 years (above median).

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener
Means Test Guide

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

1328(f) Screener
Ch. 7 vs 13

## Bankruptcy in Ames, Iowa - Filing Guide for Ames Residents
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/des-moines/suburbs/ames.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
Des MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy›Des Moines›Ames

Filing Bankruptcy in Ames, Iowa

A guide to bankruptcy for Ames residents. Covers filing location, Iowa exemptions, costs, and your options under Chapter 7 and Chapter 13.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Bankruptcy Filing for Ames Residents

Ames is served by the Southern District of Iowa (S.D. Iowa). The courthouse is located at 123 E. Walnut St, Des Moines, IA 50309.

Iowa exemptions: UNLIMITED homestead (must be less than 1/2 acre in a city or 40 acres elsewhere), $7,000 vehicle. Iowa is one of the most debtor-friendly states in the country for homestead protection.

Whether you file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13 depends on your income, assets, and financial goals.

Iowa Exemptions

ExemptionAmount

HomesteadUnlimited

Vehicle$7,000

Wildcard$1,000

Full Iowa exemptions guide →

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13

Chapter 7

Eliminates most unsecured debt

Takes 3-4 months

Must pass means test

Filing fee: $338

Chapter 13

3-5 year repayment plan

Keep all your property

No means test required

Filing fee: $313

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Ames residents file for bankruptcy?

Ames residents file through the Southern District of Iowa (S.D. Iowa). The courthouse is at 123 E. Walnut St, Des Moines, IA 50309.

What exemptions apply in Ames?

Ames residents use Iowa state exemptions: Unlimited homestead, $7,000 vehicle, $1,000 wildcard.

How much does bankruptcy cost in Ames?

Costs are the same across Iowa. Chapter 7 filing fee is $338, Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees typically range from $1,000 to $2,500 for Chapter 7 and $3,000 to $5,000 for Chapter 13.

Filing Bankruptcy in Johnston, Iowa

A guide to bankruptcy for Johnston residents. Covers filing location, Iowa exemptions, costs, and your options under Chapter 7 and Chapter 13.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Bankruptcy Filing for Johnston Residents

Johnston is served by the Southern District of Iowa (S.D. Iowa). The courthouse is located at 123 E. Walnut St, Des Moines, IA 50309.

Iowa exemptions: UNLIMITED homestead (must be less than 1/2 acre in a city or 40 acres elsewhere), $7,000 vehicle. Iowa is one of the most debtor-friendly states in the country for homestead protection.

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
Des MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy›Des Moines›Urbandale

Filing Bankruptcy in Urbandale, Iowa

A guide to bankruptcy for Urbandale residents. Covers filing location, Iowa exemptions, costs, and your options under Chapter 7 and Chapter 13.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Iowa exemptions: UNLIMITED homestead (must be less than 1/2 acre in a city or 40 acres elsewhere), $7,000 vehicle. Iowa is one of the most debtor-friendly states in the country for homestead protection.

Open Bankruptcy Project -- openbankruptcyproject.org

Bankruptcy Means Test by State -- bankruptcymeanstest.org

## Bankruptcy in Waukee, Iowa - Filing Guide for Waukee Residents
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/des-moines/suburbs/waukee.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
Des MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy›Des Moines›Waukee

Filing Bankruptcy in Waukee, Iowa

A guide to bankruptcy for Waukee residents. Covers filing location, Iowa exemptions, costs, and your options under Chapter 7 and Chapter 13.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Bankruptcy Filing for Waukee Residents

Waukee is served by the Southern District of Iowa (S.D. Iowa). The courthouse is located at 123 E. Walnut St, Des Moines, IA 50309.

Iowa exemptions: UNLIMITED homestead (must be less than 1/2 acre in a city or 40 acres elsewhere), $7,000 vehicle. Iowa is one of the most debtor-friendly states in the country for homestead protection.

Whether you file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13 depends on your income, assets, and financial goals.

Iowa Exemptions

ExemptionAmount

HomesteadUnlimited

Vehicle$7,000

Wildcard$1,000

Full Iowa exemptions guide →

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13

Chapter 7

Eliminates most unsecured debt

Takes 3-4 months

Must pass means test

Filing fee: $338

Chapter 13

3-5 year repayment plan

Keep all your property

No means test required

Filing fee: $313

Frequently Asked Questions

Where do Waukee residents file for bankruptcy?

Waukee residents file through the Southern District of Iowa (S.D. Iowa). The courthouse is at 123 E. Walnut St, Des Moines, IA 50309.

What exemptions apply in Waukee?

Waukee residents use Iowa state exemptions: Unlimited homestead, $7,000 vehicle, $1,000 wildcard.

How much does bankruptcy cost in Waukee?

Costs are the same across Iowa. Chapter 7 filing fee is $338, Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees typically range from $1,000 to $2,500 for Chapter 7 and $3,000 to $5,000 for Chapter 13.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new bankruptcy discharge.

## Bankruptcy in West Des Moines, Iowa - Filing Guide for West Des Moines Residents
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/des-moines/suburbs/west-des-moines.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
Des MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy›Des Moines›West Des Moines

Filing Bankruptcy in West Des Moines, Iowa

A guide to bankruptcy for West Des Moines residents. Covers filing location, Iowa exemptions, costs, and your options under Chapter 7 and Chapter 13.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Bankruptcy Filing for West Des Moines Residents

West Des Moines is served by the Southern District of Iowa (S.D. Iowa). The courthouse is located at 123 E. Walnut St, Des Moines, IA 50309.

Iowa exemptions: UNLIMITED homestead (must be less than 1/2 acre in a city or 40 acres elsewhere), $7,000 vehicle. Iowa is one of the most debtor-friendly states in the country for homestead protection.

Whether you file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13 depends on your income, assets, and financial goals.

## Bankruptcy Disqualifications in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/disqualifications.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Disqualifications

Bankruptcy Disqualifications in Iowa

Not everyone qualifies for bankruptcy relief. Understanding the disqualifications can help Iowa residents plan their filing strategy.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Filing Bars: When You Cannot File

Prior Discharge Time Bars

Prior DischargeNew Chapter 7New Chapter 13

Chapter 78 years4 years

Chapter 136 years*2 years

*The 6-year bar for Ch.13-to-Ch.7 is waived if you paid 100% of unsecured claims or at least 70% in a good-faith plan.

Use the 1328(f) Screener to calculate your exact eligibility dates.

Grounds for Denial of Discharge

Under 11 U.S.C. section 727(a), the court can deny your Chapter 7 discharge entirely if:

Concealment or transfer of property with intent to defraud within 1 year before filing or during the case (section 727(a)(2))

Destruction or concealment of records relating to your financial condition (section 727(a)(3))

Perjury or false statements in connection with the case (section 727(a)(4))

Failure to explain loss of assets or deficiency of assets (section 727(a)(5))

Failure to complete the post-filing financial management course (section 727(a)(11))

Prior discharge within the applicable time period (section 727(a)(8) and (a)(9))

Credit Counseling Requirement

You cannot file bankruptcy in N.D. Iowa and S.D. Iowa without first completing a credit counseling course from an approved provider within 180 days before filing. There is no exception except:

Exigent circumstances that merit a waiver (very rarely granted)

The approved providers for your district are unable to provide services (almost never happens)

Courses can be completed online, by phone, or in person and typically take about one hour. A second course (debtor education / financial management) is required after filing but before discharge.

180-Day Dismissal Bar

Under 11 U.S.C. section 109(g), you cannot file a new bankruptcy petition if a prior case was dismissed within the past 180 days for:

Willful failure to obey orders of the court

Willful failure to appear before the court

Filing a motion to dismiss after a creditor requested relief from the automatic stay

If you have had a recent dismissal, count the days carefully. Filing before the 180-day period expires can result in immediate dismissal of the new case.

Means Test Disqualification (Chapter 7 Only)

If your income is above the Iowa median and you do not pass the means test expense deductions, you cannot file Chapter 7. Your options are:

File Chapter 13 instead (no income ceiling)

Wait until your income drops (e.g., job change, retirement)

Recalculate after 6 months if a high-income period falls out of the lookback window

Frequently Asked Questions

What disqualifies you from filing bankruptcy in Iowa?

You may be disqualified from bankruptcy if: you did not complete credit counseling, you received a recent prior discharge (8 years for Ch.7-to-Ch.7, 4 years for Ch.7-to-Ch.13, 6 years for Ch.13-to-Ch.7, 2 years for Ch.13-to-Ch.13), you committed fraud, your prior case was dismissed within 180 days for cause, or your income is too high for Chapter 7 and debts exceed Chapter 13 limits.

Can I be denied a bankruptcy discharge?

Yes. Even if you are allowed to file, the court can deny discharge for cause under section 727(a). Grounds include concealing assets, destroying records, committing perjury, failing to explain loss of assets, or failing to complete the financial management course.

What happens if my bankruptcy was recently dismissed in Iowa?

If a case was dismissed within the past 180 days for willful failure to obey a court order, appear before the court, or properly prosecute the case, you cannot file a new case immediately. Additionally, repeat filings within one year can limit or eliminate automatic stay protection.

Check Your Eligibility Now

Our free screener checks prior filing bars and time limits instantly.

Discharge Screener
Means Test Guide

## Divorce and Bankruptcy in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/divorce-and-bankruptcy.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Divorce & Bankruptcy

Divorce and Bankruptcy in Iowa

Divorce and bankruptcy often occur together. Understanding how they interact in Iowa can help you protect your assets, manage shared debts, and plan the best filing strategy.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Filing Order: Bankruptcy Before, During, or After Divorce

1

Before Divorce

Filing a joint bankruptcy before divorce can eliminate shared unsecured debt and simplify the divorce property settlement. You save on filing fees by filing one joint case instead of two individual cases.

2

During Divorce

You can file bankruptcy during divorce proceedings. The automatic stay will pause any debt-related orders but does not affect custody, support, or the divorce itself. Coordination between courts is needed.

3

After Divorce

Filing after divorce means you file individually. Your divorce decree does not override bankruptcy law - a creditor can still come after you for joint debts even if the decree assigns them to your ex-spouse.

What Bankruptcy Cannot Discharge in Divorce

Under 11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(5) and (a)(15), the following divorce-related debts are not dischargeable:

Child support - all arrears and ongoing obligations

Alimony / spousal support - all arrears and ongoing obligations

Property settlement obligations - debts to a former spouse under a divorce decree, separation agreement, or property settlement (non-dischargeable in Chapter 7; potentially dischargeable in Chapter 13)

Hold-harmless agreements - if you agreed to pay a joint debt in the divorce, that obligation survives Chapter 7

Key difference: In Chapter 13, property division debts (section 523(a)(15)) may be dischargeable, while domestic support obligations (section 523(a)(5)) are never dischargeable.

Joint Debts and the Co-Debtor Stay

When one spouse files Chapter 13, the co-debtor stay under 11 U.S.C. section 1301 temporarily protects the non-filing spouse from creditors trying to collect on joint consumer debts. This protection does not exist in Chapter 7.

This is particularly important in Iowa where both spouses may be on the mortgage, car loan, or credit cards. The co-debtor stay lasts as long as the Chapter 13 plan is active.

Iowa Property Considerations

Homestead exemption: unlimited - the bankruptcy and divorce courts both consider home equity

Vehicle exemption: $7,000

See the full Iowa exemptions guide for details.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I file bankruptcy during a divorce in Iowa?

Yes. You can file bankruptcy before, during, or after a divorce in Iowa. The timing affects how debts and property are divided. Filing before the divorce can simplify property division by eliminating joint unsecured debt.

Does bankruptcy discharge alimony or child support?

No. Domestic support obligations including alimony and child support are not dischargeable in bankruptcy under any chapter. They are priority debts that must be paid in full.

What happens to joint debts in bankruptcy?

If one spouse files bankruptcy and discharges a joint debt, the other spouse remains fully liable. The creditor can pursue the non-filing spouse for the entire balance. A Chapter 13 co-debtor stay can temporarily protect the non-filing spouse during the repayment plan.

Planning Your Next Step

If you are considering bankruptcy alongside divorce, check your discharge eligibility first.

Discharge Screener
Chapter 7 Guide

## Emergency Bankruptcy Filing in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/emergency-bankruptcy.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Emergency Filing

Emergency Bankruptcy Filing in Iowa

When foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment is days or even hours away, Iowa residents can file an emergency bankruptcy petition to trigger the automatic stay immediately.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Is an Emergency Bankruptcy Filing?

An emergency bankruptcy filing - sometimes called a bare-bones or skeleton filing - is when you file only the minimum required documents to open a case and activate the automatic stay. You file the rest within 14 days.

Minimum Documents Required

Voluntary Petition - the two-page form that officially opens the case

Creditor Matrix - a list of all known creditors with names and addresses

Credit Counseling Certificate - proof of the required pre-filing credit counseling course (can be completed online in about an hour)

When to Consider Emergency Filing in Iowa

Emergency Filing Makes Sense When

A foreclosure sale is scheduled within days

Your employer received a garnishment order that starts next pay period

A creditor is actively repossessing your vehicle

A bank account levy is about to clear

A lawsuit judgment is about to be enforced

Utility shutoff is imminent

Better to File a Complete Petition When

You have several weeks before any deadline

You want to avoid the risk of missing the 14-day supplement deadline

Your financial situation is complex and needs careful preparation

You need time to gather all financial documents

The 14-Day Deadline

After an emergency filing in N.D. Iowa and S.D. Iowa, you have 14 days to file all remaining documents:

Schedules A/B through J (assets, liabilities, income, expenses)

Statement of Financial Affairs

Means Test calculation (Form 122A or 122C)

Tax returns for the most recent year

Pay stubs for the 60 days prior to filing

If you miss the 14-day deadline and do not receive an extension, the court will dismiss your case. This can limit the automatic stay protection in any future filing within one year.

Iowa Exemption Quick Reference

When you complete your schedules, you will need to claim exemptions to protect your property:

Homestead: unlimited

Vehicle: $7,000

For a complete list, see the Iowa exemptions page.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I file an emergency bankruptcy in Iowa?

Yes. You can file an emergency (bare-bones) bankruptcy petition in Iowa to get the automatic stay protection immediately. You file only the two-page voluntary petition, a creditor list, and proof of credit counseling. You then have 14 days to file the remaining schedules and documents.

How fast can the automatic stay take effect in Iowa?

The automatic stay takes effect the instant your petition is filed with the N.D. Iowa and S.D. Iowa bankruptcy court. If you file electronically during court hours, it can happen within minutes. Emergency filings are designed for situations where a foreclosure sale, wage garnishment, or repossession is imminent.

What happens if I miss the 14-day deadline?

If you fail to file the remaining schedules and documents within 14 days, the court may dismiss your case. You can request a one-time extension, but it is not guaranteed. A dismissal could limit your ability to refile and get the automatic stay again.

Check Your Filing Eligibility

Before filing - emergency or otherwise - make sure you are eligible for a bankruptcy discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

## Bankruptcy and Employment in Iowa - Discrimination, Hiring, Your Rights
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/employment.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Bankruptcy and Employment in Iowa

Bankruptcy and Employment in Iowa

Federal law protects you from employment discrimination based on bankruptcy. Government employers have strict prohibitions. Private employer protections are narrower but still significant. Here is what Iowa workers need to know.

Quick answer: Under 11 U.S.C. section 525(a), government employers cannot fire, refuse to hire, or discriminate against you based on bankruptcy. Under section 525(b), private employers cannot fire you solely for filing. However, most courts hold that private employers CAN consider bankruptcy in hiring decisions. Some states add extra protections.

Federal Protections: Section 525

Section 525(a): Government Employers

Cannot terminate based on bankruptcy

Cannot refuse to hire based on bankruptcy

Cannot discriminate in any way based on bankruptcy

Covers federal, state, and local government

Also covers government-issued licenses and permits

Broad and well-established protection

Section 525(b): Private Employers

Cannot terminate solely because of bankruptcy

Hiring: Most courts say section 525(b) does NOT prohibit refusing to hire

The word "hire" was deliberately omitted from 525(b)

Cannot discriminate against current employees

Narrower than government employer protections

Some states fill the gap with credit check restrictions

Iowa-Specific Employment Protections

11 U.S.C. section 525(a) protects government employees. Iowa Code Chapter 216 (Iowa Civil Rights Act) prohibits employment discrimination on certain grounds but does not specifically list bankruptcy. The 8th Circuit (covering Iowa) has addressed section 525(b) regarding private employers. Iowa's strong agricultural and manufacturing sectors have many positions where bankruptcy is unlikely to affect employment.

Credit Checks in Employment

The Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) governs employer access to credit reports:

Written consent required: An employer must get your written permission before pulling your credit report

You can say no: But declining may affect your candidacy for positions requiring financial trust

Adverse action notice: If an employer takes adverse action based on your credit report, they must notify you and provide a copy of the report

Employment versions: Employers receive a modified credit report that does not include your credit score - they see payment history, accounts, and public records (including bankruptcy)

Several states and cities restrict employer use of credit checks. Check whether Iowa has additional restrictions.

Jobs Most Affected by Bankruptcy

While most jobs are unaffected by bankruptcy, certain positions may be more sensitive:

Financial industry: Banks, investment firms, insurance companies may consider bankruptcy for positions handling money

Government with clearance: Security clearance positions are affected but not disqualified (see our security clearance page)

Law enforcement: Some agencies consider financial history

Corporate officers: C-suite positions may have different standards

Bonded positions: Jobs requiring a surety bond may be affected if the bonding company considers bankruptcy

For the vast majority of jobs - retail, healthcare, education, technology, manufacturing, service industry - bankruptcy has no effect on employment.

Professional Licenses in Iowa

Under section 525(a), Iowa licensing authorities cannot deny, revoke, or refuse to renew professional licenses solely because of bankruptcy. This includes:

Real estate licenses

Medical and nursing licenses

Legal licenses (bar admission)

Teaching certificates

Trade licenses (plumbing, electrical, etc.)

Insurance agent licenses

If a licensing board requires a "financial responsibility" showing, the underlying financial condition (not the bankruptcy filing itself) is what matters. Successfully completing bankruptcy actually demonstrates financial responsibility.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I be fired for filing bankruptcy?

Government employers: absolutely not. Private employers: cannot fire you solely for bankruptcy under section 525(b). Using it as one factor among many is a gray area.

Can an employer refuse to hire me?

Government employers cannot. Most courts hold private employers can consider bankruptcy in hiring. Some states restrict credit check use in hiring, providing indirect protection.

Can my employer check my credit?

Only with your written consent under the FCRA. You can decline, but it may affect your candidacy for financial positions.

Does bankruptcy affect professional licenses?

Government licensing authorities cannot deny or revoke licenses based on bankruptcy under section 525(a). Financial responsibility requirements may still apply.

Should I tell my employer I filed?

Generally not required unless you are in a position with financial disclosure requirements. If wage garnishment stops because of bankruptcy, your employer may become aware. Focus on your job performance.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## Can I Keep My Jewelry in Iowa Bankruptcy?
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/engagement-ring.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Jewelry Exemptions

Can I Keep My Jewelry in Iowa Bankruptcy?

Your engagement ring, wedding band, and other jewelry may be protected by Iowa exemptions. Here is what the law says and how values are determined.

Quick answer: Iowa does not have a specific jewelry exemption. Instead, jewelry falls under Iowa's $7,000 aggregate personal property exemption (Iowa Code 627.6(5)), which covers clothing, household goods, and personal items. This is shared across all personal property, so high-value jewelry can consume most of this exemption. Iowa does not allow federal exemptions.

Iowa Jewelry Exemption Details

Iowa does not have a specific jewelry exemption. Instead, jewelry falls under Iowa's $7,000 aggregate personal property exemption (Iowa Code 627.6(5)), which covers clothing, household goods, and personal items. This is shared across all personal property, so high-value jewelry can consume most of this exemption. Iowa does not allow federal exemptions.

The exemption amount is based on the jewelry's replacement value - what a comparable item would cost at retail, not what a pawnshop or jeweler would pay you. In practice, most everyday jewelry (wedding rings, watches, earrings) falls well within the exemption limits.

How Jewelry Is Valued

Replacement value standard: The Bankruptcy Code uses replacement value - the price a retail buyer would pay for a comparable item in similar condition.

Not purchase price: What you paid is irrelevant. A ring purchased for $5,000 may have a replacement value of $2,000-$3,000.

Not resale value: Pawn shop or "we buy gold" prices are lower than replacement value and are not the correct standard.

Professional appraisal: If you have valuable jewelry, get a written appraisal from a certified appraiser. The appraiser should provide a replacement value for insurance purposes.

Trustee assessment: The Chapter 7 trustee may request an appraisal if they believe jewelry values are understated. Most trustees do not pursue jewelry worth less than $1,000-$2,000 above exemption limits.

Practical Reality: Trustees and Jewelry

In practice, bankruptcy trustees rarely pursue jewelry unless its value significantly exceeds the exemption amount. Here is why:

Transaction costs: Selling jewelry at auction or through consignment involves fees that reduce the net recovery for creditors.

Low recovery rates: Used jewelry typically sells for 20-40% of retail replacement value at liquidation.

De minimis recovery: If the net benefit to creditors is less than a few hundred dollars, most trustees determine the sale is not worth the effort.

Emotional factor: Trustees understand that taking wedding rings creates bad optics and may exercise discretion.

Important: Always disclose all jewelry honestly on your schedules. Hiding assets is fraud. If your jewelry is within the exemption, the trustee will not take it. If it is close to the limit, the trustee will likely not pursue it.

Protecting Jewelry That Exceeds the Exemption

If your jewelry exceeds Iowa's exemption, you may have options:

Wildcard exemption: If Iowa offers a wildcard (general property) exemption, you can stack it on top of the jewelry exemption to protect additional value.

Chapter 13: In Chapter 13, you keep all your property. Instead of surrendering jewelry, you pay creditors the equivalent value through your plan. This is often the better option for people with valuable jewelry.

Negotiate with trustee: You may be able to pay the trustee the non-exempt value in cash to keep the jewelry - called "buying back" the asset from the estate.

Federal exemptions: If Iowa allows the federal exemptions (11 U.S.C. 522(d)), you may get a higher jewelry exemption ($1,875) plus a significant wildcard ($1,475 + up to $13,950 of unused homestead).

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my engagement ring in Iowa bankruptcy?

It depends on the ring's value and Iowa's exemptions. $7,000 aggregate personal property (Iowa Code 627.6(5)) - includes jewelry. If your ring's value is below the exemption limit, you can keep it. If it exceeds the limit, you may need to use additional exemptions (like a wildcard) to protect it.

What is the jewelry exemption in Iowa?

$7,000 aggregate personal property (Iowa Code 627.6(5)) - includes jewelry. This applies to all jewelry including engagement rings, wedding bands, watches, and other personal adornment. Values are based on resale/liquidation value, not what you paid.

How is jewelry valued in bankruptcy?

Jewelry is valued at replacement value - what it would cost to buy a comparable item at retail, not what a jeweler would pay you. In practice, bankruptcy trustees often accept values significantly below retail. A professional appraisal can help establish the value.

What if my jewelry is worth more than the exemption?

If your jewelry exceeds the exemption limit, you may be able to protect the excess using a wildcard exemption (if available in your state). Otherwise, the trustee may seek to sell the jewelry and return the exempt portion to you. In practice, trustees rarely pursue jewelry unless the value significantly exceeds exemptions.

Do I have to disclose all jewelry in bankruptcy?

Yes. All assets must be disclosed on Schedule A/B, including jewelry. Failure to disclose assets is bankruptcy fraud and can result in denial of discharge, fines, or criminal prosecution. Always disclose - even if you believe the item is fully exempt.

What about inherited jewelry or family heirlooms?

Inherited jewelry and heirlooms are valued the same way as any other jewelry - replacement value. Sentimental value does not factor into the financial analysis. However, trustees rarely pursue items with modest resale value, even if sentimental value is high.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Garnishment
FAQ

An Open Bankruptcy Project resource

Data sourced from the Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database (2008--2024).

This site provides general information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for your specific situation.

© 2026 Open Bankruptcy Project. GitHub

You May Also Find Helpful

1099-C Cancellation of Debt Tax Guide -- 1099cdebt.com

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 Comparison -- chapter7vs13.org

Open Bankruptcy Project -- openbankruptcyproject.org

---

## Can Bankruptcy Stop an Eviction in Iowa?
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/eviction.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Eviction

Can Bankruptcy Stop an Eviction in Iowa?

Timing is everything. Bankruptcy can halt an eviction if you file before a judgment for possession is entered. After a judgment, the protection is limited.

Quick answer: If no eviction judgment has been entered, filing bankruptcy immediately stops the eviction through the automatic stay. If a judgment already exists, section 362(b)(22) allows the landlord to proceed. File before the judgment to maximize protection.

Before vs. After Eviction Judgment

Before Judgment

Automatic stay stops eviction

Landlord cannot proceed in state court

Chapter 13 can cure back rent over 3-5 years

You must pay post-petition rent going forward

Emergency filing possible in hours

After Judgment

Section 362(b)(22) limits protection

Landlord can proceed with eviction

Exception: if state law allows cure after judgment, you may file certification under 362(l)

Most Iowa courts apply this strictly

Chapter 7 will discharge the rent debt but not stop eviction

Iowa Eviction Timeline

Governing law: Iowa Code Chapter 648

Iowa requires a 3-day notice for nonpayment. Forcible entry and detainer cases are heard in small claims court and typically resolved within 1-3 weeks.

Understanding this timeline is critical for deciding when to file bankruptcy. Once the landlord obtains a judgment for possession, your bankruptcy protections are significantly reduced.

Key window: File bankruptcy BEFORE the judge enters a judgment for possession. Once a judgment is entered, section 362(b)(22) may allow the eviction to continue despite your filing.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Evictions

Chapter 7

Discharges back rent (pre-petition)

Automatic stay is temporary (case lasts 3-4 months)

Does not allow you to cure arrears

Best if you plan to move anyway

Eliminates personal liability for lease balance

Chapter 13

Allows curing back rent through plan

Automatic stay lasts 3-5 years (plan duration)

You must assume the lease and stay current

Best if you want to keep your housing

Plan payments include arrears catch-up

Emergency Filing to Stop Eviction

If eviction is imminent, an emergency bankruptcy filing can be done quickly:

Bare-bones petition: File just the petition, Statement of Social Security Number, and credit counseling certificate. You have 14 days to file the remaining schedules.

Immediate protection: The automatic stay takes effect the moment the petition is filed with the bankruptcy court - not when the landlord is notified.

Proof of filing: Get a copy of the filed petition with the case number and give it to your landlord and the state court handling the eviction.

Filing fee: Chapter 7 costs $338 and Chapter 13 costs $313. Fee waivers are available for those below 150% of the poverty line.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can bankruptcy stop an eviction in Iowa?

It depends on timing. If no eviction judgment has been entered, filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay, which immediately halts eviction proceedings. If a judgment for possession already exists, section 362(b)(22) limits the stay's protection, and the landlord may be able to proceed.

What is section 362(b)(22)?

Section 362(b)(22) of the Bankruptcy Code is an exception to the automatic stay. If a landlord obtained a judgment for possession before you filed bankruptcy, the stay does not prevent the landlord from continuing the eviction. This was added by the 2005 BAPCPA amendments to prevent abuse of bankruptcy to delay evictions.

What if I file bankruptcy before the eviction judgment in Iowa?

If no judgment for possession has been entered, the automatic stay stops the eviction cold. The landlord cannot proceed with the eviction, change locks, or remove your property. However, you must still pay post-petition rent - the stay does not excuse future rent payments.

Can I cure a lease default in Chapter 13?

In Chapter 13, you can cure lease defaults (back rent) through your repayment plan. You assume the lease and pay arrears over the plan period (3-5 years). This only works if your landlord has not yet obtained a judgment for possession.

Does filing bankruptcy erase back rent?

Chapter 7 can discharge back rent owed before filing, but it does not stop an eviction if a judgment already exists. Chapter 13 lets you cure arrears while keeping your home. Post-petition rent must always be paid regardless of the chapter filed.

How quickly can I file bankruptcy to stop an eviction in Iowa?

Emergency (bare-bones) bankruptcy filings can be done in hours. You file the petition with minimal schedules and have 14 days to complete the rest. The automatic stay takes effect the moment the petition is filed with the court.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Garnishment

## Iowa Bankruptcy Exemptions - What You Can Keep
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/exemptions.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Exemptions

Iowa Bankruptcy Exemptions

Exemptions determine what property you get to keep when you file bankruptcy. Understanding Iowa's exemptions is critical - they can mean the difference between keeping and losing your home, car, and savings.

What Are Exemptions?

Bankruptcy exemptions are laws that protect certain property from being taken to pay your creditors. In Chapter 7, non-exempt property can be sold by the trustee. In Chapter 13, the value of non-exempt property affects how much you must pay through your plan.

Iowa requires filers to use state exemptions - you cannot choose the federal exemption system.

Iowa State Exemptions - Full List

Homestead - Unlimited

Iowa has an unlimited homestead exemption, protecting all equity in your primary residence regardless of value, as long as the property does not exceed 1/2 acre in a city or 40 acres in rural areas.

Iowa Code 561.2

Motor Vehicle - Up to $7,000 (one motor vehicle)

Protects equity in one motor vehicle up to the exemption limit. If you owe more than the car is worth (you are "underwater"), there is no equity to protect and the exemption is not needed.

Other Iowa Exemptions

PropertyAmount

Household goods, furnishings$7,000 total

Clothing$1,000

Tools of trade$10,000

Jewelry$2,000

Wildcard$1,000

Retirement accounts (IRA, 401k, pension)Fully exempt

Social Security benefitsFully exempt

Workers' compensationFully exempt

Public assistance benefitsFully exempt

Special Notes

Iowa requires filers to use state exemptions - federal exemptions are not available. The unlimited homestead exemption is one of the most protective in the nation.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the Iowa homestead exemption?

Iowa has an unlimited homestead exemption, protecting all equity in your primary residence regardless of value, as long as the property does not exceed 1/2 acre in a city or 40 acres in rural areas.

How much vehicle equity can I protect in Iowa?

Iowa's motor vehicle exemption is Up to $7,000 (one motor vehicle). If your car equity is within that limit, you keep it in Chapter 7.

Does Iowa allow federal exemptions?

No. Iowa requires filers to use state exemptions only.
