## Iowa Bankruptcy FAQ - 15 Common Questions Answered
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/faq.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Faq

Iowa Bankruptcy FAQ

Answers to the 15 most common questions from Iowa residents considering bankruptcy.

Cost and Eligibility

How much does it cost to file bankruptcy in Iowa?

Chapter 7 filing fee is $338 and Chapter 13 is $313. Attorney fees typically range from $1,000 to $1,800 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $4,000 for Chapter 13. Credit counseling costs about $25-50 total. Chapter 13 fees can be paid through your repayment plan. Fee waivers available for low-income Chapter 7 filers.

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy in Iowa?

Iowa's homestead exemption protects up to Unlimited of equity in your home. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home and catch up on missed mortgage payments through your plan. Most homeowners keep their homes in bankruptcy.

Can I keep my car in bankruptcy in Iowa?

Iowa protects Up to $7,000 (one motor vehicle) in vehicle equity. If your car equity is within the exemption limit, you keep it in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you can keep your car and catch up on payments through your plan.

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7 takes 3-4 months from filing to discharge. Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan. The 341 meeting of creditors happens about 30 days after filing.

Will bankruptcy stop wage garnishment?

Yes. The automatic stay takes effect the moment your petition is filed and immediately stops wage garnishments, bank levies, lawsuits, foreclosures, and repossessions.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 is faster (3-4 months) and eliminates most unsecured debt, but requires passing a means test. Chapter 13 lets you keep all property and catch up on secured debt arrears, but requires 3-5 years of payments. Your income, assets, and goals determine the best choice.

Where do I file for bankruptcy in Iowa?

Iowa has 2 districts: N.D. Iowa, S.D. Iowa. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

Will bankruptcy affect my credit score?

Yes. Chapter 7 stays on your credit report for 10 years and Chapter 13 for 7 years. However, many filers see credit scores start recovering within 1-2 years as debt-to-income ratios improve.

Can I file bankruptcy on medical bills?

Yes. Medical bills are unsecured debts that are fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13. Medical debt is one of the most common reasons people file bankruptcy.

Will bankruptcy eliminate my student loans?

Generally no. Student loans are not dischargeable except in cases of undue hardship, which requires a separate adversary proceeding and a difficult legal standard.

What is the means test?

The means test determines whether you qualify for Chapter 7. It compares your household income to the Iowa median. If below, you qualify automatically. If above, a detailed expense calculation determines eligibility.

Can creditors still call me after I file?

No. The automatic stay prohibits all collection activity, including phone calls, letters, and emails. Creditors who violate the stay can be held in contempt and ordered to pay damages.

What is the 341 meeting of creditors?

A brief hearing about 30 days after filing where the bankruptcy trustee asks you questions under oath about your finances. Bring photo ID and Social Security proof. Most meetings last 5-10 minutes.

Can I file bankruptcy more than once?

Yes, but there are time limits. After a Chapter 7 discharge, you must wait 8 years for another Chapter 7 or 4 years for Chapter 13. After a Chapter 13 discharge, wait 6 years for Chapter 7 or 2 years for another Chapter 13.

What is the automatic stay?

The automatic stay is a court order that takes effect immediately when you file bankruptcy. It stops lawsuits, garnishments, foreclosures, repossessions, and collection calls.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility for a new discharge.

Free Discharge Screener

## How to Find Bankruptcy Filings in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/find-bankruptcy-filing.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Find Filings

How to Find Bankruptcy Filings in Iowa

Bankruptcy filings are public records. Here is how to search them in Iowa - from free options to the official PACER system.

Quick answer: Search PACER at pacer.uscourts.gov ($0.10/page, $3 cap per doc). For free access, use CourtListener.com (RECAP archive) or call the Northern and Southern Districts of Iowa clerk's office. The Iowa bankruptcy court website is https://www.ianb.uscourts.gov.

Option 1: PACER (Official System)

PACER (Public Access to Court Electronic Records) is the federal court system's official electronic records platform.

Website: pacer.uscourts.gov

Cost: $0.10 per page, capped at $3.00 per document. Quarterly charges under $30 are waived.

Search by: Name, case number, Social Security number (last 4), date range

Iowa court: https://ecf.ianb.uscourts.gov

Registration: Free to create an account. Takes about 5 minutes.

1

Register

Create a free PACER account at pacer.uscourts.gov

2

Search

Use the national case locator or go directly to the Iowa bankruptcy court

3

View

View docket entries and download documents ($0.10/page)

Option 2: RECAP / CourtListener (Free)

RECAP is a free browser extension that contributes PACER documents to a public archive. CourtListener hosts this archive and makes it searchable.

Website: courtlistener.com

Cost: Completely free

Coverage: Millions of documents, growing daily. Not every document is available, but many are.

Browser extension: Install RECAP for Chrome or Firefox. When you view PACER documents, RECAP automatically saves a copy to the public archive.

Option 3: Court Clerk's Office

You can contact the Northern and Southern Districts of Iowa bankruptcy court clerk's office directly:

Phone: Call the clerk and request a case search by name or case number. Basic case information (filing date, chapter, status) is typically provided for free.

In person: Visit the courthouse and use the public access terminals. No PACER account needed.

Website: https://www.ianb.uscourts.gov may have a free case search or CM/ECF lookup

What Information Is Available

Docket sheet: Every document filed in the case, with dates and descriptions

Petition: Debtor's name, address, chapter filed, attorney

Schedules: Assets, liabilities, income, expenses (with sensitive info redacted)

Claims register: Every creditor claim filed in the case

Orders: Court orders, discharge, dismissal

Meeting of creditors (341): Date and outcome

Sensitive information is redacted: full Social Security numbers, complete financial account numbers, and the names of minor children are not visible in public filings.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I find bankruptcy filings in Iowa?

Bankruptcy filings in Iowa are public records available through PACER (Public Access to Court Electronic Records) at pacer.uscourts.gov. You can search by name, case number, or Social Security number. The Northern and Southern Districts of Iowa has its electronic filing system at https://ecf.ianb.uscourts.gov.

Is PACER free?

PACER charges $0.10 per page viewed, capped at $3.00 per document. Quarterly fees under $30 are waived. You can also use RECAP (a free browser extension) to access previously downloaded documents at no cost via CourtListener.com.

Can I search bankruptcy filings for free?

Yes. CourtListener.com (via the RECAP archive) has millions of free bankruptcy documents. Many court websites also offer free case information lookups. The bankruptcy court clerk's office will search records for free over the phone for basic information.

Which courts handle bankruptcy in Iowa?

Bankruptcy cases in Iowa are filed in the Northern and Southern Districts of Iowa. The court website is https://www.ianb.uscourts.gov. PACER electronic filing is at https://ecf.ianb.uscourts.gov.

How long are bankruptcy records available?

Bankruptcy records on PACER are generally available for the life of the case plus several years. Older cases may be archived but still accessible. Bankruptcy stays on your credit report for 7 years (Chapter 13) or 10 years (Chapter 7) from the filing date.

Can I see someone else's bankruptcy filing?

Yes. Bankruptcy filings are public records. Anyone can search PACER or visit the court clerk to view case documents. Some sensitive information (Social Security numbers, financial account numbers) is redacted from public filings.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Garnishment

## Foreclosure and Bankruptcy in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/foreclosure.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesCedar RapidsChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Foreclosure

Foreclosure and Bankruptcy in Iowa

Facing foreclosure in Iowa? Bankruptcy can stop the process immediately and give you time to save your home. Here is how non-judicial foreclosure works in Iowa and how bankruptcy provides relief.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

How Foreclosure Works in Iowa

Iowa allows non-judicial foreclosure, but most foreclosures proceed judicially. Iowa provides a generous 12-month right of redemption after a foreclosure sale, giving homeowners significant time to reclaim their property.

Timeline: Judicial foreclosure in Iowa typically takes 5-7 months, but the 12-month redemption period adds substantial additional time.

How Bankruptcy Stops Foreclosure

The automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. section 362 immediately halts all foreclosure activity the moment you file bankruptcy. This includes:

Foreclosure lawsuits

Scheduled foreclosure sales

Sheriff's sales

Trustee's sales

Deficiency judgment actions

The stay applies even if the lender does not know about your filing yet. Once the lender is notified, any foreclosure action taken after filing is void.

Chapter 13: The Foreclosure Defense Tool

How Chapter 13 Saves Your Home

Stops foreclosure through the automatic stay

Catch up on missed payments over 3-5 years

Continue making regular monthly payments going forward

May strip off underwater second mortgages

Eliminate other debts to free up cash for mortgage

Iowa Homestead Protection

Homestead exemption: Unlimited

Protects your home equity in bankruptcy

Applies in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13

Iowa offers one of the most generous homestead exemptions in the country - it is unlimited in value. Your home is fully protected as long as it is on half an acre or less in a city or 40 acres or less in rural areas.

Timing Is Critical

The sooner you act, the more options you have:

Early stage (first missed payments): Contact your lender about forbearance or loan modification before considering bankruptcy

Pre-foreclosure notice received: Consult with an attorney or legal aid about bankruptcy options

Foreclosure filed: Filing bankruptcy still stops the process, but you have less time

Sale date scheduled: Emergency bankruptcy filing can stop the sale, but must be filed before the sale occurs

After the sale: Options are very limited - in some states, a redemption period may still apply

Legal assistance: Iowa Legal Aid (iowalegalaid.org) may provide free help with foreclosure defense and bankruptcy.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can bankruptcy stop foreclosure in Iowa?

Yes. Filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay, which immediately halts all foreclosure proceedings in Iowa. Chapter 13 is especially powerful because it allows you to catch up on missed mortgage payments over 3-5 years while keeping your home.

How does foreclosure work in Iowa?

Iowa uses non-judicial foreclosure. Iowa allows non-judicial foreclosure, but most foreclosures proceed judicially. Iowa provides a generous 12-month right of redemption after a foreclosure sale, giving homeowners significant time to reclaim their property.

How quickly do I need to file to stop a foreclosure sale?

The automatic stay takes effect the instant your bankruptcy petition is filed. Even if a foreclosure sale is scheduled for tomorrow, filing today stops it. However, filing as early as possible gives you more options. Judicial foreclosure in Iowa typically takes 5-7 months, but the 12-month redemption period adds substantial additional time.

Can I save my home in Chapter 7?

Chapter 7 can delay foreclosure temporarily through the automatic stay, but it does not provide a long-term mechanism to catch up on missed payments. Iowa's Unlimited homestead exemption protects your equity, but you must be current on mortgage payments to keep the home. Chapter 13 is usually better for saving a home from foreclosure.

What is the redemption period in Iowa?

Iowa allows non-judicial foreclosure, but most foreclosures proceed judicially. Iowa provides a generous 12-month right of redemption after a foreclosure sale, giving homeowners significant time to reclaim their property.

Facing Foreclosure?

Time is critical. Check your options and learn how bankruptcy can help save your home.

Keep Your House Guide

## Iowa Bankruptcy Glossary
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/glossary.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Glossary

Iowa Bankruptcy Glossary

Key bankruptcy terms and definitions for Iowa filers, from automatic stay to wildcard exemption.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Bankruptcy Terms A-Z

341 Meeting (Meeting of Creditors)
A required hearing where the bankruptcy trustee (and any creditors who choose to attend) asks you questions under oath about your finances, assets, and petition. Held about 30 days after filing in N.D. Iowa and S.D. Iowa.

Adversary Proceeding
A lawsuit within a bankruptcy case, often filed to challenge the dischargeability of a specific debt or to recover fraudulent transfers.

Automatic Stay
A court order that takes effect immediately upon filing, stopping most collection actions, lawsuits, foreclosures, repossessions, and garnishments. See 11 U.S.C. section 362.

Chapter 7
Liquidation bankruptcy that eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months. A trustee may sell non-exempt assets to pay creditors. Requires passing the means test.

Chapter 13
Reorganization bankruptcy for individuals with regular income. Debtor proposes a 3-5 year repayment plan to catch up on secured debts and pay a portion of unsecured debts.

Cramdown
Reducing the amount owed on a secured debt to the current value of the collateral. Available in Chapter 13 for vehicles purchased more than 910 days before filing and other secured debts.

Credit Counseling
A required course from an approved provider that must be completed within 180 days before filing bankruptcy. A second course (debtor education) is required before discharge.

Creditor
Any person or entity to whom you owe money. Includes secured creditors (backed by collateral), unsecured creditors (no collateral), and priority creditors (taxes, support obligations).

Discharge
A court order that permanently eliminates your personal liability for dischargeable debts. The discharge injunction (section 524) prevents creditors from collecting on discharged debts forever.

Disposable Income
Income remaining after subtracting allowed expenses. In Chapter 13, disposable income determines your plan payment amount.

Exemptions
Laws that protect specific property from being taken by the trustee in Chapter 7 or reduce what you pay in Chapter 13. Iowa homestead exemption: unlimited; vehicle: $7,000.

Fraudulent Transfer
A transfer of property made to hinder, delay, or defraud creditors. The trustee can recover such transfers made within 2 years before filing (or longer under state law).

Lien
A legal claim on property as security for a debt. Common types include mortgage liens, auto liens, tax liens, and judgment liens.

Lien Stripping
Removing a wholly unsecured junior mortgage in Chapter 13. If the home value is less than the first mortgage balance, the second mortgage can be treated as unsecured debt.

Means Test
A formula under section 707(b) that determines Chapter 7 eligibility based on income and expenses. Compares your income to Iowa median income for your household size.

Motion for Relief from Stay
A request by a creditor asking the court to lift the automatic stay, usually to allow foreclosure or repossession to proceed.

Non-Dischargeable Debt
Debts that survive bankruptcy, including student loans (absent undue hardship), most taxes, child support, alimony, debts from fraud, and DUI-related debts.

Petition
The official document filed with the bankruptcy court to start a case. Includes your personal information, chapter selected, and basic financial data.

Plan (Chapter 13)
A proposal showing how you will repay creditors over 3-5 years. Must pay priority debts in full, cure arrears on secured debts, and pay unsecured creditors at least what they would receive in Chapter 7.

Priority Debt
Debts that must be paid in full in Chapter 13 and cannot be discharged in Chapter 7. Includes recent taxes, child support, alimony, and certain government fines.

Proof of Claim
A form filed by a creditor establishing the amount and type of debt owed. Required for the creditor to receive payment in the case.

Reaffirmation Agreement
A voluntary agreement to remain liable for a debt after bankruptcy, typically used to keep a car or other secured property. Must be filed with the court.

Schedules
Detailed forms listing all assets, debts, income, expenses, and recent financial transactions. Required within 14 days of filing.

Secured Debt
A debt backed by collateral (like a mortgage or car loan). The creditor can repossess the collateral if the debt is not paid.

Trustee
A court-appointed official who administers your bankruptcy case. Chapter 7 trustees liquidate non-exempt assets. Chapter 13 trustees collect and distribute plan payments.

Unsecured Debt
A debt not backed by collateral, such as credit cards, medical bills, and personal loans. Generally dischargeable in bankruptcy.

Wildcard Exemption
An exemption that can be applied to any property type. Check Iowa exemption statutes for availability.

Frequently Asked Questions

What is the difference between Chapter 7 and Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt in 3-4 months through liquidation of non-exempt assets. Chapter 13 is a 3-5 year repayment plan for people with regular income. Chapter 7 has income limits (means test); Chapter 13 has debt limits ($2,750,000).

What does discharge mean in bankruptcy?

A discharge is a permanent court order that eliminates your personal liability for specified debts. After discharge, creditors can never collect on those debts again. Not all debts are dischargeable - student loans, most taxes, child support, and alimony typically survive.

Learn More About Bankruptcy in Iowa

Chapter 7 Guide
Chapter 13 Guide

## Bankruptcy Income Limits in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/income-limits.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Income Limits

Bankruptcy Income Limits in Iowa

Your income determines which bankruptcy chapter you qualify for in Iowa and how much you pay in Chapter 13. Here is how the income limits work.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Chapter 7 Income Limits

Chapter 7 uses the means test to determine eligibility. The test compares your household's current monthly income (averaged over the prior 6 months) to the Iowa median income for your household size.

If Below Median Income

You automatically pass the means test and qualify for Chapter 7. No further calculation is needed.

If Above Median Income

You must complete the full means test, which deducts allowed expenses from your income. If the remaining disposable income falls below the threshold, you still qualify. The means test uses IRS expense standards plus your actual secured debt payments.

Iowa median income figures are updated twice per year (April and October) by the U.S. Trustee Program. Check bankruptcymeanstest.org for current figures.

Chapter 13 - No Income Ceiling

Chapter 13 has no maximum income limit. Anyone with regular income can file, regardless of how high that income is. However, your income affects:

Plan length: Above-median income requires a 5-year plan; below-median allows 3-5 years

Payment amount: Higher income means higher monthly plan payments

Debt limits: Total debts (secured plus unsecured) cannot exceed $2,750,000

Chapter 13 requires regular income - wages, salary, self-employment, Social Security, pension, or other consistent sources.

Income Calculation Details

Current monthly income (CMI) includes almost all income from all sources over the 6 months before filing:

Wages, salary, overtime, bonuses, commissions

Self-employment income (gross receipts minus ordinary business expenses)

Interest, dividends, rental income

Pension, retirement, annuity payments

Unemployment compensation

Contributions from household members (spouse, non-filing partner)

Excluded from CMI: Social Security benefits, payments to victims of war crimes or terrorism.

Frequently Asked Questions

What are the income limits for bankruptcy in Iowa?

For Chapter 7, your household income must be at or below the Iowa median for your household size, or you must pass the means test expense deduction. Chapter 13 has no income ceiling - anyone with regular income can file. However, Chapter 13 has a debt limit of $2,750,000.

What if my income is too high for Chapter 7?

If your income exceeds the Iowa median, you still might qualify through the means test expense deductions. If not, Chapter 13 allows any income level - higher income just means higher plan payments over 3-5 years.

Does household size affect income limits?

Yes. The median income threshold increases with household size. A single filer has a lower threshold than a family of four. Household size typically includes everyone living in the home and dependents you support.

Check Your Eligibility

The means test and discharge screener can help determine your options.

## Iowa Bankruptcy - Chapter 7 & 13 Filing Guide [2026]
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/

Skip to main content

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesCedar RapidsChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsOBP Rule 9037 PII Scanner

Iowa Bankruptcy - Your Complete Filing Guide for 2026

You are not alone. Iowa's bankruptcy filing rate is moderate and has declined steadily since the post-recession peak. This free guide covers everything Iowa residents need to know about filing bankruptcy, from exemptions to costs to what happens after your case is filed.

Read the FAQ
Check Your Eligibility

Unlimited

Homestead Exemption

2

Federal Districts

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13: Which Is Right for You?

Chapter 7 - Fresh Start

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) in about 3-4 months. You must pass a means test based on your income.

Discharge in 3-4 months

Eliminates credit cards, medical debt

Must pass the means test

May lose non-exempt property

Filing fee: $338

Learn more about Chapter 7 →

Chapter 13 - Payment Plan

Chapter 13 lets you keep your property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Good for saving a home from foreclosure or a car from repossession. No means test required.

3-5 year repayment plan

Keep your home and car

Catch up on missed payments

No means test requirement

Filing fee: $313

Learn more about Chapter 13 →

Not sure which chapter fits? Compare them in detail.

Iowa Federal Court Districts

Iowa has 2 federal bankruptcy districts. Where you live determines where you file.

Northern District of Iowa (N.D. Iowa)

over 25,000 cases

Courthouse: 111 7th Ave SE, Cedar Rapids, IA 52401

Phone: (319) 286-2200 | ianb.uscourts.gov

Southern District of Iowa (S.D. Iowa)

over 35,000 cases

Courthouse: 110 E. Court Ave, Des Moines, IA 50309

Phone: (515) 284-6230 | iasb.uscourts.gov

Iowa Exemptions at a Glance

Iowa has an unlimited homestead exemption, protecting all equity in your primary residence regardless of value, as long as the property does not exceed 1/2 acre in a city or 40 acres in rural areas.

PropertyIowa Exemption

HomesteadUnlimited

VehicleUp to $7,000 (one motor vehicle)

Household goods$7,000 total

Wildcard$1,000

Retirement (IRA/401k)Fully exempt

Social SecurityFully exempt

Full exemption details →

How Much Does Bankruptcy Cost in Iowa?

Chapter 7 filing fee: $338 (can request installment payments)

Chapter 13 filing fee: $313 (can be paid through your plan)

Attorney fees: Typically $1,000 to $1,800 for Chapter 7; $2,500 to $4,000 for Chapter 13 in Iowa

Credit counseling: $15-25 per course (two required)

Fee waivers are available for Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150% of the federal poverty guidelines.

Full cost breakdown → | National cost guide

Frequently Asked Questions

How much does bankruptcy cost in Iowa?

Chapter 7 filing fees are $338 and Chapter 13 filing fees are $313. Attorney fees in Iowa typically range from $1,000 to $1,800 for Chapter 7 and $2,500 to $4,000 for Chapter 13. You will also need to pay for two required credit counseling courses, usually $25-$50 total. Full cost breakdown.

Will I lose my house if I file bankruptcy in Iowa?

Iowa's homestead exemption protects up to Unlimited of equity in your home. If your equity is below that amount, you can keep your home in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you can keep your home while catching up on missed payments through your repayment plan. See all exemptions.

How long does bankruptcy take?

Chapter 7 typically takes 3-4 months from filing to discharge. Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan before receiving a discharge. The 341 meeting of creditors usually happens about 30 days after filing.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13?

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt quickly but requires passing a means test. Chapter 13 allows you to keep property while repaying debts over 3-5 years. Your income, assets, and type of debt determine which chapter is best. Compare chapters.

Where do I file for bankruptcy in Iowa?

Iowa has 2 federal bankruptcy districts: N.D. Iowa, S.D. Iowa. You file in the district where you have lived for the greater part of the last 180 days.

See all frequently asked questions →

## Judgments and Bankruptcy in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/judgments.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Judgments

Judgments and Bankruptcy in Iowa

A civil judgment against you does not mean you are out of options. Bankruptcy can discharge the underlying debt and even remove judgment liens from your property in Iowa.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Discharging Judgments in Bankruptcy

Whether a judgment is dischargeable depends on the type of debt behind it:

Generally Dischargeable

Credit card judgments

Medical debt judgments

Personal loan judgments

Breach of contract judgments

Deficiency judgments (auto, mortgage)

Generally NOT Dischargeable

Judgments based on fraud

Judgments for willful injury

DUI/DWI damage judgments

Domestic support judgments

Student loan judgments (absent hardship)

Government fine/penalty judgments

Removing Judgment Liens Under Section 522(f)

Even when a judgment is discharged, the lien it created on your property may survive unless you take action. Under 11 U.S.C. section 522(f), you can file a motion to avoid (remove) a judicial lien if it impairs your exemptions.

How It Works in Iowa

Home: If your home equity minus the judgment lien and any mortgages is less than the unlimited homestead exemption, the lien impairs your exemption and can be avoided

Vehicle: Same analysis using the $7,000 vehicle exemption

Other property: Any exempt property with an impairing judgment lien qualifies

You must file a separate motion with the N.D. Iowa and S.D. Iowa court to avoid the lien. The discharge alone does not remove it.

Stopping Judgment Enforcement

The moment you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay immediately stops all judgment enforcement:

Wage garnishment must stop

Bank account levies are frozen

Property liens cannot be enforced

No new collection lawsuits can be filed

Existing lawsuits are paused

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I discharge a judgment in bankruptcy in Iowa?

Yes. Most money judgments based on unsecured debt (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) are dischargeable in bankruptcy in Iowa. The underlying debt determines dischargeability - if the original debt was dischargeable, the judgment based on that debt is also dischargeable.

Can I remove a judgment lien from my property?

Yes. Under 11 U.S.C. section 522(f), you can avoid (remove) a judicial lien on your property to the extent it impairs your bankruptcy exemptions. In Iowa, this is commonly used to remove judgment liens from homes (homestead exemption: unlimited) and vehicles (exemption: $7,000).

Does the judgment creditor have to stop collecting?

Yes. The automatic stay stops all collection on judgments the instant you file. The creditor cannot garnish wages, levy bank accounts, or enforce the judgment in any way while the stay is in effect.

Facing a Judgment?

Find out if bankruptcy can eliminate your judgment debt.

Discharge Screener

## Keep Your Car in Iowa Bankruptcy
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/keep-your-car.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesCedar RapidsChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Keep Your Car

Keep Your Car in Iowa Bankruptcy

One of the biggest fears people have about bankruptcy is losing their car. The good news: most people in Iowa keep their vehicles. Here is how car protection works in Iowa bankruptcy.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Iowa's Vehicle Exemption: $7,000

Iowa allows a $7,000 vehicle exemption. If your car is worth less than $7,000 (accounting for loans), it is fully protected.

How to calculate your equity: Take the fair market value of your car (check KBB or NADA) and subtract what you still owe on the loan. The result is your equity.

Example 1: Car worth $10,000, you owe $8,000 = $2,000 equity. Protected under the $7,000 exemption.

Example 2: Car worth $15,000, you owe $5,000 = $10,000 equity. Exceeds the exemption - but options exist.

Example 3: Car worth $6,000, you owe $9,000 = negative equity. Fully protected (no equity for trustee to take).

Your Options for Keeping Your Car

Chapter 7 Options

Reaffirmation: Sign a new agreement to keep paying the loan. The car and loan survive your bankruptcy.

Redemption: Pay the car's current market value in one lump sum, even if you owe more.

Surrender: Give back the car and discharge the remaining debt if the car is not worth keeping.

Chapter 13 Options

Catch up on arrears: Spread missed payments over your 3-5 year plan while keeping the car.

Cramdown: If the loan is more than 910 days old, reduce the balance to the car's actual value.

Reduce interest rate: The court may lower your interest rate to a market rate.

The 910-Day Rule (Cramdown)

Under 11 U.S.C. section 1325(a), if you purchased your car more than 910 days (about 2.5 years) before filing Chapter 13, you can "cram down" the loan to the car's current value.

Example: You bought a car 3 years ago for $25,000. You still owe $18,000, but the car is now worth $12,000. Through a cramdown, you would only pay $12,000 through your plan (plus interest), and the remaining $6,000 is treated as unsecured debt.

If you purchased the car within the last 910 days, you must pay the full loan balance through your plan.

What About Repossession?

If you are behind on payments and facing repossession, filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay, which immediately stops repossession efforts.

Before repo: Filing stops the repossession from happening

After repo but before sale: You may be able to get the car back through bankruptcy

After the car is sold: Recovery is much more difficult, but you may be able to discharge the deficiency balance

If repossession is imminent, timing is critical. Contact an attorney or legal aid immediately.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my car if I file bankruptcy in Iowa?

In most cases, yes. Iowa's vehicle exemption protects up to $7,000 of equity in your car. If your equity is below that amount, your car is fully protected in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you can keep your car while catching up on payments through your repayment plan.

What is Iowa's vehicle exemption?

Iowa allows you to exempt up to $7,000 of equity in a motor vehicle. Equity means the vehicle's market value minus what you owe on it. Iowa allows a $7,000 vehicle exemption. If your car is worth less than $7,000 (accounting for loans), it is fully protected.

What if my car equity exceeds the exemption?

If your car equity exceeds Iowa's $7,000 exemption, you have options: negotiate to pay the trustee the non-exempt amount, convert to Chapter 13 (where you keep property and pay through a plan), or use other available exemptions to cover the difference.

What is reaffirmation?

Reaffirmation is an agreement to continue paying a car loan after Chapter 7 bankruptcy. You keep the car and the loan, and the debt survives your discharge. This is common when you are current on payments and want to keep the car.

What is vehicle redemption in bankruptcy?

Redemption allows you to pay the current market value of the car in one lump sum, regardless of what you owe. If you owe $15,000 but the car is worth $8,000, you can pay $8,000 to keep it. This requires full payment at once (or through a redemption lender).

Can Chapter 13 help me keep my car?

Yes. Chapter 13 lets you catch up on missed payments over 3-5 years while keeping your vehicle. If the loan is more than 910 days old (about 2.5 years), you may be able to reduce the loan balance to the car's current value through a cramdown.

Check Your Discharge Eligibility

Use our free screener to see if you qualify for a bankruptcy discharge that could eliminate your car debt.

Free Discharge Screener
Keep Your Car Guide

## Keep Your House in Iowa Bankruptcy
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/keep-your-house.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesCedar RapidsChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Keep Your House

Keep Your House in Iowa Bankruptcy

Your home is often your most important asset. Here is how Iowa's Unlimited homestead exemption and bankruptcy law work together to help you keep your house.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Iowa's Homestead Exemption: Unlimited

Iowa offers one of the most generous homestead exemptions in the country - it is unlimited in value. Your home is fully protected as long as it is on half an acre or less in a city or 40 acres or less in rural areas.

How to calculate your home equity: Take the fair market value of your home and subtract all mortgages and liens. The result is your equity.

Example 1: Home worth $250,000, mortgage balance $200,000 = $50,000 equity. Protected under Iowa's Unlimited exemption.

Example 2: Home worth $400,000, mortgage balance $150,000 = $250,000 equity. Check if this exceeds Iowa's exemption limit.

Example 3: Home worth $180,000, mortgage balance $200,000 = negative equity. Fully protected (no equity for trustee to take).

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Your Home

Chapter 7

Homestead exemption protects equity up to Unlimited

Eliminates unsecured debt, making mortgage payments more affordable

Does NOT stop foreclosure long-term - only delays it during the case

You must stay current on mortgage payments to keep the home

Best if you are current on mortgage and need to eliminate other debts

Chapter 13

Stops foreclosure through the automatic stay

Catch up on missed payments over 3-5 years

May strip off underwater second mortgages

Protects home regardless of equity amount

Best if you are behind on mortgage payments

Foreclosure in Iowa

Iowa allows non-judicial foreclosure, but most foreclosures proceed judicially. Iowa provides a generous 12-month right of redemption after a foreclosure sale, giving homeowners significant time to reclaim their property.

Timeline: Judicial foreclosure in Iowa typically takes 5-7 months, but the 12-month redemption period adds substantial additional time.

If you are facing foreclosure in Iowa, bankruptcy can stop the process. The automatic stay halts all foreclosure proceedings the moment you file. Chapter 13 then gives you a structured way to catch up on missed payments while keeping your home.

Lien Stripping in Chapter 13

If your home is "underwater" on the first mortgage (you owe more than the home is worth), Chapter 13 allows you to strip off junior liens like second mortgages and HELOCs. The junior lien is reclassified as unsecured debt and may be discharged at the end of your plan.

Example: Your home is worth $200,000. First mortgage: $210,000. Second mortgage: $50,000. Because the first mortgage exceeds the home's value, the $50,000 second mortgage can be stripped and treated as unsecured debt, potentially saving you $50,000.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my house if I file bankruptcy in Iowa?

In most cases, yes. Iowa's homestead exemption protects up to Unlimited of equity in your home. If your equity is below that amount, your home is fully protected in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, you keep your home while catching up on missed mortgage payments through a 3-5 year repayment plan.

What is Iowa's homestead exemption?

Iowa offers one of the most generous homestead exemptions in the country - it is unlimited in value. Your home is fully protected as long as it is on half an acre or less in a city or 40 acres or less in rural areas.

Can Chapter 13 stop foreclosure in Iowa?

Yes. Filing Chapter 13 triggers the automatic stay, which immediately stops foreclosure proceedings. You can then catch up on missed mortgage payments over 3-5 years through your repayment plan while continuing to make regular monthly payments going forward.

How does foreclosure work in Iowa?

Iowa uses non-judicial foreclosure. Iowa allows non-judicial foreclosure, but most foreclosures proceed judicially. Iowa provides a generous 12-month right of redemption after a foreclosure sale, giving homeowners significant time to reclaim their property.

What happens to my second mortgage in bankruptcy?

If your home is worth less than what you owe on the first mortgage, Chapter 13 may allow you to strip off (remove) the second mortgage entirely. The second mortgage is treated as unsecured debt. This is called lien stripping and can save homeowners thousands.

Protect Your Home Today

Learn about your options and check your discharge eligibility with our free tools.

Free Discharge Screener
Keep Your House Guide

## Filing Bankruptcy for Medical Debt in Iowa - Discharge, Alternatives, Protections
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/medical-debt-only.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Filing Bankruptcy for Medical Debt in Iowa

Filing Bankruptcy for Medical Debt in Iowa

Medical debt is the leading cause of bankruptcy in America. It is fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13. You do not need to feel guilty about discharging medical debt - the system is designed for exactly this situation.

Quick answer: Medical debt is 100% dischargeable in bankruptcy. You cannot file only for medical debt (all eligible debts are included), but medical bills are unsecured, non-priority debt - the easiest category to discharge. Before filing, check if you qualify for hospital charity care or financial assistance programs in Iowa.

Medical Debt in Bankruptcy: The Basics

Medical debt is classified as general unsecured, non-priority debt in bankruptcy - the same category as credit cards and personal loans. This is the most favorable classification for discharge:

Chapter 7: Medical debt is completely eliminated when you receive your discharge (typically 3-4 months after filing)

Chapter 13: Medical debt is paid at whatever percentage your plan pays to unsecured creditors. In many Chapter 13 plans, unsecured creditors receive 10% or less. A $50,000 medical bill might result in paying $5,000 or less over 3-5 years.

No exception to discharge: Unlike student loans or certain tax debts, there is no exception that prevents medical debt from being discharged

Iowa Medical Debt Protections

Medical debt is fully dischargeable in Iowa bankruptcy. Iowa does not have a specific medical debt protection statute comparable to some states. Iowa hospitals receiving Hill-Burton funds must provide charity care. The University of Iowa Hospitals and Clinics, as a state institution, has specific financial assistance programs. Iowa's rural hospitals often have more flexible charity care policies. Iowa expanded Medicaid under the ACA, helping reduce medical debt burdens.

Before Filing: Exhaust Other Options

Hospital Financial Assistance (Charity Care)

Under the ACA, nonprofit hospitals are required to have financial assistance policies. Many hospitals will significantly reduce or eliminate bills for patients below certain income thresholds (typically 200-400% of the federal poverty level). Ask the billing department about financial assistance before paying or filing bankruptcy.

Negotiate the Bill

Medical bills are often negotiable. Strategies include:

Request an itemized bill and dispute any errors

Ask for the "self-pay" or "uninsured" rate (often 40-60% less than the billed amount)

Offer a lump-sum settlement for less than the full amount

Request a payment plan with no interest

When Bankruptcy Makes More Sense

Bankruptcy is often the better choice when:

Medical debt is combined with other unsecured debts (credit cards, personal loans)

Total debt exceeds your ability to repay within a reasonable time

Creditors are suing or garnishing wages

The hospital denies charity care or the remaining balance is still unaffordable

Medical debt is on top of existing financial stress

Credit Report Changes for Medical Debt

Recent changes to credit reporting have reduced the impact of medical debt:

Medical debt under $500: No longer reported by Equifax, Experian, or TransUnion (as of 2023)

Paid medical collections: Removed from credit reports

1-year waiting period: Medical debt cannot appear on credit reports until at least 1 year after going to collections

FICO 10T and VantageScore 4.0: Newer scoring models reduce the weight of medical collections

Despite these improvements, large medical debts sent to third-party collectors can still damage your credit. Bankruptcy provides a definitive resolution.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I file bankruptcy just for medical debt?

You cannot selectively discharge only medical debt - all eligible unsecured debts are included. But if medical debt is your primary problem, bankruptcy eliminates it along with other unsecured debts.

Is medical debt dischargeable?

Yes, 100%. Medical debt is unsecured, non-priority debt - the easiest category to discharge. There is no exception preventing discharge.

Should I negotiate or file bankruptcy?

If medical debt is your only debt, try negotiation and charity care first. If you have multiple debts or the amounts are unmanageable, bankruptcy may be more efficient.

Will medical debt affect my credit?

Medical debt under $500 is no longer reported. Paid medical collections are removed. Large unpaid amounts can still affect credit until resolved through payment, settlement, or bankruptcy.

Does Iowa have medical debt protections?

Protections vary. Check with your hospital about financial assistance programs. Nonprofit hospitals are required to have charity care policies under federal law.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## Medical Debt and Bankruptcy in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/medical-debt.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesCedar RapidsChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Medical Debt

Medical Debt and Bankruptcy in Iowa

Medical debt is the leading cause of bankruptcy in America. If you are overwhelmed by medical bills in Iowa, bankruptcy can eliminate that debt and give you a fresh start.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Medical Debt Is Fully Dischargeable

Medical debt is classified as general unsecured debt under bankruptcy law. This means it receives no special protection and is typically eliminated entirely in bankruptcy.

Chapter 7: Medical debt is wiped out completely within 3-4 months of filing

Chapter 13: Medical debt is included in your repayment plan, and any unpaid balance is discharged at the end

No minimum amount: You can file bankruptcy whether you owe $5,000 or $500,000 in medical debt

All providers included: Hospital bills, doctor bills, ambulance fees, lab work, prescription drugs - all dischargeable

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Medical Debt

Chapter 7

Eliminates 100% of medical debt

Completed in 3-4 months

Must pass the means test

Non-exempt assets may be sold by trustee

Iowa exemptions: Unlimited homestead, $7,000 vehicle

Best for: people with primarily unsecured debt and limited assets

Chapter 13

Pay what you can afford over 3-5 years

Remaining medical debt discharged at end

No means test requirement

Keep all your property

Can also catch up on mortgage/car payments

Best for: people with assets to protect or higher income

Before Filing: Alternatives to Consider

Negotiate directly. Many hospitals offer significant discounts for uninsured patients or will reduce bills by 30-60% for prompt payment.

Financial assistance programs. Non-profit hospitals are required to have charity care policies. Ask about financial hardship applications.

Payment plans. Most providers will set up interest-free payment plans. Make sure any plan is affordable - do not agree to payments you cannot sustain.

Statute of limitations. Medical debt has a limited collection window. If the debt is old, check whether it is still legally enforceable in Iowa.

Medical billing errors. Studies show a significant percentage of medical bills contain errors. Request itemized bills and dispute anything incorrect.

If these alternatives do not resolve your situation, bankruptcy remains a powerful tool for eliminating medical debt entirely.

Iowa-Specific Medical Debt Rules

Iowa follows the federal standard: creditors can garnish the lesser of 25% of disposable earnings or the amount by which weekly disposable earnings exceed 30 times the federal minimum wage ($217.50/week in 2026).

Medical providers who obtain a judgment against you in Iowa can use wage garnishment, bank levies, and property liens to collect. Filing bankruptcy stops all of these collection methods through the automatic stay.

Legal aid: Iowa Legal Aid (iowalegalaid.org) may offer free assistance with medical debt and bankruptcy.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can bankruptcy eliminate medical debt?

Yes. Medical debt is unsecured debt, which is typically fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy. In Chapter 7, medical debt is eliminated entirely within 3-4 months. In Chapter 13, you may pay a portion through your plan, with the remainder discharged.

Can a hospital sue me for medical debt in Iowa?

Yes. Hospitals and medical providers can sue to collect unpaid medical bills in Iowa. If they win a judgment, they may be able to garnish your wages or levy your bank accounts. Filing bankruptcy stops all lawsuits and collection actions through the automatic stay.

Will bankruptcy stop medical debt wage garnishment?

Yes. The automatic stay immediately stops all wage garnishment, including garnishment for medical debt. In Iowa, Iowa follows the federal standard: creditors can garnish the lesser of 25% of disposable earnings or the amount by which weekly disposable earnings exceed 30 times the federal minimum wage ($217.50/week in 2026).

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13 for medical debt?

Chapter 7 is usually preferred for medical debt because it eliminates the debt completely in 3-4 months with no repayment. Chapter 13 is better if you have assets to protect that exceed Iowa's exemptions, or if you do not qualify for Chapter 7 under the means test.

Does medical debt affect my credit score?

Yes, but less than before. Since 2023, paid medical debt no longer appears on credit reports. Unpaid medical debt under $500 also no longer appears. Medical debt in collections still affects your score but is given less weight than other types of debt by newer scoring models.

Overwhelmed by Medical Bills?

Check your eligibility for a bankruptcy discharge that could eliminate your medical debt.

Free Discharge Screener

## Payday Loans and Bankruptcy in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/payday-loans.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Payday Loans

Payday Loans and Bankruptcy in Iowa

Payday loans trap borrowers in cycles of high-interest debt. Bankruptcy can break that cycle by discharging payday loan obligations entirely.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Payday Loans Are Dischargeable

Payday loans are unsecured consumer debt. They are fully dischargeable in bankruptcy - meaning you will not owe anything after your case is completed.

Chapter 7: Payday loan debt is eliminated entirely in 3-4 months

Chapter 13: Payday loans are included in your repayment plan as general unsecured debt, typically receiving only a fraction of the balance

Stopping ACH Debits and Post-Dated Checks

Many payday lenders require ACH (electronic) authorization or post-dated checks as a condition of the loan. Once you file bankruptcy:

The automatic stay prohibits the lender from processing any further debits

Revoke your ACH authorization in writing with both the lender and your bank

Notify your bank of the bankruptcy filing and request a stop payment on any post-dated checks

If the lender processes a payment after filing, they have violated the stay and may owe you damages

Take action immediately upon filing: contact your bank, revoke ACH authorizations in writing, and keep copies of all communications.

Can Payday Lenders Challenge Discharge?

In rare cases, a payday lender might argue that the loan was obtained through fraud (11 U.S.C. section 523(a)(2)). To succeed, they would need to prove:

You made a false representation (e.g., lied about income or employment)

You intended to deceive the lender

The lender relied on your misrepresentation

In practice, payday lenders rarely challenge discharge because the amounts are small and the legal costs are high. Taking out a payday loan when you know you plan to file bankruptcy could raise issues, so consult an attorney about timing.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I discharge payday loans in bankruptcy in Iowa?

Yes. Payday loans are unsecured debt and are fully dischargeable in both Chapter 7 and Chapter 13 bankruptcy in Iowa. Once discharged, the lender cannot collect, even if they have post-dated checks or ACH authorization.

Can a payday lender cash my check after I file bankruptcy?

No. The automatic stay prevents payday lenders from cashing post-dated checks or processing ACH debits after you file. If they do, it is a stay violation and you may be entitled to damages.

Should I stop paying payday loans before filing?

Once you decide to file bankruptcy, continuing to pay payday loans means less money available for necessary living expenses. However, consult an attorney about timing, especially regarding any pending ACH authorizations or post-dated checks.

Break the Payday Loan Cycle

Check if you qualify for a bankruptcy discharge to eliminate payday loan debt.

Discharge Screener

## Undoing a Reaffirmation Agreement in Iowa - 60-Day Rescission, Options
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/reaffirmation-regret.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Undoing a Reaffirmation Agreement in Iowa

Undoing a Reaffirmation Agreement in Iowa

Reaffirmation agreements make you personally liable for a debt that would otherwise be discharged. If you regret signing one, you may have options - especially if you are still within the 60-day rescission window.

Quick answer: You can rescind (cancel) a reaffirmation agreement within 60 days of filing it with the court, or before discharge, whichever is later (11 U.S.C. section 524(c)(4)). No reason needed - just send written notice to the creditor. After 60 days, options are limited but include negotiation, voluntary surrender, or a new filing.

The 60-Day Rescission Window

Under 11 U.S.C. section 524(c)(4), you have an absolute right to rescind any reaffirmation agreement at any time before discharge or within 60 days after the agreement is filed with the court, whichever is later.

No reason required - you do not have to explain why

Written notice to the creditor is sufficient

The debt becomes dischargeable again once rescinded

The creditor may repossess the collateral (vehicle, etc.) after rescission

If you are considering rescission, act quickly. Count the days carefully from when the agreement was filed with the court (check your case docket), not when you signed it.

Options After the 60-Day Window

If You Cannot Afford the Payments

After the rescission window closes, you have several options:

Negotiate with the creditor: Request lower payments, reduced interest, or loan modification. Creditors often prefer this to repossession/foreclosure.

Refinance: If your credit has improved since filing, refinancing at a lower rate may make payments affordable.

Voluntary surrender: Return the property. You will owe any deficiency balance (the difference between what you owe and what the property sells for). This is a regular debt - not discharged because you reaffirmed it.

File a new bankruptcy: If eligible under 11 U.S.C. section 727(a)(8) (Chapter 7: 8 years between filings) or section 1328(f) (Chapter 13: varies), the reaffirmed debt can be discharged in the new case.

Iowa-Specific Considerations

Iowa uses state exemptions only. The motor vehicle exemption is $7,000 per debtor (Iowa Code 627.6(9)(b)). The N.D. Iowa and S.D. Iowa courts review reaffirmation agreements. Iowa's relatively generous vehicle exemption means many vehicles can be protected without reaffirmation. The 60-day rescission period applies. Iowa Consumer Credit Code provides additional consumer protections.

Common Reaffirmation Regrets

Vehicle Reaffirmation

The most common reaffirmation regret involves vehicles. Debtors reaffirm a car loan to keep the vehicle but later realize the payment is unsustainable or the car is worth far less than the loan balance. Without reaffirmation, the debt would have been discharged - you could have surrendered the car and owed nothing.

Mortgage Reaffirmation

Most attorneys advise against mortgage reaffirmation. Without reaffirmation, you can keep making payments and stay in the home. If you ever need to walk away, the deficiency is discharged. With reaffirmation, you are personally liable for any deficiency after foreclosure.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I undo a reaffirmation agreement?

Within 60 days of filing or before discharge: yes, automatically. After that: options are limited to negotiation, refinancing, surrender, or a new bankruptcy filing.

What happens after the 60-day window?

The agreement is binding. You can negotiate with the creditor, refinance, voluntarily surrender, or file a new bankruptcy if eligible.

Do I need a reason to rescind?

No. The right to rescind within 60 days is absolute - no reason or justification needed. Simply send written notice to the creditor.

What if the court approved my reaffirmation?

Court approval does not prevent rescission during the 60-day window. The right to rescind under section 524(c)(4) applies regardless of court approval.

Should I have reaffirmed in the first place?

That depends on the specific asset and your financial situation. Many attorneys advise against reaffirmation (especially on mortgages) because it restores personal liability on debt that could have been discharged.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## Renting After Bankruptcy in Iowa - Landlord Screening, Tips, Tenant Rights
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/renting-after-bankruptcy.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Renting After Bankruptcy in Iowa

Renting After Bankruptcy in Iowa

Bankruptcy does not prevent you from renting. Many landlords will approve applicants with a bankruptcy on their record if you can show stable income and a reliable rental history. Here is how to navigate the process in Iowa.

Quick answer: You can rent after bankruptcy. Landlords can see the filing on your credit report, but it is not an automatic disqualifier. Focus on stable income, rental references, an honest explanation, and (if needed) a larger security deposit. Government housing assistance cannot be denied based on bankruptcy under section 525(a).

What Landlords See on Your Credit Report

When a landlord runs a credit check, they will see:

The bankruptcy filing - Chapter 7 (10 years) or Chapter 13 (7 years)

Discharged debts - accounts included in the bankruptcy will show as "included in bankruptcy" or "discharged"

Your credit score - typically drops significantly at filing but recovers over time

Post-filing payment history - new accounts opened after filing show your current reliability

Many landlords focus more on recent history than old entries. A bankruptcy from 2-3 years ago with perfect payment history since then is viewed very differently than a recent filing.

Iowa Tenant Rights and Protections

Iowa has the Iowa Uniform Residential Landlord and Tenant Act (Iowa Code Ch. 562A). Landlords can run credit checks including bankruptcy history. Iowa does not have rent control. Iowa's rental market is generally more affordable than coastal states, but landlords still screen applicants. Iowa law does not prohibit denial based on bankruptcy. Iowa's smaller cities may have more flexible landlords. Providing proof of steady employment and a larger deposit can help.

Tips for Getting Approved

1. Prepare a Rental Resume

Include your rental history, employment details, income verification, and references from previous landlords. A well-organized application signals reliability.

2. Write a Brief Explanation Letter

Address the bankruptcy directly. Explain the circumstances (job loss, medical emergency, divorce) and emphasize what has changed. Show that you have stable income and have been managing finances responsibly since filing.

3. Offer a Larger Security Deposit

Offering an additional month's deposit reduces the landlord's risk and shows good faith. Check Iowa law for any limits on security deposit amounts.

4. Consider Individual Landlords Over Large Complexes

Individual landlords and small property owners often have more flexibility in their screening criteria than large corporate-managed apartment complexes that use automated approval algorithms.

5. Get References

A letter from a previous landlord confirming on-time payments and good tenancy can outweigh a credit blemish. Employment references showing stable income also help.

Government Housing After Bankruptcy

Under 11 U.S.C. section 525(a), government agencies cannot deny public benefits based solely on a bankruptcy filing. This includes:

Section 8 Housing Choice Vouchers

Public housing

State housing assistance programs

FHA-insured mortgages (after applicable waiting periods)

The waiting period for FHA loans is 2 years after Chapter 7 discharge or 1 year into a Chapter 13 plan with court approval.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I rent after bankruptcy in Iowa?

Yes. Many landlords will approve applicants with bankruptcy if you show stable income, good rental history, and an honest explanation of your filing.

How long does bankruptcy show on my credit report?

Chapter 7: 10 years. Chapter 13: 7 years. The impact on your score diminishes over time, especially with good post-filing payment history.

Can a landlord deny me for bankruptcy?

For new applications, landlords can consider bankruptcy as part of screening in most states. For renewals, some courts extend section 525 protections. Government housing cannot be denied based on bankruptcy.

Should I explain the bankruptcy to a landlord?

Yes. A brief, honest explanation shows responsibility and maturity. Emphasize what changed and your current financial stability.

Can I get Section 8 after bankruptcy?

Yes. Under section 525(a), government agencies cannot deny public benefits based on bankruptcy. Section 8, public housing, and other government housing programs remain available.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House

## Repossession and Credit Reports After Bankruptcy in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/repo-credit-report.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Repo & Credit Report

Repossession and Credit Reports After Bankruptcy in Iowa

Bankruptcy does not erase the repo from your credit report - but it eliminates the debt. Here is what changes, what stays, and how to fix errors.

Quick answer: The repossession entry stays on your credit report for 7 years from the date of first delinquency. Bankruptcy discharges the remaining balance (deficiency), which should be reported as $0. If the creditor still reports a balance after discharge, dispute it under the FCRA.

What Changes After Discharge

What Bankruptcy Fixes

Deficiency balance discharged - you owe $0

Collection activity must stop permanently

Account status should update to "discharged"

No more calls, letters, or lawsuits about the balance

Creditor cannot report a balance owed

What Bankruptcy Does Not Fix

Repossession entry remains on credit report

Late payment history leading up to repo remains

7-year reporting clock does not reset

Bankruptcy filing itself appears on credit report

Credit score impact from both events

Disputing Credit Report Errors

After bankruptcy discharge, review your credit reports from all three bureaus (Equifax, Experian, TransUnion) for these common errors:

Balance still showing: Should be $0 after discharge. This is the most common error.

"Past due" status: Should show "discharged in bankruptcy" or "included in bankruptcy."

Active collection: The account should not show as in active collection after discharge.

Incorrect dates: The date of first delinquency determines when the entry falls off. Verify this date is correct.

1

Pull Reports

Get free reports from annualcreditreport.com (all three bureaus)

2

Identify Errors

Compare each repo account against your discharge order

3

Dispute

File disputes online with each bureau. Include your discharge order as evidence.

FCRA Rights After Bankruptcy

The Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) gives you powerful tools to correct your credit report after bankruptcy:

Dispute right: You can dispute any inaccurate information. The bureau has 30 days to investigate and correct or remove the entry.

Creditor obligations: Creditors who report to credit bureaus must update accounts to reflect the discharge. Failure to do so violates the FCRA.

Damages: If a creditor willfully reports incorrect information after discharge, you may be entitled to statutory damages ($100-$1,000), actual damages, and attorney fees under the FCRA.

Discharge injunction: Reporting a balance owed on a discharged debt may also violate the bankruptcy discharge injunction (11 U.S.C. section 524), which can result in contempt sanctions.

Rebuilding Credit After Repo + Bankruptcy

Secured credit card: Start with a secured card ($200-$500 deposit). Use it for small purchases and pay in full monthly.

Credit-builder loan: Some Iowa credit unions offer loans specifically designed to rebuild credit.

Authorized user: Ask a trusted person to add you as an authorized user on their account.

On-time payments: Every on-time payment builds your score. Payment history is the largest factor (35%).

Time: The repo and bankruptcy impact your score less as they age. Most people see significant score improvement within 2-3 years of discharge.

Frequently Asked Questions

Does bankruptcy remove a repossession from my credit report in Iowa?

No. Bankruptcy does not remove the repossession entry from your credit report. The repo stays on your report for 7 years from the date of first delinquency. However, the bankruptcy discharge eliminates the deficiency balance - the amount you still owe after the repo sale.

What happens to the repo balance after bankruptcy?

In Chapter 7, the deficiency balance (what you owe minus what the vehicle sold for) is discharged. In Chapter 13, it is treated as unsecured debt and paid at the plan percentage, often cents on the dollar. Either way, the balance should be reported as $0 after discharge.

How should a repo appear on my credit report after discharge?

After bankruptcy discharge, the repossession account should show: balance $0, 'included in bankruptcy' or 'discharged in bankruptcy.' If the report still shows a balance owed, that is a reporting error you can dispute.

Can I dispute a repo on my credit report after bankruptcy?

Yes. Under the FCRA (Fair Credit Reporting Act), you can dispute inaccurate information. If the repo account shows a balance after discharge, shows as 'past due' rather than 'discharged,' or has incorrect dates, file disputes with all three bureaus.

How long does a repo stay on my credit report in Iowa?

A repossession stays on your credit report for 7 years from the date of first delinquency, regardless of bankruptcy. The bankruptcy filing itself stays for 7 years (Chapter 13) or 10 years (Chapter 7). These are federal rules that apply uniformly across all states including Iowa.

Can the repo lender collect after bankruptcy discharge?

No. The discharge injunction under 11 U.S.C. section 524 permanently prohibits the creditor from collecting the discharged debt. If they attempt collection (calls, letters, reporting a balance), you may have a claim for violation of the discharge injunction.

## Vehicle Repossession and Bankruptcy in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/repossession.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesCedar RapidsChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Repossession

Vehicle Repossession and Bankruptcy in Iowa

Is your car about to be repossessed? Bankruptcy can stop repossession immediately and may help you get your vehicle back. Here is how it works in Iowa.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Before Repossession: Filing Stops It

If you are behind on your car payments and facing repossession, filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. section 362. The stay immediately prohibits the lender from:

Sending a tow truck to take your car

Hiring a repossession agent

Demanding you surrender the vehicle

Contacting you about the delinquent payments

The stay takes effect the instant your petition is filed - even before the creditor is notified. However, you should inform the creditor as soon as possible to prevent a violation.

After Repossession: Your Options

If your car has already been repossessed, your options depend on whether it has been sold:

Car Not Yet Sold

Filing bankruptcy and requesting turnover of the vehicle may force the creditor to return it

Chapter 13 allows you to catch up on payments while keeping the car

You must act quickly - creditors typically sell repossessed vehicles within 10-14 days

Car Already Sold

You generally cannot get the car back after it is sold

The deficiency balance (what you still owe after the sale) is unsecured debt

This deficiency can be discharged in Chapter 7 or Chapter 13

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 After Repossession

Chapter 7

Discharges deficiency balance after repo

Cannot catch up on missed payments

May require surrendering the car if you still have it

Reaffirmation or redemption options available

$7,000 vehicle exemption protects equity

Chapter 13

Catch up on missed payments over 3-5 years

May recover vehicle after repo (if not yet sold)

Cramdown option if loan is over 910 days old

Keep the vehicle while repaying through plan

Better for keeping your car long-term

Iowa Vehicle Protection

Iowa allows a $7,000 vehicle exemption. If your car is worth less than $7,000 (accounting for loans), it is fully protected.

See our full guide on keeping your car in Iowa bankruptcy for more details on exemptions, reaffirmation, redemption, and cramdown options.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can bankruptcy stop repossession in Iowa?

Yes. Filing bankruptcy triggers the automatic stay, which immediately prohibits creditors from repossessing your vehicle. If repossession is imminent, an emergency bankruptcy filing can stop it.

Can I get my car back after repossession?

If the car has not been sold yet, filing bankruptcy may allow you to recover it. The automatic stay requires the creditor to return the vehicle in some jurisdictions. Once the car is sold, recovery is much more difficult.

What is a deficiency balance after repossession?

If your car is repossessed and sold for less than you owe, the remaining balance is called a deficiency. For example, if you owed $15,000 and the car sold for $10,000, the $5,000 deficiency is now unsecured debt. This deficiency is dischargeable in bankruptcy.

How does Iowa's vehicle exemption work with repossession?

Iowa protects up to $7,000 of equity in your vehicle. If you file Chapter 7, the exemption protects that amount of equity from the bankruptcy trustee. The exemption does not stop a secured creditor from repossessing the vehicle, but the automatic stay does.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13 to keep my car?

Chapter 13 is usually better if you want to keep a car you are behind on payments for. It allows you to catch up on missed payments over 3-5 years. Chapter 7 can discharge the debt but may require surrendering the vehicle unless you reaffirm or redeem.

Stop Repossession Today

Check your eligibility and learn about your options to protect your vehicle.

Free Discharge Screener
Keep Your Car Guide

## Iowa Bankruptcy Resources - Courts, Legal Aid, Credit Counseling
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/resources.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Resources

Iowa Bankruptcy Resources

Courts, legal aid organizations, credit counseling agencies, and free educational tools to help you navigate the bankruptcy process in Iowa.

Bankruptcy Courts

Northern District of Iowa

Address: 111 7th Ave SE, Cedar Rapids, IA 52401

Phone: (319) 286-2200

Website: ianb.uscourts.gov

Southern District of Iowa

Address: 110 E. Court Ave, Des Moines, IA 50309

Phone: (515) 284-6230

Website: iasb.uscourts.gov

Legal Aid Organizations

Iowa Legal Aid

Free legal services for low-income Iowans, including bankruptcy assistance.

Phone: (800) 532-1275

Website: iowalegalaid.org

Credit Counseling and Debtor Education

Two courses are required for bankruptcy: a pre-filing credit counseling course and a post-filing debtor education course. You must use agencies approved by the U.S. Trustee Program.

Approved agencies: justice.gov - approved agencies

Cost: $15-25 per course (reduced fees available)

Format: Online, phone, or in-person

Time: About 60-90 minutes per course

U.S. Trustee Program

The U.S. Trustee Program oversees the administration of bankruptcy cases.

Region 12: (515) 284-4982

Website: justice.gov/ust

Free Educational Tools

Open Bankruptcy Project Network

1328(f) Discharge Eligibility Screener - check if a prior discharge affects your eligibility

Automatic Stay Guide

341 Meeting Guide

Means Test Explained

National Cost Guide

How to File Bankruptcy

Case Dismissed?

File Again?

Dismissal Rate Data

Frequently Asked Questions

Where is the bankruptcy court in Iowa?

Iowa has 2 federal bankruptcy courts. N.D. Iowa: 111 7th Ave SE, Cedar Rapids, IA 52401 ((319) 286-2200); S.D. Iowa: 110 E. Court Ave, Des Moines, IA 50309 ((515) 284-6230)

Is there free legal help for bankruptcy in Iowa?

Yes. Iowa Legal Aid ((800) 532-1275) provides free legal services.

Where can I take the required credit counseling course?

The U.S. Trustee Program maintains a list of approved credit counseling agencies at justice.gov/ust. Most offer courses online or by phone for $15-25.

## Can I Keep My 401k and Retirement in Iowa Bankruptcy?
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/retirement-accounts.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Retirement Accounts and Bankruptcy in Iowa

Retirement Accounts and Bankruptcy in Iowa

Your retirement savings are among the most protected assets in bankruptcy. ERISA-qualified plans are 100% exempt under federal law, and IRAs are protected up to $1,512,350. Here is what Iowa filers need to know.

Quick answer: Your 401(k), 403(b), pension, and other ERISA-qualified retirement plans are 100% exempt in bankruptcy - no dollar limit. IRAs (traditional and Roth) are protected up to $1,512,350. Do NOT cash out retirement to pay debts or avoid filing. That converts protected money into unprotected money.

ERISA Plans: 100% Protected

Under federal law (11 U.S.C. section 522(b)(3)(C) and ERISA section 206(d)), employer-sponsored retirement plans are completely exempt from the bankruptcy estate. This includes:

401(k) plans - no dollar limit on exemption

403(b) plans (tax-sheltered annuities) - fully exempt

Defined benefit pension plans - fully exempt

Profit-sharing plans - fully exempt

457(b) government plans - fully exempt

Thrift Savings Plan (TSP) - fully exempt for federal employees

This protection applies nationwide regardless of state exemption choices. The trustee cannot touch these accounts.

IRAs: Protected Up to $1,512,350

Traditional IRAs and Roth IRAs are protected under 11 U.S.C. section 522(n) up to a combined limit of $1,512,350 (adjusted for inflation). This limit applies to:

Traditional IRAs

Roth IRAs

SEP-IRAs (may receive full ERISA protection if employer-sponsored)

SIMPLE IRAs (may receive full ERISA protection)

Rollover amounts from an ERISA plan into an IRA retain their unlimited ERISA protection - they do not count against the $1,512,350 cap.

Iowa-Specific Retirement Protections

Iowa has an unlimited homestead exemption and provides broad retirement protections. ERISA-qualified plans are 100% exempt under federal law. Under Iowa Code 627.6(8)(e), pensions, annuities, and retirement accounts are exempt to the extent reasonably necessary for support of the debtor and dependents. Iowa public employee retirement (IPERS) benefits are fully exempt. The 'reasonably necessary for support' standard gives Iowa courts flexibility.

Important: Iowa uses state only (no federal option) exemptions. If you have a choice between state and federal exemptions, compare the total exemption picture - retirement is just one factor.

The Biggest Mistake: Cashing Out Retirement

Every year, thousands of people cash out retirement accounts to pay debts or avoid bankruptcy. This is almost always a mistake because:

You lose the exemption. Money in a retirement account is protected. Money in a bank account is not (or minimally protected).

You owe income taxes on the entire withdrawal amount.

You pay a 10% early withdrawal penalty if under age 59 1/2.

The debts may be dischargeable anyway. Credit card debt, medical debt, and personal loans are wiped out in bankruptcy - you could have kept the retirement money and discharged the debt.

You cannot replace the money. Annual contribution limits mean it takes years to rebuild what you withdrew.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I keep my 401(k) in Iowa bankruptcy?

Yes. ERISA-qualified plans are 100% exempt under federal law. Your 401(k) is completely protected regardless of balance.

Are IRAs protected?

Yes, up to $1,512,350 combined for all traditional and Roth IRAs. Rollover amounts from ERISA plans keep their unlimited protection.

Can I contribute to retirement during Chapter 13?

Generally yes at your existing level. Increasing contributions during the plan may be challenged by the trustee as reducing disposable income.

What about inherited IRAs?

Inherited IRAs are NOT protected in bankruptcy (Clark v. Rameker, 2014). Only IRAs you funded yourself are exempt. Spousal inherited IRAs may be protected if rolled into your own IRA.

Should I cash out retirement to avoid bankruptcy?

Almost never. You lose the exemption, owe taxes, pay penalties, and the debt might have been dischargeable. Talk to a bankruptcy attorney before touching retirement accounts.

Can creditors garnish retirement accounts?

ERISA plans are generally protected from garnishment. Exceptions: IRS tax levies, divorce QDROs, and certain federal criminal penalties.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt
