## Second Mortgages and Bankruptcy in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/second-mortgage.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Second Mortgage

Second Mortgages and Bankruptcy in Iowa

If your Iowa home is worth less than your first mortgage, Chapter 13 lien stripping can eliminate your second mortgage entirely.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

What Is Lien Stripping?

Lien stripping is a powerful tool available in Chapter 13 bankruptcy under 11 U.S.C. section 506(a). It allows you to remove a junior mortgage (second mortgage, HELOC, or other junior lien) from your home when:

Your home's current fair market value is less than the balance on your first mortgage

The second mortgage is therefore wholly unsecured - there is no equity supporting it

You file a motion with the N.D. Iowa and S.D. Iowa court asking to strip the lien

Once the Chapter 13 plan is completed, the second mortgage lien is permanently removed from your property.

How It Works

1

Get Home Appraised

Determine your home's fair market value. It must be less than the first mortgage balance for the second mortgage to qualify for stripping.

2

File Lien Strip Motion

Your attorney files a motion to value the collateral and strip the wholly unsecured junior lien. The second mortgage holder can object.

3

Complete the Plan

The second mortgage is treated as unsecured debt in your 3-5 year plan. Upon plan completion and discharge, the lien is permanently removed.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Second Mortgages

Chapter 7

Lien stripping is not available in Chapter 7 per Bank of America v. Caulkett (2015). The personal liability on the second mortgage can be discharged, but the lien survives. The creditor cannot collect from you personally, but the lien remains attached to the property.

Chapter 13

Lien stripping is available. If the home value is below the first mortgage balance, the second mortgage lien is stripped and the debt is treated as unsecured. Upon successful plan completion, the lien is permanently removed from your title.

Iowa Homestead Exemption Impact

The Iowa homestead exemption of unlimited protects your home equity from creditors. In a lien stripping scenario, the exemption is less relevant because the home is underwater - but it becomes critical if property values recover.

Learn more about protecting your home in Iowa →

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I strip a second mortgage in bankruptcy in Iowa?

Yes, through a process called lien stripping in Chapter 13. If your home is worth less than what you owe on your first mortgage, the second mortgage is considered wholly unsecured and can be treated as general unsecured debt in your plan. At completion of the plan, the lien is removed.

Can I strip a second mortgage in Chapter 7?

No. The Supreme Court ruled in Bank of America v. Caulkett (2015) that Chapter 7 debtors cannot strip wholly underwater junior liens. Lien stripping is only available in Chapter 13 (and Chapter 11) in Iowa.

What if I have some equity above the first mortgage?

If your home value exceeds the first mortgage balance even slightly, the second mortgage is not wholly unsecured and cannot be stripped. The full second mortgage balance would need to be addressed in your Chapter 13 plan or reaffirmed.

Explore Lien Stripping Options

Learn more about how lien stripping can save your home and reduce your debt.

Lien Stripping Guide
Iowa Chapter 13 Guide

## Bankruptcy and Security Clearance in Iowa - Will I Lose My Clearance?
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/security-clearance.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Bankruptcy and Security Clearance in Iowa

Bankruptcy and Security Clearance in Iowa

Filing bankruptcy does not automatically revoke your security clearance. In many cases, resolving debt through bankruptcy can actually help your clearance by eliminating financial vulnerability. Here is what clearance holders in Iowa need to know.

Quick answer: Bankruptcy does NOT automatically revoke your security clearance. The adjudicative guidelines recognize that taking responsible steps to resolve debt - including filing bankruptcy - can be a mitigating factor. Unresolved, spiraling debt is a bigger clearance risk than filing bankruptcy to resolve it.

Guideline F: Financial Considerations

Security clearance adjudications are governed by the Adjudicative Guidelines, specifically Guideline F (Financial Considerations). The concern is that financial problems can indicate poor self-control, unreliability, or vulnerability to pressure.

However, Guideline F also lists mitigating conditions, including:

The behavior happened under unusual circumstances (job loss, medical emergency, divorce) and is unlikely to recur

The individual has initiated and is adhering to a good-faith effort to resolve debts - bankruptcy qualifies

The conditions that resulted in the financial problem were largely beyond the person's control

There are clear indications the problem is being resolved or is under control

Filing bankruptcy directly addresses multiple mitigating conditions. It eliminates the debt, demonstrates proactive behavior, and removes the financial vulnerability that is the actual clearance concern.

Iowa Military and Defense Presence

Iowa hosts the Iowa National Guard, Camp Dodge, and various defense industry jobs. Major defense contractors have operations in Iowa. Filing bankruptcy does not automatically revoke clearance. Iowa's agricultural and manufacturing sectors also include some positions with financial bonding requirements, where bankruptcy may be relevant.

Self-Reporting Requirements

If you currently hold a security clearance, you are typically required to self-report significant financial events. This includes:

Filing for bankruptcy (either chapter)

Judgments or liens against you

Significant delinquencies (typically 120+ days past due)

Wage garnishments

Report through your Facility Security Officer (FSO) or Security Manager. Timely self-reporting demonstrates integrity and compliance - two qualities adjudicators value highly.

Failing to report a bankruptcy filing when required is a separate security concern. The cover-up is worse than the event itself. Always report promptly.

Why Bankruptcy Can Help Your Clearance

Without Bankruptcy

Growing debt creates ongoing vulnerability

Debt collectors and creditors pursuing you

Potential garnishments and judgments

Financial stress affects job performance

Adjudicator sees unresolved problems

After Bankruptcy

Debt eliminated or structured into a plan

No more creditor pressure or coercion risk

Clean financial slate going forward

Demonstrates proactive problem-solving

Adjudicator sees responsible resolution

Frequently Asked Questions

Will I lose my security clearance if I file bankruptcy?

No. Bankruptcy does not automatically revoke clearance. It can demonstrate financial responsibility by resolving debt that creates vulnerability.

Should I tell my security officer?

Yes. Self-reporting is typically required and demonstrates integrity. Failure to report is a separate concern.

Can bankruptcy help my clearance?

Yes. Eliminating debt removes financial vulnerability - the actual clearance concern. Adjudicators recognize bankruptcy as a responsible step.

Is Chapter 13 better than Chapter 7 for clearance?

Chapter 13 is sometimes viewed more favorably because it shows a repayment commitment. But Chapter 7 is also recognized as a legitimate debt resolution method.

Can I get a new clearance after bankruptcy?

Yes. Many people receive clearances after bankruptcy. Adjudicators evaluate the whole person, not just the bankruptcy filing.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## Small Business Bankruptcy in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/small-business.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesCedar RapidsChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Small Business

Small Business Bankruptcy in Iowa

Running a small business in Iowa is hard enough without crushing debt. If your business is struggling, bankruptcy offers several paths - from reorganization to an orderly wind-down.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Bankruptcy Options for Iowa Business Owners

Chapter 7 Liquidation

Business assets are sold to pay creditors

Remaining debts are discharged (for individuals)

Business ceases to exist

Personal guarantees can be discharged

Fastest option: 3-4 months

Best for: businesses with no viable future

Chapter 11 Subchapter V

Continue operating the business

Restructure debts over 3-5 years

No creditor committee (usually)

Debtor remains in control

Debt limit: $7.5 million

Best for: viable businesses needing debt relief

Chapter 13 (Sole Proprietors)

Available only to individuals (sole proprietors)

Continue operating the business

Restructure personal and business debts

Debt limits apply (currently ~$2.75M)

3-5 year repayment plan

Best for: sole proprietors under the debt limits

Close + Personal Chapter 7

Shut down the business first

File personal Chapter 7 to discharge guarantees

Eliminates personal liability for business debts

Must pass the means test

Clean break: start fresh

Best for: sole proprietors with personal guarantees

Personal Guarantees: The Hidden Trap

Even if your business is an LLC or corporation, you may be personally liable for business debts if you signed personal guarantees. This is extremely common for:

Business leases (office, retail, equipment)

Business loans and lines of credit

Business credit cards

Vendor credit accounts

If your LLC or corporation cannot pay these debts, creditors will come after you personally. Filing personal bankruptcy can discharge these personal guarantee obligations.

Subchapter V: The Game-Changer for Small Business

The Small Business Reorganization Act of 2019 created Subchapter V of Chapter 11, dramatically simplifying the reorganization process for small businesses:

No creditors' committee: Saves significant legal costs

Trustee role: A Subchapter V trustee facilitates the case but does not take over operations

Faster timeline: Plan must be filed within 90 days

No absolute priority rule: Owners can retain equity without paying creditors in full

Consensual or nonconsensual plans: The court can confirm a plan even if some creditors object

Lower costs: Typically 50-75% less expensive than traditional Chapter 11

Cases are filed in N.D. Iowa and S.D. Iowa. Courthouse: U.S. Bankruptcy Court, 123 E Walnut St, Des Moines, IA 50309 (S.D. Iowa)

Frequently Asked Questions

What are my bankruptcy options as a small business owner in Iowa?

Small business owners in Iowa have several options: Chapter 7 (liquidation of business assets), Chapter 11 Subchapter V (streamlined reorganization for businesses with less than $7.5 million in debt), Chapter 13 (if you are a sole proprietor with debts under the limit), or simply closing the business and filing personal Chapter 7 to discharge personal guarantees.

What is Subchapter V of Chapter 11?

Subchapter V is a streamlined Chapter 11 process created by the Small Business Reorganization Act of 2019. It is designed for businesses with less than $7.5 million in non-contingent, liquidated debt. It is faster, cheaper, and more debtor-friendly than traditional Chapter 11.

Am I personally liable for business debts?

It depends on your business structure. Sole proprietors are personally liable for all business debts. LLC members and corporate shareholders are generally not personally liable unless they signed personal guarantees, pierced the corporate veil, or committed fraud. Personal guarantees are the most common source of personal liability.

Can I keep my business and file bankruptcy?

Yes. Chapter 11 Subchapter V and Chapter 13 (for sole proprietors) allow you to continue operating your business while reorganizing debts. The business remains under your control, and you propose a repayment plan.

What happens to my employees if I file business bankruptcy?

If you reorganize under Chapter 11 or Chapter 13, you can continue employing your staff. If you liquidate under Chapter 7, employees are typically let go. Unpaid wages are a priority claim in bankruptcy and are paid before general creditors.

Explore Your Business Options

Whether you want to reorganize or start fresh, learn about your paths forward.

Free Discharge Screener

## Iowa Bankruptcy Statistics - Filing Data and Trends
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/statistics.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQStatsResourcesOBP

Home›Statistics

Iowa Bankruptcy Statistics

Data from the Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database (2008-2024). Iowa's bankruptcy filing rate is moderate and has declined steadily since the post-recession peak.

2

Federal Districts

Unlimited

Homestead Exemption

$338

Chapter 7 Filing Fee

$313

Chapter 13 Filing Fee

Iowa Federal Court Districts

DistrictCases (2008-2024)Primary Location

Northern District of Iowa (N.D. Iowa)over 25,000Cedar Rapids

Southern District of Iowa (S.D. Iowa)over 35,000Des Moines

Key Facts About Bankruptcy in Iowa

Homestead exemption: Unlimited - Iowa has an unlimited homestead exemption, protecting all equity in your primary residence regardless of value, as long as the property does not exceed 1/2 acre in a city or 40 acres in rural areas.

Chapter 13 dismissal risk: Nationally, about one-third to two-fifths of Chapter 13 cases end in dismissal. Quality of legal representation is one of the strongest predictors of plan completion.

Prior filer rate: Across the nation, roughly 25-30% of Chapter 13 filers had a prior bankruptcy case, suggesting many debtors cycle through the system without achieving lasting relief.

Means test: Iowa's median income for a single filer is approximately $50,000/year.

Understanding the Data

Chapter 13 plans run 3-5 years. Cases filed in recent years (2020-2024) are still active if succeeding - only early dismissals have been counted. The most reliable dismissal rates come from mature years (2008-2019) where most cases have reached final outcomes.

Source: Federal Judicial Center Integrated Database, 2008-2024. Analysis by Open Bankruptcy Project.

Frequently Asked Questions

How many bankruptcy cases are filed in Iowa?

Since 2008, Iowa's districts have processed over 25,000+ bankruptcy cases. Filing volumes peaked around 2010-2011 following the financial crisis and have generally declined since.

What is the Chapter 13 success rate?

Nationally, about 33-40% of Chapter 13 cases are dismissed before completion. Rates vary by district and depend on factors including the local legal market, judicial practices, and the complexity of cases filed. National dismissal rate data.

Check Your Eligibility

Use the free 1328(f) screener to check whether a prior discharge affects your eligibility.

## What Bills to Stop Paying Before Bankruptcy in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/stop-paying-bills.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Stop Paying Bills

What Bills to Stop Paying Before Bankruptcy in Iowa

Not all debts are created equal. Knowing which bills to stop paying - and which to keep paying - can save you thousands before you file.

Quick answer: Stop paying unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) that will be discharged. Keep paying secured debts you want to keep (mortgage, car loan) and essential services (utilities, insurance). Do not make large payments to any single creditor in the 90 days before filing.

Bills You Can Stop Paying

Credit cards: All credit card debt is dischargeable. Stop paying, but stop using the cards too. New charges near filing can be challenged as fraud.

Medical bills: Medical debt is unsecured and fully dischargeable. Collections calls are annoying but temporary.

Personal loans: Unsecured personal loans from banks, online lenders, and finance companies are dischargeable.

Old utility bills: Past-due amounts on closed accounts are dischargeable. (Keep current utilities paid - see below.)

Payday loans: Dischargeable unsecured debt. Payday lenders may threaten criminal action but cannot follow through on threats related to civil debt.

Collection accounts: Debts already in collections are dischargeable. The collection agency loses its right to collect once you file.

Bills You Should Keep Paying

Mortgage (if keeping the house): Falling behind creates arrears that complicate your case. In Chapter 13, you must be current on post-petition payments.

Car loan (if keeping the car): Same logic. Stay current on vehicles you need for work and daily life.

Current utilities: Electricity, water, gas, internet. Utility companies can require a deposit after bankruptcy but cannot shut off service solely because you filed.

Insurance: Auto insurance is legally required in Iowa. Health insurance protects against new medical debt.

Child support/alimony: These are not dischargeable and must be paid. Falling behind creates a priority debt that must be paid in full in Chapter 13.

Taxes: Recent income taxes (within 3 years) are priority debts. Older tax debts may be dischargeable under specific conditions.

The 90-Day Preference Period

Under 11 U.S.C. section 547, the bankruptcy trustee can claw back payments made to creditors during the 90 days before filing if they exceed $600 to a single creditor. For payments to insiders (family, business partners), the look-back period is one year.

Common mistake: Paying off a credit card or repaying a family loan right before filing. The trustee can sue the recipient to recover that money for the benefit of all creditors. This is called a preferential transfer.

$600+ to one creditor in 90 days - potentially recoverable

Any amount to family in 12 months - potentially recoverable

Regular, ordinary payments (mortgage, car loan, utilities) - generally safe

Frequently Asked Questions

What bills should I stop paying before bankruptcy in Iowa?

Generally, you should continue paying secured debts you want to keep (mortgage, car loan) and essential bills (utilities, insurance). You can typically stop paying unsecured debts (credit cards, medical bills, personal loans) that will be discharged in bankruptcy. Consult with a bankruptcy attorney before making changes.

Should I stop paying credit cards before filing bankruptcy?

Yes, in most cases. Credit card debt is unsecured and will be discharged in bankruptcy. Continuing to pay credit cards before filing wastes money that could go toward filing costs or essential expenses. However, stop making new charges on the cards - using credit cards with no intent to repay is fraud.

What is the preference period in bankruptcy?

The preference period is 90 days before filing (1 year for payments to insiders like family members). Payments of $600 or more to a single creditor during this period can be clawed back by the trustee as preferential transfers. Paying a favorite creditor right before filing can backfire.

Should I keep paying my mortgage before bankruptcy?

If you want to keep your house, yes - keep paying the mortgage. If you plan to surrender the house, you can stop paying. In Chapter 13, mortgage arrears can be cured through the plan, but you must stay current on post-petition payments.

Should I keep paying my car loan before bankruptcy?

If you want to keep the vehicle, continue making payments. If you plan to surrender it, you can stop. In Chapter 13, you can cure arrears through the plan and may be able to reduce the loan balance to the vehicle's value (cramdown) if the loan is more than 910 days old.

Can creditors sue me if I stop paying before filing?

Yes, creditors can file lawsuits and obtain judgments. However, once you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay halts all collection activity. If you plan to file within 30-60 days, the risk of a completed lawsuit is low. A judgment lien, however, can complicate your bankruptcy.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House

Garnishment

## Student Loans and Bankruptcy in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/student-loans.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesCedar RapidsChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Student Loans

Student Loans and Bankruptcy in Iowa

Student loan debt is a growing crisis. While discharging student loans in bankruptcy has historically been difficult, recent changes are making it more achievable for Iowa borrowers.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

The Changing Landscape of Student Loan Discharge

For decades, the conventional wisdom was that student loans could never be discharged in bankruptcy. That was never entirely true, and recent developments have shifted the landscape significantly:

November 2022: DOJ issued new guidance creating a more structured, less adversarial process for evaluating student loan discharge

Brunner reconsideration: Several circuit courts have reconsidered the rigid application of the Brunner test

Adversary proceedings: Filing an adversary proceeding (a lawsuit within your bankruptcy case) is required, but the process is becoming more straightforward

Partial discharge: Even if full discharge is not granted, courts can discharge a portion of student loan debt

The Undue Hardship Standard

Brunner Test (3 Prongs)

Cannot maintain minimal standard of living if forced to repay the loan

Hardship likely to persist for a significant portion of the repayment period

Good faith effort to repay (applied for deferments, IDR plans, made payments when able)

Totality of Circumstances

Some courts use a more flexible test that considers all relevant factors:

Past, present, and future financial resources

Reasonable living expenses

Other relevant facts and circumstances

No rigid three-prong requirement

Alternatives to Bankruptcy for Student Loans

Income-Driven Repayment (IDR): Plans like SAVE, IBR, PAYE, and ICR cap payments at 5-20% of discretionary income. Remaining balance forgiven after 20-25 years.

Public Service Loan Forgiveness (PSLF): Full forgiveness after 120 qualifying payments while working for government or non-profit employers.

Total and Permanent Disability (TPD): Full discharge if you have a qualifying disability.

Closed School Discharge: If your school closed while you were enrolled or shortly after.

Borrower Defense: If your school engaged in fraud or misrepresentation.

If none of these alternatives resolve your student loan burden, bankruptcy with an adversary proceeding for undue hardship is a viable path.

Filing in Iowa

Student loan adversary proceedings in Iowa are filed in N.D. Iowa and S.D. Iowa. The process involves:

File your Chapter 7 or Chapter 13 bankruptcy case

File a separate adversary proceeding (complaint) seeking discharge of student loans

The DOJ or loan servicer responds

Discovery, mediation, or trial may follow

The court rules on whether undue hardship is established

Courthouse: U.S. Bankruptcy Court, 123 E Walnut St, Des Moines, IA 50309 (S.D. Iowa)

Legal assistance: Iowa Legal Aid (iowalegalaid.org) may provide guidance or representation for student loan adversary proceedings.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can student loans be discharged in bankruptcy?

Yes, but it is harder than other debts. Student loans can be discharged in bankruptcy by filing an adversary proceeding and proving undue hardship. Recent DOJ guidance (November 2022) has made this process more accessible, and some courts in N.D. Iowa and S.D. Iowa may apply a more flexible standard.

What is the Brunner test for student loans?

The Brunner test requires you to show: (1) you cannot maintain a minimal standard of living if forced to repay, (2) your financial situation is likely to persist for a significant portion of the repayment period, and (3) you have made good faith efforts to repay. Some courts use the totality of circumstances test instead, which is generally more flexible.

What did the DOJ change about student loan discharge?

In November 2022, the Department of Justice issued new guidance making it easier to discharge student loans in bankruptcy. The DOJ now considers factors like income, expenses, loan balance, and repayment history in a more holistic way, rather than applying the Brunner test rigidly.

What about income-driven repayment plans?

Income-driven repayment (IDR) plans like SAVE, IBR, PAYE, and ICR can reduce monthly payments to as low as $0 based on income. However, they do not eliminate the debt. Bankruptcy may be a better option if the total debt is unmanageable even with reduced payments.

Does filing bankruptcy stop student loan collection?

Yes. The automatic stay stops all collection activity, including wage garnishment for federal student loans, lawsuits, and collection calls. This protection lasts for the duration of your bankruptcy case.

Explore Your Options

Check your discharge eligibility and learn about your rights as a borrower.

Free Discharge Screener
Student Loan Guide

## Surrendering Your Car in Iowa Bankruptcy - Process, Deficiency, Options
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/surrender-car.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Surrendering Your Car in Iowa Bankruptcy

Surrendering Your Car in Iowa Bankruptcy

Surrendering a vehicle in bankruptcy eliminates both the car payment and any deficiency balance. This can be the right choice when you owe more than the car is worth or cannot afford the payments.

Quick answer: Surrender means returning the vehicle to the lender. In Chapter 7, the deficiency balance (what you owe minus what the car sells for) is discharged - you owe nothing. In Chapter 13, the deficiency becomes unsecured debt in your plan. Surrender is often the smartest move for upside-down loans.

How Car Surrender Works

Step-by-Step Process

File bankruptcy and indicate "surrender" on your Statement of Intention (Chapter 7) or plan (Chapter 13)

Automatic stay activates - the lender cannot repossess while the stay is in effect

Return the vehicle - the lender arranges pickup or designates a drop-off location (usually 30-45 days after filing)

Lender sells the vehicle - typically at auction, often for wholesale value

Deficiency balance calculated - what you owe minus sale price

Deficiency discharged - in Chapter 7, the deficiency is eliminated; in Chapter 13, it becomes unsecured debt in your plan

Iowa Vehicle Exemptions

Iowa's motor vehicle exemption ($7,000) is moderate. Surrendering an underwater vehicle eliminates the deficiency in Chapter 7. Iowa's rural character makes personal transportation essential - public transit is limited outside Des Moines, Cedar Rapids, and Iowa City. The deficiency balance after surrender is unsecured debt that gets discharged.

When Surrender Makes Sense

Consider Surrender When

You owe significantly more than the car is worth (upside down)

The payment is unaffordable

The vehicle has high mileage or needs expensive repairs

You have access to alternative transportation

You can acquire a cheaper replacement vehicle

Interest rate is very high (20%+ on a buy-here-pay-here loan)

Consider Keeping When

You have positive equity (worth more than you owe)

The payment is affordable on your budget

The vehicle is reliable and you need it for work

You are close to paying it off

Chapter 13 cramdown can reduce the balance to current value

Interest rate is reasonable

The Chapter 13 Cramdown Alternative

If your car loan is more than 910 days old (approximately 2.5 years), Chapter 13 offers a powerful alternative to surrender: the cramdown. Under 11 U.S.C. section 1325(a), you can reduce the secured claim to the vehicle's current value and pay that amount through your plan at a court-approved interest rate.

Example: You owe $15,000 on a car worth $8,000. If the loan is older than 910 days, you pay $8,000 as a secured claim in your plan. The remaining $7,000 becomes unsecured debt (paid at whatever percentage your plan pays unsecured creditors - often pennies on the dollar).

The 910-day rule (sometimes called the "hanging paragraph") prevents cramdown on newer vehicle purchases.

Getting a Replacement Vehicle

After surrender, you will need replacement transportation. Options include:

Buy-here-pay-here lots: Available immediately but often have high interest rates and limited selection

Cash purchase: Save up for a reliable used car ($3,000-$5,000 can get a dependable vehicle)

Credit union auto loans: Some credit unions will lend to recent bankruptcy filers, especially members

Subprime auto lenders: Available after discharge, interest rates will be high but can be refinanced later as credit improves

Family or friends: A temporary arrangement while you rebuild

Frequently Asked Questions

What happens when I surrender my car?

You return it, the lender sells it, and the deficiency is discharged in Chapter 7 or becomes unsecured debt in Chapter 13.

Do I have to surrender?

No. You can reaffirm, redeem, or (in Chapter 13) cramdown. Surrender is one option among several.

When do I give up the car?

Typically 30-45 days after filing. The lender arranges pickup or a drop-off. You can drive until then.

Will I owe after surrender?

In Chapter 7, no - the deficiency is discharged. In Chapter 13, it becomes unsecured debt in your plan.

Can I surrender a leased car?

Yes. You can reject the lease in bankruptcy. Early termination fees and remaining lease obligations are dischargeable.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## Tax Debt and Bankruptcy in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/tax-debt.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesCedar RapidsChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Tax Debt

Tax Debt and Bankruptcy in Iowa

Tax debt can feel insurmountable, but bankruptcy may offer relief. Some tax debts can be discharged in bankruptcy if they meet specific requirements.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

The Tax Discharge Tests

To discharge federal income tax debt in bankruptcy, ALL of the following must be true:

3-Year Rule: The tax return was due (including extensions) more than 3 years before you filed bankruptcy

2-Year Rule: The tax return was actually filed more than 2 years before filing bankruptcy

240-Day Rule: The tax was assessed by the IRS more than 240 days before filing bankruptcy

No fraud: The return was not fraudulent

No willful evasion: You did not willfully attempt to evade the tax

Example: Your 2021 tax return was due April 15, 2022. If you file bankruptcy after April 15, 2025, the 3-year test is met. If you actually filed the return before April 2023, the 2-year test is also met. If the IRS assessed the tax before July 2024, the 240-day test is met.

What Taxes Cannot Be Discharged

Recent taxes: Tax debts that do not meet the 3-year/2-year/240-day tests

Payroll taxes: Trust fund taxes (employee withholding) are never dischargeable

Fraudulent returns: Taxes on returns filed with intent to defraud

Unfiled returns: Taxes for years where no return was filed (or filed late and less than 2 years ago)

Tax penalties: Some penalties may survive, depending on their nature and age

State taxes: Iowa state tax rules may differ from federal rules - consult a tax professional

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Tax Debt

Chapter 7

Can discharge qualifying tax debts entirely

Completed in 3-4 months

Non-qualifying tax debt survives the case

Tax liens survive even if the debt is discharged

Best if: your tax debt meets all discharge requirements

Chapter 13

Pay priority tax debts over 3-5 years

Stops penalties and interest during the plan

Automatic stay stops all IRS collection

Non-priority tax debts may be partially discharged

Best if: tax debt does not qualify for Chapter 7 discharge

Tax Liens and Bankruptcy

A critical distinction: even when the underlying tax debt is discharged, a recorded IRS tax lien survives bankruptcy. The lien remains attached to property you owned at the time of filing.

However, the personal obligation to pay is eliminated. This means the IRS cannot garnish your wages or levy your bank accounts, but the lien remains on existing property until it expires or is satisfied.

Strategy: If you have no significant assets, a surviving lien has little practical effect. If you do have property, Chapter 13 may be more effective because it allows you to pay the tax through your plan and clear the lien.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can you discharge tax debt in bankruptcy?

Some tax debts can be discharged in bankruptcy, but it depends on the type of tax, when it was due, and whether returns were filed. Income taxes that meet specific age and filing requirements may be dischargeable.

What taxes can be discharged in bankruptcy?

Only income taxes may be dischargeable, and they must meet all of these tests: (1) the tax return was due more than 3 years ago, (2) the return was filed more than 2 years ago, (3) the tax was assessed more than 240 days ago, and (4) the return was not fraudulent and you did not willfully evade the tax.

What about tax liens?

Even if the underlying tax debt is discharged, a recorded tax lien survives bankruptcy and remains attached to your property. The lien stays until it expires or you pay it. However, you can negotiate with the IRS to remove the lien after discharge in some cases.

Can bankruptcy stop an IRS levy or garnishment?

Yes. The automatic stay immediately stops IRS levies, wage garnishment, and seizure of bank accounts or property. The IRS can continue auditing, but it cannot collect while the stay is in effect.

Should I file Chapter 7 or Chapter 13 for tax debt?

If your tax debt meets the discharge requirements, Chapter 7 can eliminate it entirely. If it does not qualify for discharge, Chapter 13 lets you pay it over 3-5 years without penalties and interest accumulating, and stops IRS collection actions during the plan.

Overwhelmed by Tax Debt?

Check your eligibility and explore your options for tax debt relief through bankruptcy.

Free Discharge Screener
Tax Debt Guide

## Tax Refunds and Chapter 13 in Iowa - Trustee Practices, Protecting Your Refund
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/tax-refund-chapter13.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostGlossaryFaqOBP

Home›Tax Refunds and Chapter 13 in Iowa

Tax Refunds and Chapter 13 in Iowa

In Chapter 13, the trustee may require you to turn over all or part of your tax refund as additional plan payments. Trustee practices vary by district, and there are strategies to protect some or all of your refund.

Quick answer: Most Chapter 13 trustees require debtors to turn over tax refunds above a threshold amount (typically $1,500-$2,500, but this varies by district). Your plan confirmation order or the trustee's standing instructions will specify requirements. Adjusting W-4 withholding to reduce the refund is one strategy, but must be done carefully to avoid bad-faith arguments.

Why the Trustee Wants Your Tax Refund

In Chapter 13, you commit all projected disposable income to your repayment plan for 3-5 years (11 U.S.C. section 1325(b)). The trustee's position is that a tax refund represents income that was over-withheld during the year - money that should have been available to pay creditors.

The logic: if you receive a $4,000 refund, that is $333/month in excess withholding that was not included in your disposable income calculation. The trustee argues this should go to your creditors.

Iowa Trustee Practices

Iowa has a state income tax (graduated, up to 5.7% for 2026). Both federal and state refunds are at risk. Trustee practices differ between N.D. Iowa and S.D. Iowa. Some Iowa trustees require refunds above a threshold to be turned over. Iowa Code 627.6(8)(a) exempts certain benefits reasonably necessary for support, which may protect some tax credit portions. Earned Income Credit portions typically receive additional protection.

Strategies to Protect Your Refund

1. Adjust Your W-4 Withholding

The most effective strategy is to adjust your W-4 so you get more per paycheck and a smaller refund. If your refund is $500, the trustee has little to claim. Do this gradually and early in the plan - a sudden, dramatic change may trigger scrutiny.

2. Plan for the Refund in Your Budget

If you know the trustee will require turnover above $2,000, plan your withholding so your refund falls below that threshold. Use the IRS withholding estimator (irs.gov) to calibrate.

3. Request Permission to Keep the Refund

Some trustees allow debtors to keep refunds for specific purposes: necessary car repairs, medical expenses, or home maintenance. File a motion or request with the trustee explaining the need. Documentation helps (repair estimates, medical bills).

4. Earned Income Credit Arguments

If a significant portion of your refund is the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC) or Child Tax Credit, some courts treat these as government benefits rather than disposable income. This argument has mixed results but is worth raising.

What Happens If You Do Not Turn Over the Refund

Failing to turn over a required tax refund can have serious consequences:

Motion to dismiss: The trustee can file a motion to dismiss your Chapter 13 case

Conversion to Chapter 7: The trustee or court may convert your case to Chapter 7

Bad faith finding: Repeated failure can be considered bad faith, affecting your ability to refile

Plan modification: The trustee may seek to modify your plan to increase payments

If you cannot turn over the refund or need to use it for an emergency, file a motion with the court BEFORE spending it. Courts are more sympathetic when you ask permission than when you ask forgiveness.

Frequently Asked Questions

Will the trustee take my tax refund?

Most likely some or all of it, depending on the amount and your district's practices. Check your plan terms for the specific threshold.

Can I adjust my withholding?

Yes, within reason. Gradual adjustments to reduce over-withholding are generally acceptable. Dramatic changes may be scrutinized.

What about Earned Income Credit?

Some courts treat EITC differently from regular refund amounts. This is not settled law everywhere - results vary by district.

When must I turn over the refund?

Typically within 30-60 days of filing your return. Check the trustee's standing instructions or your plan confirmation order.

What if I spent the refund already?

Contact your attorney immediately. The trustee may file a motion to dismiss. Being proactive about communicating with the trustee is better than ignoring the requirement.

Related Topics

Exemptions
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Wage Garnishment
Medical Debt

## Bankruptcy Timeline in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/timeline.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesCedar RapidsChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Timeline

Bankruptcy Timeline in Iowa

Understanding the bankruptcy timeline helps you plan and reduces anxiety. Here is a detailed look at what happens at each stage when filing in Iowa.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Chapter 7 Timeline: 3-4 Months

Before Filing: Credit Counseling

Complete a credit counseling course from an approved provider within 180 days before filing. This is required and takes about 60-90 minutes. Cost: $15-$50.

Day 1: File Your Petition

Your bankruptcy petition, schedules, and statements are filed with N.D. Iowa and S.D. Iowa. The automatic stay takes effect immediately. Filing fee: $338.

Days 20-40: 341 Meeting of Creditors

Appear before the bankruptcy trustee for a brief hearing. The trustee reviews your finances and assets. Usually 5-15 minutes. Location: U.S. Bankruptcy Court, 123 E Walnut St, Des Moines, IA 50309 (S.D. Iowa)

Days 30-60: Creditor Objection Period

Creditors have 60 days after the 341 meeting to object to discharge of specific debts. Objections are rare in most cases.

Before Discharge: Financial Management Course

Complete a second required course (debtor education/financial management). Cost: $15-$50. Must be completed before discharge.

Days 60-90: Discharge Granted

The court enters your discharge order, eliminating your qualifying debts. Your case is closed shortly after.

Chapter 13 Timeline: 3-5 Years

Before Filing: Credit Counseling

Same requirement as Chapter 7. Complete within 180 days before filing.

Day 1: File Petition + Plan

File your petition, schedules, and Chapter 13 repayment plan. Automatic stay takes effect. Filing fee: $313.

Day 30: First Plan Payment

Your first plan payment is due within 30 days of filing, even before the plan is confirmed.

Days 20-40: 341 Meeting

Similar to Chapter 7 but the trustee also reviews your proposed repayment plan.

Days 45-90: Plan Confirmation Hearing

The court reviews and confirms your repayment plan. Creditors may object. The judge determines if the plan meets legal requirements.

Years 1-5: Make Plan Payments

Make monthly payments to the Chapter 13 trustee, who distributes funds to creditors according to your confirmed plan.

End of Plan: Discharge

After completing all plan payments and the financial management course, the court grants your discharge.

Key Filing Requirements

1

Credit Counseling

Required before filing. Approved providers at justice.gov.

2

Means Test

Required for Chapter 7 to determine eligibility based on income.

3

Tax Returns

Must provide tax returns for the last 4 years to the trustee.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does Chapter 7 bankruptcy take in Iowa?

Chapter 7 bankruptcy typically takes 3-4 months from filing to discharge. The 341 meeting of creditors happens about 30 days after filing, and discharge is usually granted 60 days after the 341 meeting.

How long does Chapter 13 bankruptcy take in Iowa?

Chapter 13 requires completing a 3-5 year repayment plan. The plan must be confirmed by the court, typically within 2-3 months of filing. Discharge occurs after all plan payments are completed.

What happens at the 341 meeting of creditors?

The 341 meeting is a brief hearing (usually 5-15 minutes) where the bankruptcy trustee asks you questions under oath about your finances, assets, and bankruptcy schedules. It is held at the courthouse in Iowa or by phone/video. Creditors may attend but rarely do.

Can my bankruptcy case be dismissed?

Yes. Cases can be dismissed for: failing to complete credit counseling, not filing required documents, not passing the means test (Chapter 7), or failing to make plan payments (Chapter 13). Dismissal removes bankruptcy protection.

When does the automatic stay begin?

The automatic stay takes effect the instant your bankruptcy petition is filed with the court. It does not matter whether creditors have been notified yet - the stay is effective immediately.

Ready to Take the First Step?

Check your discharge eligibility with our free tool.

Free Discharge Screener
How to File Guide

## Timeshares and Bankruptcy in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/timeshare.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Timeshares

Timeshares and Bankruptcy in Iowa

Trapped in a timeshare? Bankruptcy may be the most reliable exit. You can surrender the timeshare and discharge the mortgage, maintenance fees, and assessments.

Quick answer: You can surrender a timeshare in Chapter 7 bankruptcy and discharge all associated debts - mortgage, maintenance fees, assessments, and special levies. This is often faster and cheaper than timeshare exit companies.

Chapter 7: The Clean Exit

Chapter 7 is the most straightforward way to exit a timeshare:

List the timeshare as property on Schedule A/B (real or personal property, depending on how the timeshare is structured)

Indicate surrender on your Statement of Intention (Schedule D/Form 108)

Discharge the debt: The mortgage balance, maintenance fees, assessments, and any other obligations are discharged

Deed transfer: The timeshare company typically handles transferring the deed out of your name after discharge

Important: You are responsible for maintenance fees that accrue between filing and the date the timeshare is actually transferred. Work with the trustee to expedite the surrender.

Chapter 13: Keep or Surrender

Chapter 13 gives you more options:

Surrender through plan: Include the timeshare surrender in your Chapter 13 plan. Pre-petition arrears are treated as unsecured debt, paid at the plan percentage (often 10-30 cents on the dollar).

Keep and pay: If you want to keep the timeshare, continue paying the mortgage and fees. Include any arrears in the plan.

Lien stripping: If the timeshare is worth less than what you owe, you may be able to strip the lien in some circumstances - though this is more common with second mortgages on primary residences.

What Debts Are Covered?

Timeshare mortgage: Discharged in Chapter 7, paid through plan in Chapter 13

Maintenance fees (past due): Discharged as unsecured debt

Special assessments: Discharged as unsecured debt

Exchange program fees: Discharged (RCI, Interval International, etc.)

Collection agency debts: If the timeshare company sold the debt, the buyer's claim is also discharged

Bankruptcy vs. Timeshare Exit Companies

Bankruptcy

Filing fee: $338 (Chapter 7)

Legally binding discharge

Eliminates ALL timeshare debts

Process takes 3-4 months

Court-supervised, transparent

Also eliminates other qualifying debts

Exit Companies

Typical cost: $3,000-$10,000+

No legal guarantee of results

High rate of failure/scams

Process can take 12-24 months

No regulatory oversight

Only addresses the timeshare

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I get rid of a timeshare through bankruptcy in Iowa?

Yes. In Chapter 7, you can surrender a timeshare and discharge the remaining obligations - mortgage balance, maintenance fees, and assessments. The timeshare interest is listed as property of the estate and abandoned or surrendered through the bankruptcy process.

Will bankruptcy stop timeshare maintenance fees?

Bankruptcy discharges your personal liability for pre-petition maintenance fees. However, maintenance fees that accrue after filing are your responsibility until the timeshare is actually transferred out of your name. Surrendering in bankruptcy accelerates that process.

What happens to a timeshare mortgage in bankruptcy?

A timeshare mortgage is treated like any other secured debt. In Chapter 7, you can surrender the timeshare and discharge the debt. In Chapter 13, you can either keep paying it or surrender it through the plan. The lender may still foreclose on the timeshare interest itself.

Can I keep my timeshare in Iowa bankruptcy?

Yes. If you want to keep the timeshare, you can reaffirm the debt (Chapter 7) or continue paying through your plan (Chapter 13). You must stay current on mortgage payments, maintenance fees, and assessments.

Are timeshare exit companies legitimate?

Many timeshare exit companies charge thousands of dollars and deliver no results. Bankruptcy is often a more reliable and less expensive way to exit a timeshare. A Chapter 7 filing fee is $338 - far less than most exit company fees.

Does the timeshare HOA get paid in bankruptcy?

Pre-petition HOA/maintenance fees are unsecured debt and typically receive little or nothing in Chapter 7. In Chapter 13, they are paid at the same rate as other unsecured creditors - often pennies on the dollar.

Related Topics

Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House
Credit After
Garnishment
FAQ

## Can I Travel During Chapter 13 in Iowa?
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/travel-chapter-13.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Travel in Ch. 13

Can I Travel During Chapter 13 in Iowa?

Domestic travel is fine. International travel may require notifying your trustee. The real issue is not where you go - it is what you spend.

Quick answer: Domestic travel during Chapter 13 in Iowa is unrestricted. For international travel, notify your trustee as a precaution. The key rule: do not spend money on luxury travel while your creditors are being paid less than they are owed.

Domestic vs. International Travel

Domestic Travel

No restrictions or permissions needed

No notification to trustee required

Keep spending reasonable

Budget travel is generally fine

Work travel is never an issue

International Travel

No legal prohibition, but notify your trustee

Some trustees want advance notice

Extended trips may raise questions

Keep records of purpose and expenses

Luxury international travel is risky

What Could Cause Problems

Falling behind on plan payments: If you travel and then miss a plan payment, the trustee will connect the dots. This is the fastest way to get your case dismissed.

Lavish spending: Posting photos from expensive resorts while paying creditors 10 cents on the dollar creates a credibility problem. Trustees and creditors can review social media.

Unexplained income: If you are traveling frequently on a budget that says you have no discretionary income, expect questions about undisclosed income or assets.

Extended international trips: Spending months abroad can suggest you have income or assets not disclosed in your schedules.

The safest approach: keep travel modest, stay current on plan payments, and notify your trustee before any international travel.

What Is Always Fine

Work travel: Required by your employer, fully reimbursed - no issue whatsoever

Family emergencies: Travel for funerals, medical emergencies, or family crises

Local/regional day trips: Taking the kids to a state park or visiting family

Budget vacations: A modest road trip or camping trip within your means

Medical travel: Traveling for medical treatment or appointments

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I travel during Chapter 13 in Iowa?

Yes. Domestic travel is generally not restricted during Chapter 13 in Iowa or any other state. International travel may require notifying your trustee, depending on local practice. The key concern is whether travel spending conflicts with your budget and plan obligations.

Do I need court permission to travel during Chapter 13?

For domestic travel, no court permission is needed. For international travel, some trustees and courts want advance notice. There is no statute requiring permission, but your plan requires you to devote all disposable income to creditors - luxury travel could raise issues.

Can the trustee stop me from traveling?

The trustee cannot directly stop you from traveling. However, if you spend money on non-essential travel and then fall behind on plan payments, the trustee can file a motion to dismiss your case. Lavish travel spending can also trigger a motion to modify your plan upward.

Is international travel allowed during Chapter 13?

There is no law prohibiting international travel during Chapter 13. However, best practice is to notify your trustee before international trips. Some trustees view international travel as a sign that you have more disposable income than reported.

What about work travel during Chapter 13?

Business travel is not an issue. If your employer requires travel, that is a legitimate expense. Keep records showing the travel was work-related, especially if questioned by the trustee.

Can I take a vacation during Chapter 13?

Modest vacations are generally acceptable. The concern is extravagant spending while creditors are receiving less than what they are owed. A reasonable road trip or modest vacation is unlikely to cause problems. A cruise or international resort vacation may attract scrutiny.

Related Topics

Chapter 13
Exemptions
Keep Your Car
Keep Your House

## Wage Garnishment and Bankruptcy in Iowa
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/wage-garnishment.html

Iowa Bankruptcy Guide
HomeDes MoinesCedar RapidsChapter 7Chapter 13ExemptionsCostFAQOBP

Home›Iowa Bankruptcy Guide›Wage Garnishment

Wage Garnishment and Bankruptcy in Iowa

If your paycheck is being garnished, filing bankruptcy can stop it immediately. Here is how wage garnishment works in Iowa and how bankruptcy provides relief.

This page provides general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice about your specific situation.

Iowa Wage Garnishment Rules

Iowa follows the federal standard: creditors can garnish the lesser of 25% of disposable earnings or the amount by which weekly disposable earnings exceed 30 times the federal minimum wage ($217.50/week in 2026).

How garnishment works: A creditor must first sue you and win a judgment. Then they file a garnishment order with the court, which is served on your employer. Your employer is legally required to withhold the garnished amount from your paycheck.

Exceptions that do not require a judgment:

Federal student loan garnishment (up to 15% of disposable pay)

Child support and alimony

Federal and state tax debts

How Bankruptcy Stops Garnishment

Immediately Stopped

Credit card debt garnishment

Medical bill garnishment

Personal loan garnishment

Auto deficiency garnishment

Student loan garnishment

Most judgment garnishments

NOT Stopped

Child support withholding

Alimony withholding

Domestic support obligations

The automatic stay takes effect the instant you file your bankruptcy petition. Your attorney will notify your employer and the garnishing creditor. Garnishment must stop immediately - any wages garnished after filing may need to be returned to you.

Recovering Previously Garnished Wages

Under 11 U.S.C. section 547, the bankruptcy trustee may be able to recover wages garnished within 90 days before your filing date if the total exceeds $600. These recovered funds become part of your bankruptcy estate and may be distributed to creditors or exempted by you.

This is another reason why timing your bankruptcy filing strategically matters. Consulting with an attorney or legal aid about the best time to file can maximize your recovery.

Chapter 7 vs. Chapter 13 for Garnishment

Chapter 7

Stops garnishment immediately

Discharges the underlying debt in 3-4 months

Garnishment cannot resume for discharged debts

Must pass the means test

Chapter 13

Stops garnishment immediately

Repay debts through a structured plan instead

Better for debts that cannot be discharged in Chapter 7

3-5 year repayment period

Frequently Asked Questions

How much of my wages can be garnished in Iowa?

Iowa follows the federal standard: creditors can garnish the lesser of 25% of disposable earnings or the amount by which weekly disposable earnings exceed 30 times the federal minimum wage ($217.50/week in 2026).

Can bankruptcy stop wage garnishment?

Yes. Filing bankruptcy in Iowa triggers the automatic stay under 11 U.S.C. section 362, which immediately stops all wage garnishment. Your employer will be notified and must stop withholding garnished wages.

Can I get back wages that were already garnished?

In some cases, yes. If wages were garnished within 90 days before filing and exceed $600, the bankruptcy trustee may be able to recover those funds as a preferential transfer. Discuss this with your attorney.

What debts can lead to wage garnishment?

In general, any creditor who obtains a court judgment can garnish wages. Common sources include credit card debt, medical bills, personal loans, and auto deficiency balances. Child support and tax debts can be garnished without a court judgment.

Does the automatic stay stop all types of garnishment in Iowa?

The automatic stay stops garnishment for most debts, but not for domestic support obligations (child support and alimony). Tax garnishment is also stopped temporarily. Student loan garnishment by the government is stopped during the bankruptcy case.

Stop Garnishment Now

Check your eligibility for bankruptcy and learn how to stop wage garnishment.

Free Discharge Screener
Garnishment Guide

## Privacy Policy - iowabankruptcy.org
Source: https://iowabankruptcy.org/privacy.html

iowabankruptcy.org

Home
Privacy

Privacy Policy

How we handle your data. Short version: we don't sell it, share it, or use it for advertising.

Data We Collect

We use Google Analytics (GA4) to measure aggregate site traffic. This includes page views, session duration, approximate geographic region (country and city level), and device type. We do not collect names, email addresses, or personally identifiable information through analytics.

Aggregate event data from Google Analytics is also exported to Google BigQuery for research use. The BigQuery data is used only for internal trajectory analysis and network health reports, is not shared with third parties, and is not used for advertising.

What We Don't Do

We do not sell data. We do not run advertising. We do not collect financial information. We do not use retargeting. We do not share any user data with attorneys, law firms, creditors, debt collectors, or legal service providers.

Specifically, we do not enable Google Signals, Google Ads integration, User-ID collection, or any cross-device tracking feature in Google Analytics. GA4 operates in aggregate-traffic-measurement mode only on this site.

Cookies

We use only first-party cookies set by Google Analytics for aggregate traffic measurement. We do not use advertising cookies, tracking pixels, or third-party analytics cookies.

Third-Party Services

Google Analytics (GA4) - aggregate traffic measurement

Google BigQuery - aggregate event data warehouse for research use only; not shared, not used for advertising

Google Search Console - search query measurement (clicks, impressions, our own page rankings)

GitHub Pages - hosting

Contact Forms & Email

This site may link to contact forms or email addresses operated by the Open Bankruptcy Project. Any data you submit through those channels is handled under the Open Bankruptcy Project master privacy policy, which governs all sites in this network.

Children's Privacy

This site is not directed at children under 13. We do not knowingly collect information from children.

Data Retention

Analytics data is retained for 14 months per Google's standard retention settings. After that window, individual event data is automatically deleted by Google; aggregate report metrics remain available.

Your Rights

You may request deletion of any personal data we hold by contacting info@openbankruptcyproject.org. California residents have additional rights under CCPA. EU residents have additional rights under GDPR. All such requests are handled by the Open Bankruptcy Project.

Network Policy

This site is part of the Bankruptcy Transparency Network operated by the Open Bankruptcy Project, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit. The same privacy standards apply across every site in the network. The authoritative master policy is maintained at https://openbankruptcyproject.org/privacy.html.

Changes

We may update this policy from time to time. Changes are reflected on the master policy first and then mirrored across all network sites. Check the master policy for the most current version.

Effective: March 30, 2026  |  Updated: April 11, 2026

Home  ·  Privacy  ·  Open Bankruptcy Project

Part of the Bankruptcy Transparency Network. Educational information only - not legal advice.

No ads. No affiliate links. No data sold. Supported by donations.
